(b) The item marked in the supplement to the Responsa on Orach Chaim as number 11 is what is known by the name **Piskei Dinim** of the Tzemach Tzedek, even though the title page phrasing is, as copied in the supplement: "Tzemach Tzedek, and it includes Piskei Dinim, etc." It—as well as the item marked there as number 10—is currently in print, as a photographic reproduction of the first edition. I have added at the end the Tzemach Tzedek’s notes, which I copied from the holy manuscripts of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, along with citations indicating the collection of manuscripts where I found the Chiddush or the Responsa appearing in the Piskei Dinim—similar to the notes at the end of the Shaar HaMiluim.

(c) Thank you for your notes regarding the typographical errors you found. As for your wonderment at how there could be any errors when there are holy manuscripts for comparison: surely you know that we do not print from the manuscripts themselves. Only a copy of the manuscript is given to the typesetter and subsequently to the proofreader. If you take into account that hundreds of pages were typeset and proofread, based on various and sundry manuscripts, and that the printer was rushing to say "your work is finished" so as not to delay the entire publication—and if you were to see how many errors were indeed corrected—you would surely wonder, after all, how the six volumes of the Tzemach Tzedek were published at all. And after asking your forgiveness—not as a justification, but merely as a remark—I do not possess the Talmudic Encyclopedia you mention in your letter, but based on what I have seen in all the books currently being printed, I am certain that one would find several typographical errors there as well, even though it seems from the advertisements regarding it that money is considered of no consequence in the printing of the Encyclopedia.

And let this be known: all the work in this edition of the Tzemach Tzedek—searching for and processing the manuscript collections (and if you are involved, as you write, with manuscripts, and you look at my notes at the end of the Shaar HaMiluim, you will be able to estimate how much toil and exertion this requires), comparing them with the printed editions, editing the supplement at the end of the Orach Chaim Responsa, the indices in the Choshen Mishpat and Shaar HaMiluim sections, etc., and even the back-and-forth with the printers—all of this, for several reasons, fell upon my shoulders alone without any help (except for one pair of rabbis who were the proofreaders). And all this was in addition to the regular work at the office, the camp, etc.

(d) Continuing from what I concluded in the previous paragraph, and since you write that you are a grandson of the Chassid, Rabbi A. of Zembine, and you are the one initiating the complaint, I, too, shall present my complaint:

It has been more than two years since the aforementioned edition was published, after the Tzemach Tzedek’s books had been off the market for decades. Furthermore, this edition included several of the Tzemach Tzedek’s Chiddushim and Responsa that were being published for the first time. Did one hear from anyone a word of joy regarding this event? Do you know how many copies of the Tzemach Tzedek have spread throughout Eretz Yisrael during this time? Reviews of books, both large and small, are printed in Eretz Yisrael almost every day. Was even one review printed regarding the Tzemach Tzedek (aside from the one written by a refugee who had just arrived in the country and had not yet been absorbed)? Over the past few years, the "Otzar HaChassidim" publishing house has brought out more than forty books and booklets. Did you read about any of them in the newspapers of Eretz Yisrael? If you are involved in literature, why did you not draw the attention of the religious public in Eretz Yisrael to this (whether by writing yourself or by stirring others who are specifically involved in this field)? Especially as a grandson of Rabbi A. of Zembine, this is surely your own vineyard—and "my own vineyard I have not kept"?

I do not wish to dwell on this, as I do not know what has become of you following the upheavals from our former country, and consequently, I am also doubtful if my aforementioned complaints apply to you. But in any case, the attitude of Anash and the lineage of Anash in Eretz Yisrael toward Chabad Chassidic literature is strange. And it is most painful in recent times, when those who are "far" are interested, researching, inquiring, and thirsting to know to an ever-increasing degree, while those who are "near" busy themselves with many, many fields—anything but the teachings of Chassidus.

Signing with wishes for all the best,

**M. Schneerson**
Chairman of the Executive Committee

## 454

22 Shevat, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu Harav A. [may he live],

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your questions from some time ago—the delay in answering was due to being occupied, and I ask for your forgiveness:

1) **Question.** Regarding what is written in *Iggeret HaTeshuvah* by the Alter Rebbe, Chapter 3: "And one may also eat a little about three hours before *neitz hachamah*." You raise a difficulty: Is it not forbidden to eat before *alos hashachar* (dawn), as stated in *Be'er Heitev* to *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim* 581:12, based on the *Zohar* and the writings of the Arizal?

**Response.** Regarding eating before *alos hashachar*, there are three distinct categories of prohibition:

a) The prohibition of eating before *Shacharis* prayer. This is derived from the verse, "You shall not eat upon the blood" (*Berachos* 10b). This is discussed in *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim* 89:6. The time for this prohibition begins **when the time for prayer arrives**, i.e., *alos hashachar*, **and not before**—as is proven in the Gemara there. This is explicitly stated in the *Shulchan Aruch* there, and in our Rebbe’s *Shulchan Aruch* there, section 6. Therefore, this does not apply to what is written in *Iggeret HaTeshuvah*.

b) If one is fasting during the day, one may eat and drink until *alos hashachar*, but if one sleeps a "fixed sleep" (*sheinah kavuah*), it is forbidden. This is discussed in *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim* 264. However, if one makes a stipulation, the majority of authorities—**and so it is ruled** in the *Shulchan Aruch* there—hold that it is permitted even after sleeping.

c) The prohibition of providing strength to the *Sitra Achra*, as elaborated in the *Zohar*, Vol. II (215b). Regarding the forbidden time: All agree that this prohibition exists once the time for prayer arrives, as mentioned in (a) above. All agree that it is not prohibited before *alos hashachar* if one has not yet slept, or if one has slept less than "two breaths" (*shtei nishmei*—as the *Zohar* there explains: "because he tastes the taste of death"). However, if one slept and arose from midnight onwards, some say it is forbidden even before *alos hashachar* (*Magen Avraham* 89:14, citing R. Chaim Vital; *Machzor* tractate *Chatzos* 2:1; and so it is written in *Ohr HaChamah* to the *Zohar* there in the name of R. Chaim Vital, and in *Nitzotzei Oros* there). Others say it is permitted, because the time for this prohibition is, as stated in the *Shas*, **when the time for prayer arrives** (*Responsa Hashiv Moshe*, Orach Chayim 6, citing the *Seder HaLimud* of the Arizal by R. Shmuel of Rashkov at the end of the *ashmoros*—that this is what R. Chaim Vital did—and citing the Maharil and Rema in *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim* 581, contrary to what is written there in the *Be'er Heitev*). Furthermore, even according to those who forbid it, it is permitted if one does so for medicinal purposes, to focus one’s mind, and the like (*Nitzotzei Oros* there; *Be'er Heitev* and *Eshel Avraham* in *Orach Chayim* 89, in the name of *Responsa Shev Yaakov* and others).

Now, the Alter Rebbe in *Iggeret HaTeshuvah* explains that if one stipulates, the day is considered a fast even if one eats at night. He is not discussing—regarding the *Zohar’s* prohibition—which opinion one should follow. Regardless, even according to the stringent view, there are several ways **that it is permitted**, as mentioned above. It is this that *Iggeret HaTeshuvah* addresses.

**Note:** a) The Alter Rebbe’s position in this dispute can seemingly be clarified as lenient, from the fact that in his *Shulchan Aruch* (Orach Chayim 89:6), although he brings the *Magen Avraham’s* view regarding eating not for the sake of pride, etc., he **did not** copy what the *Magen Avraham* wrote—that it is forbidden to eat from midnight onwards. Although it is not entirely the same, as that is an entirely new ruling.

b) Why he required in *Iggeret HaTeshuvah* to stop **three** hours before *alos hashachar*—this requires study, and this is not the place.

In *Eshel Avraham*—by the Rebbe of Buchach—*Mahadura Tinyana* Orach Chayim 89, he holds that it is forbidden to eat before *alos hashachar* only if one remains awake until the time of prayer; however, if one goes to sleep again before *alos hashachar*, it is different.

***

Is there a source or reason for the custom of the Sephardic community in Brooklyn to recite *Mizmor Shir L'Yom HaShabbos* twice on Erev Shabbos?

**Response:** This is also the custom in the "Tzlacha U'Mazal" synagogue and the "Kehal Chassidim Beis-El" synagogue in Jerusalem (may it be rebuilt), and in the Maharal’s synagogue in Prague (see regarding this in the periodical *Neirot Shabbos* published in Eretz Yisrael, booklet 73-74. He cites there the book *Eretz Chaim* by R. Chaim Setron—Jerusalem 5668—but I do not have this book at hand).

Regarding the reason for this and its connection to the **Sephardim** specifically: It can be said that the ruling of the *Beis Yosef* and *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim* at the end of 261 is that the recitation of the aforementioned Psalm constitutes the acceptance of *Tosefet Shabbos* (additional Shabbos) and the prohibition of work. However, in these countries, they perform work even afterwards. The reason is that initially, when they began to recite this Psalm, it was not their intention to accept *Tosefet Shabbos* until the recitation of *Barechu* (see the commentaries on the *Shulchan Aruch* and our Rebbe’s *Shulchan Aruch* there). But the Sephardim, who in many matters follow the *Beis Yosef’s* approach, wanted to uphold his custom in this as well. And since in many synagogues the custom had already spread to perform work even after reciting the Psalm, they established reciting it a second time specifically to prohibit work.

**Note.** It is known that according to the writings of the Arizal (*Shaar HaKavanot*, *Maamar* 4; *Seder HaArizal* by R. Shmuel of Rashkov), the section from *Mizmor L'Dovid Havu* etc. until *L'Orech Yamim* should be recited once in the field and a second time in the house.

With wishes for all that is good,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 455

B"H, 23 Shevat, 5709

To the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing, Torah scholar, etc., Moreinu Harav Chaim HaLevi, may he live and be well.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 12 Shevat:

1) Thank you for writing regarding the well-being of Rabbi Yaakov Landau and his family, may they live, and regarding what transpired during the time that Rabbi Shmuel Palmer, may he live, was in Eretz Yisrael. As the saying goes, "While a woman speaks, she spins"—surely you did everything necessary to increase the assistance from the Chicago fund for Yeshivas Toras Emes.

2) The sicha of 10 Shevat and the kuntres for 10-13 Shevat were sent to you. Since that date has already passed, and the Torah is protective of the funds of Yisrael, it was sent via regular mail. Enclosed in this package is also a kuntres for Shevat for our relative, Rabbi Binyomin Shneurson, may he live, and a kuntres for 19 Kislev and a kuntres for Shevat for our relative, Rabbi Chaim Shneurson, may he live. Thank you for the trouble of delivering them.

3) As a participant in the work of Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch and Kehot, I am sending you the Sefer HaNigunim that was just published. I trust you will confirm receipt of the above.

4) Enclosed are letters for each of the aforementioned individuals, as I do not know their correct addresses. On this occasion, I would be interested to know if Rabbi Binyomin Shneurson has printed any further maamarim regarding Kehot, Merkos, etc.

5) Regarding your questions on the customs of the Yahrtzeit, I will answer once I have clarified them further; perhaps it will also be printed in the kuntres for 2 Nissan, G-d willing.

6) It is a pity that you have not yet sent the notes of your father, of blessed memory, regarding the Tanya. I had said that perhaps Rabbi Shmuel Palmer would bring them, and I have written to you about this many times.

7) We read here in the newspapers that in Yerushalayim they have been harassing yeshiva bochurim and chareidim in general. Please be kind enough to inform me of the meaning of this matter, so as to calm the spirits.

8) If it is possible for you to obtain a license for this, you could purchase a certain number of all the items printed by Kehot, especially now that the number of anash and temimim in Eretz Yisrael has increased.

With regards and best wishes for all that is good,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## Tnu

B"H, 26 Shevat, 5709

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished [scholar], etc., R' D. shlita,

Peace and blessings!

... Did you make the corrections in Likutei Torah according to what was written in the letter of 17 Teves? Also, what is the status regarding the printing of the Haggadah and "Hayom Yom"?

A rumor has reached here that they are printing "Sdei Chemed" in the camps. Please be kind enough to inform me if this is so, and to announce officially that the rights to print it belong to us, and that "Kehot" is already printing it.

I will await your letter as soon as possible, and I wish you all the best.

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***Tnu***

From a photocopy of the letter.

**R' D.:** Bravman. Additional letters to him appear above, 415, and in the references noted in the footnotes there. **That they are printing... "Sdei Chemed"... printing it:** See above, Vol. 2, letter 332, and the references noted in the footnotes there. Above, 450. Below, 493*, 512, 517-8.

## 557

B"H, 1st day of Rosh Chodesh Adar, 5709

To the honorable, G-d-fearing, full of Torah and wisdom, etc.,
Rabbi Chaim, may he live, known as Dr. Bloch

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter:

1) Regarding what I wrote in my previous letter, that there are several index books for the statements of our Sages in the Gemara, etc., whereas this is not the case for the literature of the Middle Ages—I did not intend to say anything other than this: while an index for the statements of our Sages in the Gemara merely adds to and completes what is already available, an index for the literature of the Middle Ages would be something entirely new. It would fill a noticeable void that, to my knowledge, no one has even begun to address.

2) It seems you understood my letter to mean that I was surprised that you included in your index even those statements of our Sages that had already been noted by previous indexers. In my view, the opposite is true: a reference book, such as an index, must be a complete work. That is to say, it should not require one to search in additional books, nor should one find in it only the author’s own unique findings. The conclusion regarding your **Heichal** in general—with an apology for my critique—is:

- (a) That it should have included all the statements of our Sages in the Gemara, etc., even those that had been recorded previously.

- (b) The *Kuntres HaHaganah*, which comprises about one-sixth of the entire book, has no place in this reference work.

- (c) At the end of my previous letter, a note was omitted regarding the statement of our Sages, "A blow that is not written in the Torah," which refers to the passing of the righteous, the source of which is often sought.

In the *Nitzutzei Zohar* by Rabbi Margolies, at the end of Zohar Vol. 3, he cites from the *Responsa Ateres Tzvi*, Yoreh Deah 146, that a similar expression—in Aramaic—is found in Zohar Vol. 3, 10b.

Signing with wishes for all the best,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 458

B"H, 6 Adar, 5709

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished R' Elchanan HaKohen,

Shalom u'Bracha!

Appreciating your earnest interest and your ongoing assistance and cooperation for the benefit of the "Merkaz L'Inyonei Chinuch," especially during this recent critical time, it gives me great pleasure to send you a gift on behalf of the "Merkaz L'Inyonei Chinuch" — the enclosed picture of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, shlita, as an expression of our deep appreciation and gratitude to you, dear friend.

You are surely familiar with the statement of our Sages (Sotah 36b) that seeing the image of a holy person provides strength to walk in the path of Torah and mitzvos. I wish that the image of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, shlita, may grant you renewed strength to do much good in general, and specifically in our shared work within the multifaceted activities of the "Merkaz L'Inyonei Chinuch," in which my father-in-law, the Rebbe, shlita, you, and I are deeply interested. May we all merit to see much nachas from this holy work.

With heartfelt greetings and best wishes to you and yours,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Chairman of the Executive Committee

#### ***458***

From the Secretariat's copy

**To the esteemed... HaKohen:** Also known as Alexander (Koven). Further letters to him appear above in Vol. 2, 189, and in the references noted there. See also 571 below.

## 359

B”H, 7 Adar, 5709

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and faithful communal leader,
Rabbi Y. HaKohen, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

Following our telephone conversation, enclosed is a check for five thousand dollars ($5,000) as a *gemilas chasadim*.

At the conclusion of your conversation yesterday, the Rebbe, my father-in-law, may he live and be well, wished you that you should be a *Graf*, an acronym for: **G**ut (good), **R**aych (wealthy), **P**rum (pious).

(In Yiddish-speaking Russia, *Graf* is an honorary title, roughly equivalent to "Earl" in English.)

Not being certain that you were able to decipher the aforementioned words, I am transcribing them here. Surely you will endeavor to be a vessel for this blessing.

Please confirm receipt of this letter.

Signed with wishes for all that is good,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Perhaps one can explain the order of the blessing:

**Gut (Good)** – The *avodah* of *tzedakah*. This is the introduction and preparation for the blessing...

**Raych (Wealthy)** – As our Sages, of blessed memory, stated (*Taanis 9a*): "Give a tithe so that you may become wealthy." This refers both to material wealth in the literal sense, and to internal wealth, for "there is no wealth other than *da'as*," as our Sages stated (*Nedarim 41a*): "If one has *da'as*, one has everything." This is the introduction and preparation for the blessing...

**Prum (Pious)** – For through wealth in *da'as*, one contemplates as is required and comes to love Hashem and fear Him, as is explained at length in *Tanya*. Then, one **wants** and desires to walk in the ways of the Ribono shel Olam, as our Sages explained (*Rambam, Hilchos Deos, Chapter 1, Halacha 5-6*): "And you shall walk in His ways"—thus they taught the interpretation of this **mitzvah**: Just as He is called "gracious," so too must you be gracious, etc. And through wealth in the literal sense, one is also **able** to fulfill this desire and will in practice.

***

From the Secretariat copy.

**Rabbi Y. HaKohen:** Katz. Additional letters to him appear above in 321, and in the notes referenced there. **Decipher:** During those years, the illness of the Rebbe Rayatz, of blessed memory, had intensified, and the articulation of his holy speech was difficult to understand.

## 460

460

B”H, 9 Adar, 5709

To the esteemed Rabbi, the Chassid, G-d-fearing and learned, etc.,
Rabbi A. A. [shlita],

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter:

The funds you calculated as the total for Rabbi R. S. H. [shlita] have been received, and he was notified of this in due time.

Enclosed is the Kuntres for Purim, that you may use it to bring merit to the public.

Once again, I wish to urge you regarding the great necessity of arranging for appeals to be made in your community for the benefit of the Merkos L’Inyonei Chinuch, which is in extremely dire straits. Or, at the very least, that the Merkos L’Inyonei Chinuch participate in an appeal (if there is no free day available) for a significant percentage. The days of Nissan are auspicious for such matters. Naturally, this is in addition to the efforts made with private individuals regarding general donations, or for printing, etc., etc. Surely in Baltimore there is also a Federation or other organizations that can be influenced to allocate support to the Merkos L’Inyonei Chinuch. They will certainly take note of the fact that we do not send meshulachim to Baltimore, and ultimately, we might be forced to do even that—if so, why should they increase the expenses? Further elaboration on this is surely unnecessary.

I trust you will do everything in your power, and according to the letter printed at the end of the enclosed Kuntres, your words will be heeded. May you be successful, and may you report good news regarding your activities, which are of the highest order.

Signed with wishes for all the best,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

**460**

From the secretariat’s copy.

**Rabbi A. A.:** Axelrod. Additional letters to him—above, 412, and in the notes indicated there. **Rabbi R. S. H.:** Apparently Kesselman.

**Letter printed:** Sefer HaMaamarim 5709, pp. 68–70.

651

Your tenth letter has just been received. As a token of appreciation, I am replying immediately, although I will send the letter tomorrow, when the *kuntres* for the upcoming Purim is completed, and I will enclose it...

With regards and with the confidence that you will continue sending your list of memoirs.

## 462

B"H, 10 Adar, 5709

To the G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [Minkovitz] shlita,

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter of 23 Shevat was received. It is surprising that you have stopped recording the holy stories and sichos that are in your memory. Surely you will continue, and I hope that, in the end, this will also result in a benefit for the many.

Enclosed is a kuntres that was just published, and I trust you will use it to benefit the many.

With regards to the entire group, may you have a joyous Purim.

#### ***462***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 557, and completed based on the secretariat’s copy.

**Moreinu Harav Y.:** Minkovitz. Additional letters to him appear above in Vol. 2, 362, and in the notes referenced there; and below, 469.

**Benefit for the many:** Through their publication.

## 453

B"H, 10 Adar, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, the Torah scholar, G-d-fearing, and distinguished,
Rabbi M. HaKohen, may he live,

Peace and blessing!

Your letters from the 10th and 22nd of Shevat were received, and I am enclosing the *kuntres* that was just published; may you merit to share it with the public. We searched here to purchase the *Kav V'Naki* for you, but it is not available from the booksellers.

Regarding what I emphasized concerning the reward of each participant in the *Chalukas HaShas*: it is that it should not merely be a portion of the entire *Shas* (for it is obvious that studying Maseches Berachos on its own is not the same as studying that same Maseches when it is also part of studying the entire *Shas*, as explained elsewhere that in such a case there is also the "light that excels above all" — *Yichudei HaAri*, end of Chapter 12), but rather that their reward should be as if they had studied the entire *Shas*. And the proof for this is that on Shabbos, every person is obligated even if they have only [studied] **one** *shiur*, meaning that each person is obligated regarding the whole.

Regarding what you wrote and asked me some time ago about why they did not want to be lenient—including the Tzemach Tzedek—in several details concerning the matter of an agent for receiving [a *get*], and you request a *psak din* from my father, my teacher and mentor, of blessed memory:

I have not heard anything from my father, of blessed memory, regarding this. Nor do I clearly recall his practice in this matter. However, in general, since that time, they have been lenient in several of the aforementioned matters due to the increased dispersion of the exiles and the distress of the times, including in spiritual matters. Such a matter should be clarified with the *rabbanim* of *anash*, who surely possess instructions **for practice** from the time following the First World War. And the Sages were diligent regarding the welfare of the daughters of Israel.

Wishing you a happy Purim and a joyous heart,

**M. Schneerson**

## 454

B"H, 12 Adar, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., Rabbi M. Chaikin, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

Your letter of 24 Shevat was received, and enclosed is the *kuntres* for Purim that has just been published, which I am sure you will use to bring merit to the public. The letters at its conclusion are particularly relevant to *anash* and the *temimim*, may they live, who have been brought to a corner where much work has not yet begun, and it stands and waits for a long time for a *p'loni almoni* to come and perform it. Certainly, there is room to act and distinguish oneself in the line of Torah (establishing regular *shiurim* for study in *nigleh* and *nistar*), in the line of *avodah* (conveying the importance of *tefillah* to the adults, and especially habituating the young in years or in mind to *tefillah* and *brachos*), and in the line of *gemach* (especially that the *tzedakah* should be for appropriate causes and *l'fum gamla*).

And naturally, my intention in the above is not directed to him alone, but also to his father-in-law, may he live, whom it seems necessary to awaken regarding the simple words of our Sages—*nevelta* (those who are called dead even in their lifetime) *bashuk* (for which one must go out to the marketplace, as explained in *Torah Or*, beginning of *Parshas Vayeshev*, and in many other places) *min haktanos or* (whose source is the sin of the *Eitz Hadaas*), *v'shakol agra* (for the poor person—in mind—does more for the householder than the householder does for the poor person). And do not say, "I am a *kohen* (and have no connection to the affairs of this world, according to what is written in Rambam, end of *Hilchos Shemitah V'Yovel*)," or "I am a great man (and I already have **abundant** lights—of *Tohu*—in **abundant** vessels—of *Tikkun*. See the enclosed *maamar*, Chapter 7)."

With wishes for a happy and joyous Purim, and regards to your family.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

454

From the secretariat copy.

**To the esteemed Chossid... Chaikin:** Additional letters to him—above, Vol. 2, 210, 235, 440.

**To his father-in-law:** Rabbi Eliyahu Shmuel HaKohen Kahanov.

## 465

B"H, 15 Adar, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Shneur Zalman, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

My letters, along with the Kuntres for Purim, have surely arrived; please confirm receipt.

... Regarding the Vaad of Nifneh, I ask once again about the Kabbalos.

Surely you will all consult together to find a proper strategy so that, G-d forbid, there will be no deficiencies in your avodah in Nifneh due to the departure of those who have already moved from your camp, or who will be moving soon.

Before signing off, I will reiterate that in my long letter, my intention was not directed at him personally, but rather at the **entire group collectively**. This is where the avodah of Nifneh is distinguished from other matters—similar to the example that in their essential vitality all are equal, and the difference exists only in the manifestation [of that vitality], and there is no need to elaborate further.

Give my regards to the entire group, may they live.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

465

## 456

B"H, 20 Adar, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Y. [Goldstein] shlita,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your two letters:

a) Regarding what you wrote concerning the Zohar (Vol. III, 292a) that Ze’er Anpin is "held and suspended" in Atik — this refers to the seven lower Sefiros (as explained in the maamar *Samchuni* 5660); it is only held and suspended there. See *Torah Or*, Parshas Yisro, the maamar beginning *HaAvos Hein Hein HaMerkavah*, and *Amrei Binah* by the Tzemach Tzedek, *Mitzvas Tefillin*, Chapter 2. As for what is written in various places that the Pnimiyus (inner dimension) of Abba is the Pnimiyus of Atik — this means that it **is** the very Pnimiyus of Atik itself, as is explained in various places in the *Hemshech* of 5666. See also the note in *Kuntres Limud HaChassidus*, p. 6. However, this applies specifically to the Pnimiyus of Chochma and Binah, not to *mochin* in general. b) Regarding what is written in various places that "Da’as includes Chesed and Gevurah" — see the explanation in the *Siddur*, *Shaar HaTefillin*, and the beginning of the Tzemach Tzedek’s *Mitzvas Tefillin*.

From your letters and questions, I see that in my opinion, you need to establish a structured order for your study of Chassidus: which seforim or maamarim to study first, which to study afterwards, and so on. You should consult regarding this with those who guided you in the study of Chassidus.

With wishes for all the best,

**M. Schneerson**

***

456

From a photocopy of the letter.

**R' Y.:** Goldstein. Another letter to him — see above, 433.

**In the note in the Kuntres:** See also above, Vol. II, letters 265, 287, 297.

## 457

B"H, 29 Adar, 5709

To the distinguished Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and esteemed, etc., Rabbi B.Z. [Shemtov] shlita,
Shliach

Shalom u’Bracha!

My letters, along with the Kuntres for Purim, surely arrived, and it is surprising that you have not confirmed receipt.

As far as I can gather from your letters, you are still staying in Paris. While it is difficult to say anything definitive from such a great distance, it is nonetheless a great pity and a source of wonder regarding the time that passes and does not return, while you are not in the place that was assigned to you by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe shlita, to be his shliach and representative. Perhaps one could say this is similar to the story that the Maggid entrusted his students with *yichudim* pertaining to different countries; only then, they spoke openly, and everyone saw that these were truly *yichudim*, whereas now, everything is concealed, garment upon garment, and therefore some might mistakenly say that the intention is [merely] the *galoshen* (galoshes).

But in truth, if the shliach knows the language and the person, and possesses a connection and internal recognition—to the point that he knows and breaks his own reality and *yeshus* (selfhood), [realizing] that he is sent by the *Meshaleach* (the Sender)—then if the *Meshaleach* is not concerned with the *galoshen* (according to the shliach’s understanding), then what does it matter to him?

I do not write this as a sermon, but rather to reiterate what is stated in the enclosed Kuntres, at the beginning of chapter 3: that every day is one of the days allotted to a person to serve Him, may He be blessed. And it is known from our Sages that the singer must assist the gatekeeper, etc., for his *avodah* is specifically in the place that has been assigned to his portion.

Enclosed is the Kuntres for 2 Nissan and a letter from Machne Israel; see the conclusion there, that this *avodah* specifically is the true greatness.

With regards and best wishes,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 558

B"H, 29 Adar, 5709

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, etc., R' Shneur Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I received your prompt letter. Yesterday, Mr. Mendel sent him the photographs along with a short biography, and I am certain that he will now arrange everything in the best possible way and inform me of the positive results.

Likewise, you will surely inform me in due course of the results of your proposal to your brother, may he live.

Enclosed is the Kuntres for 2 Nissan and a letter from Machne Israel; please acknowledge receipt.

You surely received in due time the letter from the general Melach [Merkaz L'Inyonei Chinuch] to the Rabbis, calling for action during the Yom Tov and throughout this month to assist in alleviating the financial situation of Melach. It is certainly unnecessary to prompt you again regarding this, and we await good news.

With regards to the Levintansky brothers, may they live, and with best regards,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 741

741

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Nissan, 5709

To the distinguished Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and esteemed, etc., Moreinu HaRav Shneur Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

1) My letters from Shushan Purim surely arrived in due time, and I am awaiting your response to them.

2) In addition to the telegram for his daughter’s wedding, may she live, I hereby double my blessings that the union should be a beautiful one and that they should build a faithful home upon the foundations of Torah and Mitzvos.

Our custom, as instructed by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, may he live, is to say *Samayach Tisamach* (with the first *mem* also vocalized with a *patach* and not a *tzere*). Perhaps one can suggest a hint for this based on Chassidus: because *patach* is in *Chochma*, the right line, whereas *tzere* is in *Binah* (note the concept of "she is my sister, the daughter of my father, but not the daughter of my mother, and she became my wife," in the *Biurei HaZohar* on Parshas Emor). May it be His will that **only** goodness and kindness shall find them, speedily and soon. See the additions to *Torah Or*, in the *maamar* beginning "Lehavin Shoresh Chalav U'Dvash," at the end of the first chapter, regarding the Arizal’s ruling that *Chochma* is **only** *Chesed*.

3) Your letter from the 22nd of Adar was received. For certain reasons, the notes you sent in your letter of the 8th of Adar have not yet been submitted, but I will bring them into the sanctuary of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, may he live, on the day of the *hilula*, the 2nd of Nissan. Surely, every delay is for the good.

4) Regarding your letters in the Sefer Torah, I will inquire.

5) Enclosed are the *kuntresim* for the 2nd of Nissan and a letter from the camp, so that you may merit the public through them.

Awaiting good news and sending regards to your family,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 472

B"H, Rosh Chodesh Nissan, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [shiyichye],

Greetings and blessings!

Enclosed is the Kuntres for the 2nd of Nissan that has just been published, along with a general letter from Machne Israel and a general letter from Merkos L’Inyonei Chinuch.

These are for the purpose of bringing merit to the public.

Needless to say, it is superfluous to point out to one such as yourself the great necessity of acting upon these matters in practice, as well as regarding the situation of Merkos L’Inyonei Chinuch, for the days of Yom Tov are also auspicious for this. I look forward to hearing good news.

With regards to your family, and with the blessing of a good month—the month of the true Redemption.

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***472***

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 23, p. 423, and completed according to a photocopy of the letter.

**Moreinu Harav Y.:** A copy of this was sent to many individuals. See the note to letter 471 below.

## 473

473

B"H, 5 Nissan, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., Rabbi Y. [Landau]
May he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby gratefully acknowledge receipt of your two letters, along with the copies of the conclusion of the letter from the Rebbe, our master and teacher, of blessed memory, regarding the Holy Tongue; a copy of the aforementioned letter from the days of 29 Tammuz, 4 Menachem Av, 6 Menachem Av of the year *Ezras* (5664), and 1 Rosh Chodesh Nissan (without the year indicated), and 18 Shevat of *Ateres* (5665). Thank you for the past, and I have a great request for the future: to continue in this manner with copies of these precious items. It goes without saying that all expenses involved in this (hiring a copyist and the like) are for me to cover, as per your instruction.

A special thank you for the copy of the letter from 29 Tammuz mentioned above, which appears to have been written to my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. This is especially appreciated, as my mother, may she live and be well, was unable to bring anything here from the holy manuscripts and books of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

I am currently preparing for print an index of the *maamarim* of the Rebbe, our master and teacher, of blessed memory. In those instances where there was no new *maamar*, but rather a repetition of one that had already been said previously, the text of the *maamar* is naturally not in his holy handwriting. I am searching for these in the notebooks and *hanachos* of *anash* and the *temimim*. Perhaps there are *maamarim* that you have heard or know about which are not in the proofs enclosed?

I saw your letter to my father-in-law, the Rebbe, may he live and be well, but I did not find in it what, in my opinion, is the main point: namely, the names of the people in the Holy Land, may it be built and established, who will bring into actuality what they decide to do regarding changing the state of Chassidus there. Many decisions have been made, committees have been chosen, etc., but due to local obstacles—the nature of which you surely know even more deeply than I—they have not yet come to fruition. The lengthiness of the discussion on the matter, which is present there, is certainly superfluous.

Enclosed is the *Kuntres* for 2 Nissan; please acknowledge receipt.

Signing with wishes for a kosher and joyous Pesach for you and your family, may they live.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 474

B"H, 6 Nissan, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, the Torah scholar, the G-d-fearing man, etc., Rabbi M. 
May he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I have finally received the small booklet of explanations on *Sefer Shel Beinonim* that R' S. Palmer brought, and I extend double thanks to you for this. I would certainly appreciate it if you would let me know if these are from the explanations of R' Sh. G. that he recorded for himself, and whether there is additional material that he would say orally during study, or if that is also included in the aforementioned booklet; for some of the precise points that were related to me in his name, I did not find in this manuscript.

Also, the booklet ends in the middle of *Perek* 44, and I do not know if this is all that was recorded or if the conclusion is missing. I offer my thanks in advance for clarifying this.

Enclosed is the *Kuntres* that was published recently, and I am sure you will merit the public with it.

I will conclude with wishes for a kosher and joyous Pesach, and regards to your family.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

 **Rabbi M.:** Axelrod.
 **R' S.:** Shmuel.
 **R' Sh. G.:** Rabbi Shmuel Gronem Esterman. See above, Vol. 2, Letter 313; above, 418.

## 375

7 Nissan, 5709

Mr. Boruch Litvin, may he live and be well,

Shalom u’vracha!

I have received your letter inquiring about how one "gets a handle on" (literally: "bites into") learning Tanya.

You do not explain clearly what you mean by the word "bites into," so I assume you simply mean **understanding.** It is just that there exists a perception that Chassidus in general, and Tanya in particular, are especially difficult and almost incomprehensible subjects, which is why you call the act of understanding them "biting into" them.

I must warn you right from the start that Tanya and Chassidus are, like other parts of Torah, accessible to **anyone** who truly wants to learn and understand them.

On the other hand, they possess a depth such that even the greatest sage in Chassidus cannot say that he has already "grasped" everything. In this respect, they are also the same as other parts of Torah.

The **proof** of this is that new maamarim and seforim are constantly appearing which, based on the principles of Torah, reveal new insights and explain what was said previously with ever-increasing depth and breadth.

The **reason** for these two aforementioned characteristics of Torah in general and Chassidus in particular—simplicity and infinite depth—which, on the face of it, are opposites, is because they are the wisdom of the Ribono shel Olam. Just as regarding the Ribono shel Olam: a) simple people also know Him, and b) even the greatest sage engaged in the wisdom of Elokus cannot grasp Him to His ultimate essence; so too is it with Torah, for "the Torah and the Ribono shel Olam are entirely one."

Regarding the order of study for Tanya and Chassidus Chabad—you do not write in your letter what you have already learned, or whether you are just beginning. If you are starting out, my suggestion would be—insofar as one can give advice without seeing the person—that the order should be: Shaar HaYichud VeHaEmunah, Iggeres HaTeshuvah, and then Tanya, Chelek Rishon.

The first time you learn it, do **not** stop on individual words or terms, even if they are not entirely clear, as long as you understand the general concept of the perek. To help understand the general concept of the perakim of Tanya, Chelek Alef, the *Kitzurei HaPerakim* by the Tzemach Tzedek (published by Kehot) are very helpful. After learning it through that way the first time, and if you also learn the maamarim from *Sefer HaMaamarim* from time to time, I expect that you will certainly be able to learn the Tanya more thoroughly, stopping in more detail on the subjects of the perakim.

It goes without saying that if you have questions regarding the above or regarding the study of Chassidus in general, and you think I can be of help, please write to me in detail and in whichever language is easier for you to express yourself, and I will answer what I know. I only want to stipulate in advance that if my response is delayed due to my work at Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch and the like, please do not take it amiss.

I hope to hear good news from you regarding your progress in learning Tanya and Chassidus, and I conclude with blessings for a kosher and happy Pesach.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**
Chairman of the Executive Committee

## 476

B"H, 13 Nissan, 5709

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, engaged in the work of Heaven, etc.,
Rabbi Y. Pleyer, may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

Following up on our conversation during your visit here, I am pleased to inform you that we have finally succeeded in overcoming the various obstacles and have published an English translation of the memoirs of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, may he live and be well, Volume 1, for the time being. A copy is enclosed herewith. I will conclude for now with a blessing for a kosher and joyous Pesach in every detail. And just as the calendar configuration of this year is the same as the configuration of the year of the Exodus from Egypt (Shabbos 87a), so may it be His will that in this year the complete and true Redemption will arrive, and our eyes shall behold it.

With regards,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***476***

From the secretariat’s copy.

## 157

B”H, Erev Pesach, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [shlita],

Greetings and blessings!

I have surely received your letter along with the kuntres. I am writing now to enclose the kuntres for the upcoming Yom Tov, which has just been published, and may you merit to share it with the public in an appropriate manner.

I will conclude with the blessing for a kosher and joyous Yom Tov. Just as the calendar arrangement of this year is the same as the year of the Exodus from Egypt (Shabbos 87b), may the prophecy be fulfilled in it: "As in the days of your exodus from the land of Egypt, I will show you wonders," and may our eyes behold it.

With regards and best wishes for all that is good, awaiting good tidings regarding your activities for the benefit of the Meluchah and the camp of Yisrael,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Regarding your comment on the well-known concept that the celestial spheres (galgalim) possess intellect, in contrast to what is written in Iggeres HaKodesh (108a) that in this world it is not so—this difficulty applies equally to human beings **in this world** (excluding souls **before** their descent). The matter, in my humble opinion, is that all existences in this world—which in its entirety is from the Sefirah of Malchus, the realm of action—where only Malchus resides within it, cannot grasp anything beyond this (see Tanya, chapters 36 and 39, and elsewhere). That is to say, for example, it is only an intellectual grasp of action alone. This is consistent with the well-known principle that it is impossible to know a soul within a body except for the **existence** of its spirituality, and not its **essence**. All the more so, the knowledge of the celestial spheres is only in this manner. See the enclosed kuntres, chapters 13 and 20; the Kuntres of 19 Kislev 5708, chapter 9; and elsewhere. Time does not permit me to elaborate.

#### 157

The first part was sent to many (see below, note to letter 151), and the addition in the margin was printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 14, p. 475.

**Just as the calendar arrangement:** See also the previous letter.

**The celestial spheres possess intellect:** Rambam, Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah, chapter 3, halacha 9.

**In contrast to what is written in Iggeres HaKodesh (108a):** "The angels and souls, etc., possess wisdom and intellect, etc., but this lowly world, etc."

**In the enclosed kuntres:** Sefer HaMaamarim 5709, p. 121; 130.

**Kuntres of 19 Kislev 5708:** Sefer HaMaamarim 5708, p. 88.

## 478

B"H, 3 Iyar, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., Moreinu HaRav Shneur Zalman, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

It is surprising that I have not yet received confirmation from you regarding the receipt of the letter and the *kuntresim*...

Enclosed is a copy of the committee’s list for the *Sefer Torah*, including the letters.

I am sure you will inform me in detail regarding the arrangements for the *nifneh* (status) once some of those involved in this matter have ascended to *Eretz Yisrael*.

To conclude with a topic of the day, regarding the *kiddush* of this *Rosh Chodesh*: the virtue of the month of Iyar over all other months is that **every** single day within it results in a *mitzvah* (the *mitzvah* of *Sefiras HaOmer*).

Signing with wishes for all the best and regards to all *Anash*,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

**Moreinu HaRav Shneur Zalman:** Kesselman. Further letters to him—see above, 425, and the references in the notes there. **From the committee’s list for the *Sefer Torah*:** *L’kabalas Pnei Moshiach*. See above, letter 445, and the references in the notes there.

**Nifneh:** Status/standing.

**Once some... have ascended to *Eretz Yisrael*:** See above, letter 465. Most of the *Anash* who left Russia to ascend to *Eretz Yisrael* did so in the month of Iyar, 5709. See *Igros Kodesh* of the Rebbe Rayatz, Vol. 10, letter 3779. **Kiddush of *Rosh Chodesh*:** Meaning, from the *sicha* of the Rebbe *shlita* on Shabbos *Rosh Chodesh* Iyar.

## 479

479

B"H, 3 Iyar, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, Torah-scholar, etc.,
Rabbi Chaim Yehuda Shmuckler

Greetings and blessings!

During our current review of the committee’s lists for the Sefer Torah, we also found the list of those who contributed through you. Since I am unsure if it was sent to you at the time, I am enclosing a copy herewith; please be so kind as to confirm its receipt.

To conclude with words of Torah that are "good" (Yerushalmi, end of Berachos): It is known even in the revealed dimension of Torah that Iyar is an acronym for Avraham, Yitzchak, Yaakov, Rachel (Even HaEzer, 126, Beis Shmuel, subsection 2), who are the *Merkavah*. There is both a virtue and a limitation in the *Merkavah*: the *Merkavah* is something separate from the rider—unlike the body and the soul, and the like—yet its *bitul* is of the highest order. This is a general lesson: the concept of the month of Nissan, which implies *nes* (miracle), refers to matters of *is'arusa d'le'eila* (arousal from Above) that are revealed to them. But afterwards, there must be *avodah* and the *bitul* of the separate entity—*is'arusa d'letata* (arousal from below). Only after these two can one receive the Torah and *mitzvos* in the third month, the month of Sivan.

Best regards to the entire group, and wishing you all the best.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

479

From the secretariat copy.

**For the Sefer Torah:** To greet Moshiach. See above, Letter 445, and the references in the notes there.

**The Merkavah is something separate:** See above, Vol. 2, Letter 234.

## 480

B”H, 10 Iyar, 5709

To the esteemed Rav, G-d-fearing, learned, etc., R’ D. shlita,

Shalom u’vracha!

1) Your letters from the 27th and 29th of Nissan have been received, along with further proofs for the *Piskei Din*. It is surprising that you have not yet sent all the proofs for the *Shulchan Aruch*. Please hasten to send them, as well as the title pages for the *Piskei Din*.

2) In the meantime, you have surely received my letter from Rosh Chodesh Iyar with the *sicha*, as well as the corrections for the previous proofs. In the current proofs, the following must be corrected: In *Parshas Devar*, line 11: "In his *chiddushim* on the *Shas* that are in print," should read: **"In his *chiddushim* and *she'eilos u'teshuvos* that are in print."** There, second line from the bottom: "Yoreh Deah, Vol. 2," should read: "Yoreh Deah, Vol. 3." There, after the word "Orach Chaim," there are three apostrophes, and there should be only one. Regarding the indices: Since I have seen that the printers are creating a new, sequential pagination anyway, it is best that the index reference the pages according to this pagination, which is continuous from the beginning of the book to the end. You must also indicate the pages for the *hashmatos* (omissions) and *he'aros* (notes) (in the index of Vol. 2).

3) Following the decision of my father-in-law, the Rebbe shlita, we telegraphed you on Sunday to hasten as much as possible to complete the printing of the *Shulchan Aruch* and the *Piskei Din* at the **earliest possible** time, since you have already begun them. It is not worthwhile to bind them there; rather, as you suggested, do it like a brochure, fasten them, and send them also at the **earliest possible** time via travelers. We are also making efforts regarding sending a car for this purpose.

It is possible that it will arrive in the coming days, and for this reason as well, everything must be ready.

...I reiterate the instruction of my father-in-law, the Rebbe shlita, to **hasten** the above. I look forward to hearing good news **as soon as possible** regarding the fulfillment of our requests as detailed above...

Awaiting letters with good news and regards,

**M. Schneerson**
Chairman of the Executive Committee

## 380

380

B.H., 12 Iyar, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [may he live],

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter has been received. I am enclosing the *Likutei Dibburim* for the upcoming Lag B’Omer, and you will surely merit to share it with the many in the most appropriate manner, as explained in the aforementioned *sicha*, section 2, and in many other places.

Perhaps one might suggest a connection between these matters: Lag B’Omer is alluded to in the verse [Bereishis 31:47] regarding what is written, *Gal Ed* (a heap of witness). It includes two aspects: a heap between the "upper whiteness" of holiness and Yaakov, and a heap between holiness and the *sitra achra*, as explained at length in the *Siddur*, *Shaar Lag B’Omer*. This is why Lag B’Omer also includes two aspects: the ultimate ascent of Rashbi, the inner dimension of Torah that he revealed specifically on this day, and the *hoda'ah* (gratitude) of *hoda'ah* (the Sefirah of Hod), which is the *avodah* of completely simple people, as explained in the *Kuntres* for this Yom Tov.

It could be said that as long as the Baal Shem Tov was hidden, he was also in the category of the "upper whiteness" of holiness. This is what is explained in the *Reshima* (in the *Likutei Dibburim* enclosed, p. 29) that when the time of his concealment ended and he needed to be revealed, the sign of this is specifically *Gal Ed*. And it is known what the Baal Shem Tov said (printed in the *sicha* of 20 Kislev 5693 and in *HaTamim*, Vol. 4, p. 18) that when he finished learning Torah with his teacher, Achiyah HaShiloni—(see in *Sefer HaSichos* that Rashbi is his reincarnation; and see *Yerushalmi, Berachos*, ch. 9, halacha 2)—he was revealed to the eyes of **all** of Israel.

With wishes for all the best,

**M. Schneerson**

* * *

The state of health of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, shlita, has improved. May Hashem continue to grant him a complete and speedy recovery, amen, may it be His will.

* * *

A response to your question from before the Yom Tov will come when I have a little time, and I ask for your forgiveness.

With wishes for all the best and the blessing of a "small Pesach"—*a kleineh sprung*—at least for the time being.

## 482

B"H, 14 Iyar, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [shiyichye]...

Regarding the matter of reviewing Maamarim of Chassidus so that the listeners will also understand them with human intellect—in general, such Maamarim have already been selected and written, for this very reason, in Yiddish. They have been printed in "Sefer HaMaamarim Yiddish."

Regarding why no proof was brought for sleeping at night in a Tallis Katan from the incident of the dead of the desert, this can be explained in several ways.

The simplest and clearest explanation is that in their time there were no **special** Tallis Katans; rather, they had tzitzis on the **garments** that they wore at all times.

It is obvious that they did not want to—and it is also possible to say that it was forbidden to—lie down naked. And this is understood. Although people were **usually** naked when sleeping, and therefore there is a proof from King David. See Tosafos in Bava Basra [74a] and Niddah 61b, and the halachic ruling in Hilchos Tzitzis. But this is not the place to elaborate.

***

482

In the previous edition, the letter preceding this one (see the note there), and the date in it was changed to 14 Iyar.

Printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 8, p. 310, and completed based on a photocopy of the letter.

## 583

583

B"H, 13 Iyar, 5709

The esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, etc., R' D. 

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter of 27 Nissan has been received. Enclosed is the *Likutei Dibburim* for the upcoming Lag B'Omer. You will surely merit to share it with **the many** in an appropriate manner, as explained in this *sicha* in section 2, and in many other places.

This should be connected specifically with Lag B'Omer, based on the teaching of our Sages (*Yevamos* 62b) that during the days of Sefirah [the students of Rabbi Akiva] did not treat one another with respect, and therefore [the plague occurred], whereas on Lag B'Omer they ceased [dying].

With wishes for all the best, and with the blessing of a *Pesach Ze'ira*—at the very least, *a kleine shprung* (a small leap) for the time being. I await your letters.

**M. Schneerson**

Please, I urge you to reply immediately to my letter of 10 Iyar.

***

## 484

484

B”H, 17 Iyar, 5709

To the G-d-fearing, honorable, and distinguished, who conducts his business with integrity, etc.,
R’ Y. HaKohen, may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I have received your letter of 6 Iyar, along with the enclosed schedule of the *sefarim*. As you noted in the list of *sefarim*, the books have been sent to you. Surely you have received them long ago and will inform me of this.

I am enclosing here the *sicha* from Lag B’Omer, to merit the public with it as well, for the merit of the public depends on him.

I received a letter from our friend Mr. Robinson, may he live, stating that he is currently short on funds and that we should wait to come to him until he notifies us. Presumably, you are aware of this letter.

I am currently in a great predicament: the printer wants money, and the *olam* wants the *sefarim*. Furthermore, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *shlita*, has complaints that we are printing too little in general and that we should work in this department (as well as in all others) more intensively—and then, all of a sudden, such a letter! I could not decide whether to show Mr. Robinson’s letter to the Rebbe, *shlita*—for he is not lacking in distress even without that.

I hope that you will take this matter into your own hands and see to it that it is carried out as quickly as possible with our friend Mr. Robinson. In the meantime, I am continuing with the work as before—I do not want the *kpidah* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *shlita*, neither upon myself nor upon him.

It is only a pity that, for the time being, Rabbi Kazarnovsky will not be traveling out to you this week. However, I want to hope that I will hear good news from you regarding this, so that he might be able to travel next week at the very least.

Awaiting your letter with good news soon, and wishing you all the best,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**
Chairman of the Executive Committee

## 485

B”H, Hilula of Rashbi, 5709
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing, Torah-scholar, and man of integrity, etc., R’ Shlomo Yosef Zevin, shlita,

Peace and blessing!

I happened to see, in the library of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, shlita, your book *HaMoadim BaHalacha*, second edition. In the chapter on Lag BaOmer, I found a citation of the *Pri Etz Chaim* (Dubrovna and Lashchov editions), *Shaar Sefirat HaOmer*, Chapter 7, in a footnote, which states: "The reason Rashbi died on Lag BaOmer is that he was one of the students of Rabbi Akiva who died during Sefirat HaOmer."

Now, it is well known that this passage is problematic, both from the perspective of the *nigleh* (the revealed Torah), since Rashbi was one of the five students whom Rabbi Akiva ordained later, and from the perspective of the *nistar* (the hidden Torah), based on what is explained in the *Pri Etz Chaim* there and in the *Siddur HaAriZal* regarding the level of the 24,000 students who died versus the level of the aforementioned five students. This was already addressed in *Responsa Divrei Nechemiah*, *Orach Chaim*, 34, subsection 7, where he concludes: "Perhaps there is a printing error in the *Pri Etz Chaim* there in what is written, 'because he is, etc.,' as mentioned above, and it requires further study, as I have heard that in the Koretz edition this is omitted."

And it is indeed true that in the Koretz edition of *Pri Etz Chaim*, this passage is not found. Similarly, in the *Siddurim* of the AriZal and in two manuscript copies of *Pri Etz Chaim* that are in the manuscript collection of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, shlita, this entire matter is absent. Furthermore, I have not found this explanation in any of the *Chassidic* discourses I have seen thus far.

The *Sefer HaKavanot* (Inyan Sefirat HaOmer, Discourse 12) states regarding the reasons for the customs of Lag BaOmer: "For Rashbi, peace be upon him, is one of the five great students of Rabbi Akiva, and therefore the time of his rejoicing is on Lag BaOmer." This is also the case in *Mishnat Chassidim*.

— It appears the author of *Divrei Nechemiah* did not see the *Sefer HaKavanot*, as he does not cite it — and for several reasons, it would be appropriate, in my humble opinion, to correct the aforementioned point in your book, or at the very least, to note the doubts surrounding the matter at the first opportunity.

By the way, in the aforementioned *Responsa Divrei Nechemiah*, there are several matters that touch upon the topics addressed in your article on Lag BaOmer in your book.

With respect and best wishes,

**M. Schneerson**

## 580

580

B.H., 2 Sivan, 5709

Mr. Boruch Litvin, may he live

Greetings and Blessings!

I received your letter in due time. Unfortunately, until now I have not had the opportunity to respond, due to the burden of preparing several items for print. Among them are the sicha for Lag B’Omer, the Kuntres for Shavuos, and the "Complete Story of Shavuos" (I have instructed that all of these be sent to you. The "Story of Shavuos" is enclosed here).

I was pleased to read in your letter about the strong and positive impression that the description—in the memoirs—of Binyamin Wolf and the other nistarim made upon the Yidden of your shul.

You should have specifically drawn their attention to two lessons that each of us, and they, must learn from these stories:

a) **Regarding others:** With every Yid one encounters, one must always remember that even if it appears that he is quite simple, or perhaps even worse—not honest, etc.—one cannot be certain of what he is in truth and in his inwardness. For nistarim have always existed among Yidden, and they exist now as well, and in much greater numbers than thirty-six. Consequently, one must judge him favorably, be careful with his honor, and strive to fulfill one’s obligation toward him in every possible way, etc.

b) **Regarding oneself:** Each of us possesses **nistar** (hidden) strengths, and if one only desires it strongly and exerts effort in this regard, Hashem will help him reach the greatest heights. For a Yid, when he connects himself through Torah and mitzvos to Hashem—Who is *bli hagbalah*, without any limitation whatsoever—has the power to break through all boundaries and limits. And therefore, Chazal say: A person is **obligated** to say, "When will my deeds reach the deeds of my forefathers, Avraham, Yitzchak, and Yaakov?"

How is your learning progressing? Both for yourself and with others? Surely you have a *kvius* for learning in public?

I conclude with wishes for the upcoming Yom Tov of Shavuos, that we may all receive the Torah with joy and in an inward manner, and that it may be for us a *Toras chaim*—not a Torah to which one pays respect while keeping it locked away so that it does not interfere with daily life, but rather a **Torah** that directs our lives and the lives of everyone.

With blessings for a happy Yom Tov,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

587

B"H, 3 Sivan, 5709

The proofs for the *Kuntres* for the upcoming *Chag HaShavuos* have arrived. With blessings for receiving the Torah with joy, and regards to your family.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**
