Especially one who finds himself in a city where Jewish activists are few, and he is a person of influence over a certain circle of people, and can become a person of influence over an even larger circle—he must certainly view himself as a soldier whom the millions of martyred kedoshim have stationed on the front lines of the thousand-year war being waged against Jews because "their laws are different from every other people" (of which the milah war you describe in your work is a part).

It is a war that, unfortunately, one must sometimes wage even against Jews who have erred in their way of life. Our duty is to ensure that they find their way back to the Jewish path and begin to *jalousement conserver toutes les prescriptions de la Bible et du Talmud* (zealously observe all the instructions of the Bible and the Talmud).

As a doctor, you surely know even better than I that when there is no healthy person present, the doctor must not say that the patient should wait until the doctor himself is one hundred percent as he should be, and only then attend to the patient. As we see from practice in such cases, even if the doctor himself is lacking something, the very act of healing another brings about, almost automatically, a healing of oneself.

***

Before I conclude, a remark regarding your work:

Page 51: Proceeding from the opinion that metzitzah is merely a health measure and has no connection to the religious aspect of milah, you suggest, in a book printed also for non-Jews, that the **governments** should intervene and **forbid it!**

Three objections:

(a) Page 54—where you discuss "milah" among Muslims, which is more dangerous according to your own description—you nevertheless exercise caution, and rightly so, writing that "one must hope that in time the interested peoples will understand how to utilize modern methods." Is it because Jews are in galus among the nations, and France fears the Arabs, that there must be such a distinction?

(b) No good has ever come to Jews from governments intervening with prohibitions and force in Jewish life, neither for their soul nor for their body, as shown by numerous examples from Jewish history, of which you are surely aware.

(c) Around 200 rabbis, among them also **doctors**, after having examined the various reasons you bring in your work, have come out with full strength for the absolute necessity of metzitzah. See the material in *Sdei Chemed*, Vol. 12, *Kuntres HaMilah VeHaMetzitzah* (Warsaw, 1902).

By the way: Page 15, 16th line from the bottom: *avant* should be *apres*. Page 15, 7th line from the bottom: should be *Perse* instead of *Babylonie*. Page 20, 13th line from the bottom: should be 1135 instead of 1171.

I am certain that each of us, in his own way, desires only the truth, and therefore you will not take offense at me for having taken the liberty to note here a few thoughts that clarify points in your work.

With respect and wishes for a *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah*,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 520

B"H, 13 Elul, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [Yisroel], may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

Enclosed is the Kuntres for Chai Elul and the third day of Selichos, which has just been published. May you merit to share it with the public in the most appropriate manner.

The saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, is well-known (Sicha of Chai Elul, 5700): that the Baal Shem Tov and the Alter Rebbe both drew down into every single Jew *Chai* — inner vitality — in the avodah of Elul: "I am to my Beloved and my Beloved is to me."

In Chassidic thought, it can be said that in this very avodah, the Baal Shem Tov acted primarily from above to below — through mofsim, the **foundations** of the teachings of Chassidus, and his travels **to** the recipient. The Alter Rebbe, however, acted primarily in the manner of below to above.

This can also be connected to the two matters mentioned at the beginning and end of the aforementioned maamar: 1) To inform the baal teshuvah that "with You is forgiveness." 2) To effect that it should be "for the sake of fear" — an essential, independent yirah. But this is not the place to elaborate.

With blessings for a *ksiva v'chasima tova* and regards to your family,

**M. Schneerson**

***

Enclosed is the Kuntres for Chai Elul and the third day of Selichos, which has just been published. May you merit to share it with the public in the most appropriate manner, to make them a *yachid* (a singular entity), through hiskashrus of the *yechidah* (the highest level of the soul), by means of *yechidus* (whether in person or in writing) with a *Rebbe Yachid* (a unique Rebbe), to the *Yechido shel Olam* (the Unique One of the world)...

Your two letters with the enclosed memoirs were received in due time. In the coming days, the receipts for the second set of memoirs will surely be sent as well.

It is surely unnecessary to urge you to also awaken those who have moved from your camp, from time to time, regarding the *nifneh* (the gathering/position).

***

Your letter from 3 Menachem Av was received in due time. Surely, during this time, the matter of your family’s arrival has already been arranged; please inform me regarding this.

It is surely unnecessary to urge that the days of favor of Elul and Tishrei are most auspicious for drawing the sparks closer to the Source. There is a **connecting** intermediary: "I stand between you and Hashem your G-d," and this is his merit.

***

Your letter from 14 Menachem Av was received. Surely you will gladden us with good news of actual deeds regarding the *nifneh* and the merit of the public, to make them a *yachid*, through hiskashrus of the *yechidah*, by means of *yechidus* with a *Rebbe Yachid*, to the *Yechido shel Olam*.

***

Your letter from the 15th and the letter from 25 Tammuz were received in due time. Surely you will continue to record your memories for the *nachas ruach* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe.

Regarding what you wrote: when his intent is for the *Chiddushei Tanya*, he explains that for a new thing to exist, there must be a power of the *mechadesh* (the innovator).

- The story of the Tzemach Tzedek that you bring, that he said he "bathed in tears"—I heard that the *shakla v'tarya* (discussion) regarding this was with Harav Hatzadik [Moreinu Harav Y.Y.] Cherkasser, the brother-in-law of the Tzemach Tzedek and his contemporary.

521

Your letters were received in due time. Surely, you are already accustomed to my delayed responses. What can I do? The preoccupations do not diminish; on the contrary.

Many thanks for your participation in the meeting with Rabbi Herzog and for the report regarding this. There is hope for positive results from this concerning some of the matters of the refugees of Anash in Eretz Yisrael, and a word to the wise is sufficient.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the words of the Ramban at the end of Parshas Acharei Mos, that there are three categories of Kares, and among them is one where only the nefesh is cut off while the person continues to live long, and this is what is meant by the verse "that **nefesh** shall be cut off," etc.—and these are those whose sins are many.

You raise a difficulty, that on the face of it, this removes the difficulty of the Alter Rebbe in Iggeres HaTeshuvah, Chapter 4. You suggest that this is why the Alter Rebbe said "many, many," implying that surely among them there are also those whose sins are not many.

—In my humble opinion, it is impossible to resolve it this way:

1) According to the Alter Rebbe’s explanation there, even when their sins are many, it is impossible for them to live, since the cord is severed and they have no vitality from any other place. It is a great stretch to say that when one’s sins are many, he becomes in this detail like the entire nation after the Churban.

2) At the end of that chapter, the Alter Rebbe brings the verse regarding Kares, "that **nefesh** shall be cut off," etc.—which proves **explicitly** that he does not hold by the opinion of the Ramban.

Furthermore, Rabbeinu Bachya also writes there in accordance with the opinion of the Ramban. However, the Alter Rebbe’s view is based on what is found in the writings of the Arizal (in Likkutei Torah to the Arizal, Parshas Bo; in Sefer HaLikkutim, Parshas Lech Lecha; and see also Sefer HaGilgulim, Chapter 6, and Eitz Chaim, Shaar Olam HaTohu, Chapter 3), which interpret "that nefesh shall be cut off" as Kares of the body, without making any distinction at all.

Regarding what you wrote concerning the words of the Meiri (Kiddushin, beginning of Chapter 5, s.v. *VehaMishnah Ha'a*), regarding the needs of **Shabbos enjoyment**, perhaps he intended to align with the words of the Alter Rebbe in Shulchan Aruch Orach Chaim, 250, Kuntres Acharon, subsection 2—it is a beautiful allusion, but it requires study in the commentary of the Meiri whether he is so precise in his wording.

As for your question regarding the intent of Rashi’s commentary in Kiddushin (40a, s.v. *Amar*): In my humble opinion, it is because, according to the simple reading, the statement of Rav Tzadok, "he who does this, eats this," is not understood at all (and see the Rif on the Ein Yaakov). Especially since eating non-kosher food is an explicit prohibition from the Torah punishable by Malkos, while relations with an Aramean woman are only subject to Rabbinic lashes of rebellion.

And according to Rashi’s version, that Rav Tzadok said this to himself and not to the Aramean woman, what is the content of "he who does this, eats this"?

Therefore, Rashi explains that Rav Tzadok was wondering why the one who cohabits with an Aramean woman is worthy of non-kosher food. And it is a great lesson. For in the case at hand, all of this happened under duress. It is known that the Holy One, Blessed be He, does not bring a stumbling block to the righteous, but specifically in matters of eating (Tosafos, Gittin 7a, s.v. *Re'a* and elsewhere). And when the first duress occurred, this, on the face of it, is something that can happen even to the righteous. But when Rav Tzadok saw that this was connected to the eating of non-kosher food, he learned from this that cohabitation with an Aramean woman is also in the aforementioned category. Consequently, he could also learn what his own level was, and the like. It is worth noting the Avos D'Rabbi Nosson (Chapter 15:2) regarding a similar event with Rav Tzadok HaKohen **Tanna**. But in Kiddushin, on the face of it, it speaks of an Amora, as it is brought between two Amoraim.

Regarding your difficulty with what I wrote in the ruling that great Baalei Teshuvah, etc., that this is also clear in the **Bavli**, and not a dispute, because there is no Halacha like a student, etc.—I have not found this rule in the Shas itself (even if it were found in the Shas as a **general rule**, and not in relation to this specific dispute, I would doubt if this is a complete clarification, when it is clear elsewhere to the contrary).

—If the rule applies even when they did not dispute face-to-face, see Sdei Chemed, Vol. 1, Rule 161, that this is the opinion of the Rif, etc.

## 522

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished [name], Moreinu HaRav Y. [Horowitz], may he live.

...Your letter, along with the photograph, has arrived safely. Surely there are others among Anash who will also send their photographs in due course. This is especially true during these days of *ratzon* for *baalei teshuvah*. As is known, the first forty days—which were days of *ratzon*—were on the level of *tzaddikim*, while the final forty days were for *Bnei Yisrael* as *baalei teshuvah*.

These days, I received—sent from New York itself—the sefer *Keser Pardes HaAretz*, without any accompanying letter of explanation. If it is a *tishurah* from you, I am deeply grateful that you have honored me with it.

Because of the importance of the matter, even though time does not permit me to study it as thoroughly as required, I will provide a few brief notes in haste:

1) **Page 60:** Regarding Aristotle and his book *Yavo Shiloh*, I do not have it. As for what you wrote regarding *Sefer HaGilgulim*, Chapter 7, I have not found it there, nor in other places in the writings of the Arizal, for the time being. However, "we have not seen is not proof," and I would be grateful if you could inform me of its location. What is written in *Seder HaDoros* on this matter, citing several seforim, is well known.

2) In the beginning of the *Responsa* section, page 80-81: Note what is written regarding the laws of *berirah* based on the ruling of the Tzemach Tzedek, Part 2, page 24 and onwards, and in *Shaar HaMiluim*, end of Part 3.

3) **Page 107:** Seemingly, since the one asking named the child after the deceased and not after the living, there is no concern regarding changing the custom of Yisrael. Especially since he did not know that there was a relative living by that name. Nothing remains but your reasoning that "one who is particular, etc."—and do not open [a door to such concerns]. Also, what is written in *Sefer Chassidim*, section 460, seems superficially to imply that it only speaks of naming after one's living **father**.

4) **Page 105:** My father, my teacher, of blessed memory, and my uncle, the esteemed Chossid, of blessed memory, married two sisters according to the instruction of the Rebbe [Rashab], with the directive that they should not live in the same city. The questions raised by the *Noda B'Yehuda* from the Gemara have already been answered in the sefer *Mekor Chesed* on *Sefer Chassidim*, that on the contrary, "go and see what happened in the end," as the story of our Sages relates (Yerushalmi, *Taanis* ch. 4, halacha 5; *Bava Basra* 12a, and *Ana Basrai*).

5) **Page 123:** Regarding the instruction that he should deliver the *get* to the witnesses via *mesirah*, and afterwards the signing witnesses should sign and he should deliver it again: Seemingly, for several reasons, it is better—at least *l'chatchilah*—that they write a **different** *get* and sign, etc.

6) In the beginning of the *Drush* section, page 213: The question of the manuscript. Note the concept that knowledge of the *Seder Hishtalshelus* is a lofty and exalted mitzvah (*Tanya*, 156b) and from *Shabbos* 2a.

7) On the *parsha* of the week—pages 501-502—and in continuation of what you wrote regarding "I declare this day to Hashem your G-d that I have come, etc.": It is not understood at all. The matter is: "And you shall come to the Kohen"—not to the king or the leader. "Today..."

"I have come"—as a newcomer. For the fact that one is found today in the Holy Land is only because he merits it today; his own merit is not enough. It is only because Hashem swore to our forefathers, and only after this admission, "and he shall take, etc."

## 523

523

B"H, 21 Elul, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, etc., R' Y. [may he live],

Greetings and blessings!

I am writing now to send my blessings for the coming year, for a good inscription and sealing in all matters, for you and for all who are yours, may they live.

Enclosed is the *Kuntres* for Rosh Hashanah, which has just been published. In these days, when the individual is like the community of the entire year (*Rosh Hashanah* 18a), you will surely endeavor that every single individual whom you influence will study this *Kuntres*, in its entirety or at least in part. The heart of a Yid is awake, especially at a time when the *Maor* is close to the *nitzotz* (*Kuntres HaAvodah*, end of Chapter 5).

Regarding this year, when the first day of Rosh Hashanah falls on Shabbos:

It is known that the first day is *yoma d'dina kashya* (*Pri Etz Chaim*, *Shaar Rosh Hashanah*, Chapter 4, and elsewhere), and through the *tekios* they are sweetened. Our Sages explained in *Likutei Torah* and elsewhere that on Shabbos there is no need for *tekios*, because all the effects of the *tekios* are accomplished now from Above. It is understood, by extension, that regarding the spiritual aspect of *tekios*—which is the matter of *teshuvah*, as the Rambam writes and as is explained in the enclosed *Kuntres*—it is likewise accomplished from Above, whereas in other years it must be accomplished through *is'arusa d'lesata*.

Generally, matters that come through *is'arusa d'lesata* have the advantage of being permanent; the lower realm is refined, and in such a manner the Supernal intent is fulfilled, which is specifically the service of the lower realm through *Torah* and *mitzvos*.

The advantage of matters of *is'arusa d'le'eila* is that they are of an incomparable elevation, since they are granted from Above.

All of this is explained in many places.

From the above, it is understood that the advantages of both modes are present on Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos.

See in *Likutei Torah*, *Shir HaShirim*, the *maamar* beginning "Lehavin..." regarding the *is'arusa d'le'eila* and the greatness of the elevation therein.

One may further sweeten this: that there, and in the *maamar* "Rani V'Simchi" in *Torah Or* and *Sefer HaMaamarim*, the discussion concerns the time of *Le'atid Lavo*, and therefore the *is'arusa d'le'eila* does not require any connection to *is'arusa d'lesata*. This is not the case in our situation, which precedes the time of *Le'atid Lavo*, where a grasp—at the very least—in *is'arusa d'lesata* is required. Therefore, the very fact that we do not blow the *shofar* on Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos, because it is accomplished through *is'arusa d'le'eila*, comes through the command of the Torah, and thereby fulfills the decree of our Sages. However, it is in a negative sense; it is not a *mitzvah* but only a decree and a safeguard, and this is not the place to elaborate further.

With blessings for a good inscription and sealing for you and for all your household,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**
Chairman of the Executive Committee

The *Kuntres* for 18 Elul was sent to you, as well as to R' M. Perlov [may he live], according to their address in the hall, and they should request them from there.

The content of his letter from 12 Elul was delivered and received—with a smile of satisfaction.

## 524

524

B"H, 22 Elul, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [may he live],

Greetings and blessings!

As we approach the coming year, I extend my wishes that you and all that is yours be blessed with a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year in every respect.

Enclosed is the *kunteres* for Rosh Hashanah that has just been published. "A generous eye shall be blessed" — may you merit to share it with the public in the most effective manner.

Regarding a hint for this year:

(Rosh Hashanah 4:1) "The Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos: in the *Mikdash* they would sound [the shofar], but not in the *medinah* (the provinces)."

The mitzvah of *tekios* on Rosh Hashanah is essentially *teshuvah*, as explained by the Rambam in *Hilchos Teshuvah* (Chapter 3, Halacha 4), and as clarified at length in the enclosed *kunteres*.

— One may note the precision of the Rambam’s language there, "on Rosh Hashanah," which serves to exclude the *tekios* of the Yovel, which contain a different allusion. —

*Teshuvah* is commensurate with the **quality** of one’s conduct throughout the days of the year, and **pertains** to one’s conduct throughout the days of the year. Therefore, for one who conducts himself in the manner of the *medinah*—with the thirty-nine categories of labor of the weekdays—his Rosh Hashanah and his *tekios* also "fall" on the weekdays. However, for *talmidei chachamim* who have nothing in their world but the engagement in *Torah u’mitzvos*—"the needs of Heaven, not your own needs" of the weekdays (*Shabbos* 113a)—and are therefore called "Shabbos" (*Zohar* Vol. III, 29b; and alluded to in *Shabbos* 119a: "R. Ami and R. Assi... if R. Yochanan were to visit us..."), for them, the law is that of a Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos. Therefore, they do not sound the shofar in the *medinah*, for they have no engagement with it at all. This is *kal v’chomer* from the *Kohen Gadol*, for a *talmid chacham* takes precedence over a *Kohen Gadol* (end of *Horayos*), and regarding the *Kohen Gadol* it is said, "He shall not depart from the *Mikdash*," and his Torah protects him.

— To note from Rambam, *Hilchos Kelei HaMikdash* 5:7; *Bava Basra* 8b, "Even now I am thinking of them..."; *Tosafos* s.v. *zeh*, *Sotah* 21a, and elsewhere. —

However, do not think that there is no *tekios* at all for a *talmid chacham*. For in the *Mikdash*—a place that has no connection to the matters of the mundane, for "a person may not enter the Temple Mount..." (end of *Berachos*)—they would sound the shofar. For even in this, there must be *teshuvah*, similar to the teachings of our Sages in *Berachos* (28b) regarding R. Nechunya ben HaKaneh and R. Yochanan ben Zakkai, the incident of R. Akiva according to the explanation of the *Ri of Corbeil* in *Tosafos* s.v. *mevatlin*, *Kesuvos* (17a), the incident in the *Yerushalmi*, *Pesachim* 3:7, and elsewhere.

May the Ribono shel Olam return us, very soon, in complete *teshuvah*, and may we merit **immediately** (in the language of the Rambam, *Hilchos Teshuvah* 7:5, which the Alter Rebbe cited in *Iggeres HaTeshuvah*, Chapter 11) the complete Redemption through Mashiach Tzidkeinu.

My letter with the *kunteres* for 18 Elul was surely received on time.

Signing with the wish for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**M. Schneerson**
Chairman of the Executive Committee

## 425

26 Elul, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R’ Y.Y.

Peace and blessing!

Approaching the coming year, I send my blessing to you and all that is yours for a year that is good and sweet in every respect.

It is well known that the term *Rosh Hashanah* carries two meanings: 1) The **beginning** of the year (for years—time of *chol*—and for *shemitin*—time of *mitzvah*, etc.), and 2) The **head** (*rosh*) of the year. As is explained in *Likutei Torah*, *Parshas Tavo*, in the *maamar* beginning "Hayom Hazeh": "Therefore it is called *Rosh Hashanah* and not the beginning of the year, for just as there is a *rosh* aspect in the soul—which is the head and intellect relative to the body—so too is there a *rosh* aspect in the year, and that is *Rosh Hashanah*."

The meaning of this in a person’s *avodah* to his Creator is as follows: Just as in a person’s conduct the intellect must rule over the heart, and the head directs all the limbs, so too is the state of the Jewish soul during the *Yomim Nora'im*—when the *ma'or* (the Luminary) is drawn close to the *nitzotz* (the spark) (and see *Yechezkel* 9:1, that Yom Kippur is also called *Rosh Hashanah*), and especially on *Rosh Hashanah*. One must exert influence so that the conduct of the entire year aligns with the person’s standing and desires on *Rosh Hashanah*.

Now, in *Likutei Torah* there, it is explained in the *he'arah* (note) and *ha'arah* (illumination) following the aforementioned words: It is the aspect of *gulgolta* (the skull) that covers **the *mocha*** (the brain), which is the aspect of the revelation of the Supreme Will.

This means: The concept of the "head" of *Rosh Hashanah* is not intellect and comprehension. For if one were to begin his order of *avodah* with the premise that he must first understand and comprehend before he acts—and if he does not understand, he does not agree to act, or at the very least he waits—this is the opposite of the intent of *Rosh Hashanah*, which is the revelation of the Supreme **Will**, *gulgolta* that covers **the** *mocha*.

For the *avodah* must be in the acceptance of the **yoke** of His Kingship, may He be blessed—a *geshmaker yoke* (a pleasant yoke)—as our Sages of blessed memory said (*Berachos* 17a): "**Our will** is to do Your will."

And especially in this year, when the first day of the *Yom Tov*—

from which the counting of the year now begins (see Rashi and Tosafos to *Menachos* 100b; *Tzafnas Paneach* by R. Y. Rozin of Rogatchov, *Hilchos Yom Tov*, Chapter 1, Law 22)

—falls on Shabbos, when mundane labors are prohibited and only *cheftzei shamayim* (matters of Heaven), **in that which is desired in Heaven**, are permitted (*Shabbos* 113a).

This means the *hisorerus* (awakening) on this *Rosh Hashanah* is of a different nature, and its effect on the entire year will be with added strength and abundant blessing.

I conclude with the wish for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* and all the best, and I look forward to receiving good news from you—good in material matters and good in spiritual matters.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**
Chairman of the Executive Committee

***

Regarding what you ask in your letter for instructions: Our Sages of blessed memory have already provided a general instruction in their interpretation of the verse in *Yeshayahu* 58: "Is it not to share your **bread** with the hungry, and bring the poor who are cast out to your **house**; when you see the naked, that you **cover him**"—this speaks of one who is naked of *mitzvos*, hungry for the bread of Torah, and lacking the shelter of the house of prayer (and it is understood that if one does not feel his hunger, his poverty is all the greater). And the verse adds and concludes: "And do not hide yourself from your own flesh!" An instruction for the entire duration of a person’s life in a single verse!

## 526

26 Elul, 5709

To the esteemed and distinguished individual, occupied in communal affairs, etc., Rabbi Y. [Yisroel]—may he live and be well,

Peace and blessing!

As the New Year approaches, I wish you and all yours a *shanah tovah umetukah* (a good and sweet year) in all respects.

You have surely heard the teaching of the Baal Shem Tov that from everything one sees or hears, one can derive a lesson for life (see also *Zichronos*, p. 168).

What can we learn from this year’s Rosh Hashanah in particular?

Rosh Hashanah 5710 falls on Shabbos.

Rosh Hashanah has two meanings: 1) The beginning of the year. 2) The head of the year (as stated in *Likutei Torah*, Parshas Tavo, in the *maamar* beginning *Hayom Hazeh*: "Therefore it is called *Rosh Hashanah* [Head of the Year] and not *Techilas Hashanah* [Beginning of the Year], for just as there is the level of the 'head' in the soul, which is the head and intellect relative to the body, so too there is the level of the 'head' in the year, which is Rosh Hashanah"). This means that just as the head must direct the entire body, so too must Rosh Hashanah—meaning the resolutions and the way one takes upon oneself to conduct oneself in *Torah* and *mitzvos* in daily life—direct the life of the entire year.

Two of the lessons of Shabbos are: 1) Shabbos **stresses** to us that the **Ribono shel Olam** is the Master of the entire world; consequently, everything depends solely on **Him alone**. 2) Shabbos means that one should not occupy oneself with mundane labor, but rather dedicate oneself to *Torah* and *mitzvos*.

A Rosh Hashanah that falls on Shabbos should awaken and strengthen us even more to make a resolution with our whole heart to conduct ourselves throughout the entire year by: (1) Not forgetting that in every **place, time,** and undertaking, only the **Ribono shel Olam** is the Master; (2) Always remembering that the ultimate purpose of the weekdays is to connect them with the spirit of Shabbos (which is why we say *Hayom yom echad **b'Shabbos***, *sheni **b'Shabbos***, etc., and not *echad, sheni b'shavua*).

This means that every thing, no matter how mundane it may appear in our eyes, we must turn into a matter of a *mitzvah*, a spiritual matter. *B'chol derachecha da'ehu*—in **all** your ways you shall know Him, the **Ribono shel Olam**, and connect yourself with Him.

And when one takes this upon oneself with a whole heart, the *Ribono shel Olam* helps one to be able to carry it out in life throughout the year.

(And in *Likutei Torah* there, it explains in the *he'arah* and *ha'arah* following the aforementioned words: "It is the level of *gulgolta* that covers **the brain**, which is the level of the revelation of the *ratzon* [will] of the *Ein Sof*." This means: The concept of the 'head' of Rosh Hashanah is not intellect and comprehension. For if one begins his *avodah* with the premise that he must first understand and grasp it before he will act, and if he does not understand he does not agree to act, or at the very least he waits—this is the opposite of the intent of Rosh Hashanah, which is the revelation of the **will** of the *Ein Sof*, *gulgolta* that covers **the brain**. For the *avodah* itself must be in *kabbalas ol* [acceptance of the yoke] of His sovereignty, may He be blessed—a yoke, but a pleasant yoke, as our Sages of blessed memory said (*Berachos* 17a): "**Our will** is to perform Your will.")

I conclude with a blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*, and with the hope to hear good news—good in material matters and good in spiritual matters.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**
Chairman of the Executive Committee

## 527

B"H, 27 Elul, 5709

...Regarding what you wrote that you heard in the name of the Rogatchover, that the dispute regarding the matter of Tzimtzum is the very same dispute as that concerning the name Tzevaos (Shevuos 35b) — it seems the basis for this report is what is written in Tzafnas Paneach, Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah, Chapter 2, Halacha 5. However, there he explains the reason for the dispute regarding Tzevaos as being whether the conduct of the world is by way of "cause and effect" or whether it also attains, at least to some degree, the Essence itself — and it seems his intent here with the term "attains" is more than mere intellectual apprehension; rather, it also implies an *hemshech* and unification. For it is obvious that even an effect attains its cause, as is written in the Rambam there and in Chapter 3, Halacha 7.

But as for the actual hint that the dispute regarding Tzimtzum is related to the aforementioned dispute, it does not appear so to me, for the Tzimtzum preceded **all** the names and all the worlds, and is not limited to Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah, as I have already written in my previous letter.

However, it is possible to say that the dispute regarding Tzevaos is dependent upon this: that the Kav, which returned and shone from the Ohr Ein Sof after the Tzimtzum, shines only until the end of Atzilus, yet it is present in a concealed state in Beriah, Yetzirah, and Asiyah as well. See Torah Or, Parshas Bo.

I conclude with blessings for a good and sweet year, and may you be inscribed and sealed for good, for you and all that is yours, and with regards to the entire group, may they live.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 528

B"H, 28 Elul, 5709

To the esteemed Rabbi and Chassid, G-d-fearing and distinguished, etc.,
Rabbi Avraham Aharon HaKohen, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

Your letter from the day before Rosh Hashanah was received, and as you requested, I mentioned it at the Tziyun, together with the kvitel and the Pidyon Nefesh of four shekels. Enclosed is a receipt for the fifth, for the Chassidic discourses.

Regarding the witticism, "and you shall give a fifth to Pharaoh" (*Bereishis 47:24*): through this, "all the lights are revealed"—the revelation of the Ohr Ein Sof, B"H, through the study of Chassidus—and "the four parts shall be yours" for your needs, for children, life, and sustenance in abundance.

This is based on what is written in *Etz Chaim*, *Shaar 31, Chapter 7*, in the *Likutei Torah* of the Arizal on *Parshas Vayigash*, and in the second *maamar* of *Shir HaShirim* beginning with the words "I am black," Chapter 2.

Regarding your note concerning the Tosefta at the end of *Kiddushin*, which states that the reasons for the Torah were revealed to Avraham—yet it is well known that the reasons for the Torah were not revealed:

This can be explained in several ways: 1) In *Sanhedrin 21b*, it is explained that the reason they were not revealed is that those to whom they were revealed stumbled in their observance of them. If so, this does not apply before they were commanded regarding Torah and mitzvos. Furthermore, Avraham knew many chapters of *Avodah Zarah* which have not reached us. 2) See Rashi in *Sanhedrin* there, that it speaks only of the reasons for the *chukim* (statutes), and even in the Tosefta, it does not say that *all* the reasons were revealed to our forefather Avraham. 3) The reasons for the Torah will be revealed in the time to come, as Rashi explains on *Shir HaShirim 1:2*, and the Almighty gave the Patriarchs a taste of the World to Come (*Bava Basra*, end of the first chapter; **see there in Tosafos**). Time does not permit me to elaborate.

I will conclude today with the appropriate sentiment: may you be inscribed and sealed for a good and sweet year, you and all that is yours, in everything, from everything.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Your letter regarding the note in *Torah Or* certainly arrived.

Enclosed is the *kuntres*.

## 529

B"H, 28 Elul, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Y. [may he live],

Greetings and blessings!

I am writing now to send my wishes for a good and sweet year, to you and to all that is yours, in all matters of goodness.

In these days, when the Source of Light is close to the spark and every individual is like the entire community of the rest of the year (Rosh Hashanah 18a), the responsibility and duty of every person of influence is all the greater to utilize their influence within their entire environment to strengthen Torah and Yiddishkeit.

This is especially true in this year, when the Yom Tov of Rosh Hashanah falls on Shabbos, for the beginning and head of the year is when "new souls are crowned and blessed with joy and a shining countenance" (Zohar Vol. III, 135).

Signing with the blessing of *Kesivah v'chasimah tovah*,

**M. Schneerson**
Chairman of the Executive Committee

#### ***529***

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 17, p. 542, and completed according to a photocopy of the letter.

The letter was sent to various individuals (see above, note to letter 481).

## 530

B"H, 3 Tishrei, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [may he live],

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your wishes for Rosh Hashanah:

May the one who blesses be blessed with an abundance beyond the primary measure, with a *chasimah v’gmar chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year in all respects.

"A sign is a thing" (*Horayos 12a*), especially on Rosh Hashanah. And since this is the beginning and the "head" of the year—for in the head all the limbs are included, and from it the vitality of all the limbs is drawn, as is explained at length in the *maamarim* of Rosh Hashanah—it is like the arrival of Shabbos, when "all judgments are removed, and all of Israel is adorned with new souls" (*Zohar III, 135b*). May it be the will [of Hashem] that all of this be drawn down throughout the entire year in a revealed manner, in goodness that is visible and manifest.

With wishes for a *chasimah v’gmar chasimah tovah* for you and your family [may they live],

**M. Schneerson**

* * *

I was pleased that you overcame your nature [in a matter of dispute]... Enclosed is the *maamar* for Shabbos Teshuvah that discusses this. Surely you will merit to share it with the many.

Regarding what you requested from the publisher through me—it is surely available and ready with Harav A. [may he live] in Paris. If not, please be kind enough to inform us in detail as to what, how much, and in what manner.

#### 530

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 14, p. 376; completed based on a photocopy of the original letter.

The letter was sent to several individuals.

## 351

B"H, 6 Tishrei, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Y. [Chitrik], may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

It is with joy that I inform you that the second *kuntres*—which you, may your light shine, merited to successfully facilitate the publication of—has finished being bound. Since it will only be brought from the bindery tomorrow, I will hasten to send you the final proofs now, so that they reach you before *Simchas Torah*. This will allow you to share the merit with those around you, and furthermore, it will be a "word in its proper time."

As is known, there are two aspects to Torah: 1) That which is above time; this is why anyone who occupies himself with the laws of the *olah* offering is considered as if he had offered an *olah*, and "our lips compensate for the bulls," even though it is nighttime. 2) That which is specifically within time, such as the ordinance of Moshe Rabbeinu regarding the public reading of the Torah. It is explained in many places that this relates specifically to the matters of that week, or his ordinance to expound on the laws of the holiday during the holiday, and all the more so the ordinance to expound on the matters required at that specific time (see *Shulchan Aruch Admur HaZaken*, beginning of *Hilchos Pesach*, where both are present). Seemingly, it is simple that the first aspect is loftier than the second. However, based on what is explained at the end of the *maamarim* for *Rosh Hashanah* and *Yom Kippur* regarding the superiority of revealed *ahavah* and *yirah* specifically, it is for this very reason that the second aspect of Torah possesses an advantage over the first.

And if this is true regarding the revealed Torah, then all the more so regarding *Toras HaChassidus*, which must specifically be *tapara da-placha* (an axe for the tree), and which deals with the healing of the illnesses of the soul—which are more severe than the illnesses of the body, *chas v'shalom* (see the *maamar* for *Yom Kippur*, at the end)—where a "word in its proper time" is of the utmost importance.

Especially the "proper time" of the *Aseres Yemei Teshuvah*, which possesses an additional advantage over the entire year, for then there is a drawing near of the *Maor* (the Luminary) to the *nitzotz* (the spark). See the *maamarim* regarding the *bitul* of the light to the *Maor*.

With blessings for a *chag sameach* and a *gmar chasima tova* for you and for the entire group, may they live.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 532

B"H, 13 Tishrei, 5710

To the esteemed Rabbi, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, etc.,
Moreinu HaRav R' A. A. HaKohen, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I received your letter today, and as you requested, I delivered your request and your handwritten note into the inner sanctuary.

Today is the yahrtzeit of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, which is an *eis ratzon* (a time of favor). Enclosed is the *kuntres* for 13 Tishrei and the upcoming Yom Tov of Sukkos, and a *kabbalah*.

In anticipation of the upcoming Yom Tov, I wish you a *chag sameach*, a threefold joy:

a) The *avodah* of Hashem with joy and a glad heart, as with all *mitzvos*.

b) *Moadim l'simcha*, as with all the *moadim*.

c) *Zman simchaseinu* (the time of our rejoicing).

May it be the will of Hashem that this "threefold cord" shall not be broken, and may this joy be drawn down throughout the entire year.

It is worth noting that Sukkos is [the time when] "His right hand embraces me," as is also explained in the *maamar*, and they correspond to the *Ananei HaKavod* (Clouds of Glory), for these two matters are in the category of *Kohen*.

With wishes for a *chag sameach* and all the best,

***

532

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 7, p. 391, and completed according to a copy.

This letter, except for the first and last sections, was sent to many individuals on the date of 14 Tishrei.

**Moreinu HaRav R' A. A.:** Yalles. Additional letters to him appear above, 502, and in the references noted there.

## 533

533

B”H, Erev Chag HaSukkos, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu HaRav Y. [shlita],

Greetings and blessings!

Enclosed is the Kuntres for 13 Tishrei and the upcoming Chag HaSukkos. I trust you will have a generous eye to share it with everyone you influence.

This is especially relevant during Sukkos, regarding which our Sages of blessed memory said that all of Klal Yisroel are fit to sit in one Sukkah, and the Arba Minim signify the unity of all types within Klal Yisroel, as is also brought in the maamar.

May it be the will of Hashem that through this, we will merit, "On that day, Hashem will be One and His Name will be One."

Wishing you a happy holiday and regards to all those you influence,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***533***

From the secretariat copy. Sent to many.

## 334

[Erev Chag HaSukkos, 5710]

Regarding the matter of the two sisters, where you raised a difficulty that there is no proof from the Yerushalmi (Ta’anis, Chapter 4, Halacha 5) — the proof that they do not succeed is from the **old** version of the text there and in Eichah Rabbah, Chapter 2, *v’tlonhu b’chada gufna* (and they hung them on one vine). And the proof from Rami bar Chama is that Rami bar Chama did not live a long life.

... What I noted as being difficult is the statement, "I declare **today**," regarding events that occurred several hundred years ago. And I noted, "To the Kohen and not to the King," because such stories are more appropriate for a head of state, not a Kohen. And how I explained "Today... I have come," is that each and every day one must merit **anew** to dwell in Eretz Yisrael. This is based on what is written, "And the land will not vomit you out," etc. This refers to the galus of the individual, not necessarily the destruction of the Beis Hamikdash.

My main intention was regarding the matters of this year, concerning the termination of the Mandate, etc., and a word to the wise is sufficient.

May Hashem merit us in this year, the year of *Shas* (5710), to the true and complete Geulah, and may all of Yisrael **unite** and sit in one **Sukkah** — meaning in the mitzvah, and in particular, this mitzvah which comes as a remembrance of the Clouds of Glory, so that there will not be, chas v’shalom, any among Yisrael whom the cloud ejects.

With wishes for a Chag Sameach.

#### 334

A portion of this letter was printed in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 19, p. 510, and a portion on p. 521. It is a continuation of Letter 322 above.

## Error

26 Tishrei, 5710

Mr. Boruch Litvin, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letters and questions:

**Question A:** In the *Yehi Ratzon* recited between the *tekiyos* according to some versions, it says: "Vayeshuah Sar HaPanim." You were told that the name of "that man" [Jesus] was surreptitiously inserted there.

**Answer:** Much has already been written on this (Sefer *Minchas Elazar* — printed in Munkacs, 5662 — Part 1, Siman 75, and Part 2 at the end; Sefer *Ketzah HaMatteh* — Kleinwardein, 5683; Sefer *Elbonah Shel Torah* — Berlin, 5689; the journal *Nerot Shabbos* — Jerusalem, Erev Rosh Hashanah, 5706).

The conclusion reached is that it should read "Vayeshaiah" — or "Vayeshaiahu" — "Sar HaPanim," which is one of the names of the angel Metatron. This name was chosen because its numerical value is 400, the same as "Eliyahu HaNavi Zachur LaTov," and also *KRK* (an allusion to *Tekiah, Teruah, Tekiah*). And **this is how it is found printed in old Machzorim and Seforim.** It is likely that missionaries surreptitiously changed the name to "Vayeshuah." **Note:** The Baal HaTanya, as well as the Vilna Gaon and other Gedolei Yisrael, rule that one should not recite this *Yehi Ratzon* at all.

**And such is our custom.**

**Question B:** In *Sefer HaMaamarim* p. 216, it states that R' Nosson lived "according to the laws of the Torah without any concessions of *midos chassidus*," and then it says that in "midos he did little." How do these two statements reconcile?

**Answer:** Working on *midos* — love and hatred, fear and throwing off the yoke, compassion and cruelty, etc. — consists of two parts: first, that the *midos* should follow the dictates of the intellect (*atkafya*), and **afterwards, transforming** the *midos* (*atahpa*). For example: a person has a competitor in his business, and in the same district at that. When he allows himself to be led by his *midos* and his *nefesh habehamis*, the *midah* of hatred toward his competitor is aroused within him. Then the intellect of his *nefesh ha'elokis* comes along and tells him: "You are a believer that the **Aibishter** is the Master of the world. So, logically: if Hashem has decreed your livelihood for you, how can your competitor possibly take it away, contrary to the will of the Creator? If, however, it was decided **from Above** that you should have less livelihood, you must realize that if not for this competitor, there would be no other way for it to be carried out from Above; and if so, he is not taking anything away from you at all. So why should you hate him?" After much reasoning, he feels the hatred cease to act in deed, then also not in speech, and finally not even in thought. But it has not yet reached the stage of *atahpa* to love.

The *nefesh ha'elokis*, however, goes further and tells him: "Love your fellow as yourself." You are a specialist in his line, and it **cannot harm you**, so help him out with good advice, with a loan, and the like. Eventually, the hatred is **transformed** into love, and he acts accordingly. It is specifically this second part that is called **truly** working on *midos* and *midos chassidus*, even though, according to the strict letter of the law, one could get by with an excuse and fulfill his obligation with the first part alone.

**Question C:** In the aforementioned *maamar*, R' Avraham is presented as someone who was not a *lamdan*, but a *baal midos tovos* and *midos chassidus*, higher than R' Nosson, who was a great *lamdan* but did not do enough in *midos*. It is therefore no wonder that others were against Chassidim who elevate an *am ha'aretz* who is a *baal midos* above a *talmid chacham*.

**Answer:** If so, they must also be against the Gemara which states (*Taanis* 7a): "A *talmid chacham* who is not decent... slay him," and the Rambam rules in *Hilchos Deos*, Chapter 5, how a *talmid chacham* must conduct himself, and writes (*Hilchos Talmud Torah*, Chapter 4) that if he does not conduct himself in this way, one must not learn Torah from him. Even more sharply, the Gemara says in another place (*Yoma* 86a), and the Rambam rules this as *halacha* (*Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah* 5:11), that one who is a great *talmid chacham* and pious, but his conversation with people "is not gentle and he does not receive them with a pleasant countenance, but is a man of strife and anger," is a **mechallel shem shamayim**, for which nothing helps except death!! Even more sharply, the Gemara says in *Yoma* (9b) that at the end of the Second Beis HaMikdash, they were occupied with Torah and *mitzvos*, and why was it destroyed? Because there was *sinas chinam*!

Think about this: Jews were learning Torah, they were fulfilling *mitzvos* — meaning they did not rob, did not steal, did not cheat, and did not shame others — but they were unwilling to act *lifnim mishuras hadin* (*Bava Metzia* 30b), and there was the *midah* of hatred, not the *midah* of love. Because of this, the Beis HaMikdash was destroyed and Jews have been in *galus* for almost 1900 years! At the end of the First Beis HaMikdash, however, when they committed major sins and there was a rejection of Torah, the *galus* lasted only 70 years!

Can there be any doubt after all this how highly *midos tovos* and *midos chassidus* are valued?!

With blessings of all the best,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Response to your previous letter at the next opportunity.

## 536

B"H, 26 Tishrei, 5710

The esteemed bochur, the G-d-fearing, the chossid, Shmuel, may he live.

Shalom u’vracha!

In response to your question regarding your late father, may he rest in peace, who passed away on 8 Cheshvan: his casket was shipped on 14 Mar-Cheshvan, and the day of the burial was 7 Kislev. You are uncertain how to conduct yourself regarding the recitation of Kaddish, etc. As is evident from your letter, he was **not** buried between the 8th and the 14th of Mar-Cheshvan. I have not heard specific customs regarding these matters, and as for what is written in the books of rulings and the customs of the halachic authorities, there are elders among the rabbis of anash in our camp who can instruct you according to the accepted practice.

Regarding your statement that you began saying Kaddish on 14 Mar-Cheshvan: although this is the opinion of several later authorities in Shulchan Aruch, Yoreh Deah, sec. 377, sub-sec. 4, it seems to me that you should have begun on 8 Mar-Cheshvan, in accordance with the ruling of **Admur HaZaken** in Shulchan Aruch, Orach Chaim, sec. 71, sub-sec. 1.

In brief, in my humble opinion: The recitation of Kaddish should begin on 8 Mar-Cheshvan, as mentioned above. The period of mourning begins on 14 Mar-Cheshvan (Yoreh Deah, sec. 375, sub-sec. 2). The conclusion of Kaddish recitation should be eleven months from the day of the **burial**. In your specific case, where this is significantly removed from the day of death, the eleven-month period will be shortened by several weeks (by combining three perspectives: a) that Kaddish is only relevant after the burial; b) that there are customs to recite Kaddish for twelve months minus a few days—see Knesses HaGedolah, Yoreh Deah, sec. 373; Birkei Yosef, sec. 376, sub-sec. 8; c) that one who recites it for twelve months is praiseworthy (Chanoch Beis Yehuda and Elef HaMagen). There is no contradiction here with the aforementioned ruling of Admur HaZaken, because Kaddish serves several purposes: it is a merit for the soul, and therefore one begins **even before** the burial; it is an honor for one's father, and therefore one recites it even while in the state of *aninut*; and it saves one from the judgment of Gehinom, which begins **after** the burial, and therefore that is the point from which the eleven-month count begins. One need not fear that onlookers will think you are labeling your father a wicked person, for even if they are unaware that the burial was delayed by several days from the day of death, they will see that you stop saying Kaddish **before** the completion of twelve months from the day of death).

The Yahrtzeit should be observed on the day of the **death** (see *Hayom Yom*, p. 19). Although in this case it is significantly removed and there is a dispute among the later authorities regarding this (brought in the book *Yalkut Das V’Din*, p. 107), one should also include the opinion that it is better to advance the Yahrtzeit rather than delay it (Chasam Sofer, Orach Chaim, sec. 161). **For extra** measure, in the **first** year, you may study Mishnayos, lead the prayers, etc., **also** on the day of the burial.

May it be that speedily in our days the rectification of the sin of the Tree of Knowledge—which brought death into the world, as recounted in the weekly parsha—will be completed, and "He will swallow up death forever," and we will merit eternal life and the life of the Living, as explained in the teachings of the words of the Living G-d in the *hemshech* of Rosh Hashanah 5711.

With wishes for life, blessing, and peace,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 537

B"H, 28 Tishrei, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., R' Z. shlita,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from the day after Sukkos.

I asked the esteemed Chossid, etc., R' Sh. L. shlita, and he told me that he has already written to you regarding the question of the man whose mother remained in the city of Kiev, Russia.

This is a question that must be asked **of the Rebbe**, and surely he will do so.

(As a matter of law, though not for practical application, in my humble opinion, he should choose a day—without delaying it—on which he will lead the davening from the amud, light candles, study Mishnayos, give tzedakah, and the like, but **not** call it a Yahrtzeit.) I must be brief due to the holiness of the approaching Shabbos.

I will conclude with a saying of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, shlita: "I will say a word from the Rebbeim, in the name of our holy Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe, which he heard from his Rebbe, the Maggid, in the name of his Rebbe, the Baal Shem Tov, may he rest in Eden: *Bereishis bara*—'Bereishis' is Chochma, 'the beginning of Chochma is the fear of Hashem.' The light and vitality of Chochma that shines on Shabbos Bereishis is drawn down upon the entire year, for all the days of the entire year receive from Shabbos Bereishis!"

With wishes for all the best and success in your holy work,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

537

From the secretariat copy.

**R' Z.:** Pozner.
**R' Sh. L.:** R' Shmuel Levitin.

## 538

B"H, 1 Mar-Cheshvan, 5710

To the Honorable Rabbi and Chossid, distinguished scholar, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. Halevi,
Shalom U’vracha!

I received your letter of the 26th of Tishrei, as well as your book *Yavo Shiloh*. Many thanks for this cherished gift, and also for pointing out the location in *Shaar HaGilgulim*, Part 2, regarding Aristotle.

I do not have the Alberstam edition, but I have at hand the Radomsk edition (also printed in Premishlan, 5635). I saw there—in the section *Seder HaGilgul*, entry *Mem-Ayin-Beis*—what you wrote in your letter to me. I subsequently also found it in *Seder HaDoros*, Part *Toldos Adam*, entry *Avtalmus*, which references the book *Gilgulei Neshamos* at the end of the volume, and *Chasdei Hashem*, *Ein Ha-5*, *Kaf-He*. Regarding what is written in all the above as a **known** matter, that Avtalyon taught students who were not proper and they became Tzedukim—**it requires further study** (*tzarich iyun gadol*) as to where this is recorded. Were I not afraid, I would say simply that in the original manuscript it was written **"Ant"**, referring to Antigonus of Socho, and the copyist confused the *nun* with a *beis*, and interpreted the abbreviation as Avtalyon. However, it is difficult to dismiss the printing of all the aforementioned books, and also *Midrash Talpiyos*, entry *Gilgulim*, in all of which it is written Avtalyon.

Regarding what I noted concerning the *get* (in *Pardes HaAretz*, 123 and onwards), in my humble opinion, there should be **two** *gittin*, rather than having two witnesses sign **on the get itself** that was initially given via *eidei mesirah* (witnesses of delivery) alone, and then delivering it **a second time**. My reasons, in brief, are:

1) Because, *lechatchilah*, the instruction to the scribe and the writing (of the first line) must take place in the presence of the witnesses of the signature (*eidei chasimah*). They must be aware of it, rather than being present merely by chance. In the manner you describe, it is impossible for them to be present, as one cannot reveal to the *shaliach* **in advance** that they will give the *get* twice. Even though, seemingly, this could be rectified by subsequently appointing the *eidei mesirah* as *eidei chasimah*, as you wrote in your book, page 130; nevertheless, even though regarding the *krisus* (severance), the action of *eidei mesirah* according to Rabbi Elazar is equivalent to the action of *eidei chasimah* according to Rabbi Meir, regarding the **document** of the *get*, it is clear that the role and responsibility of the witnesses **signing** the *get* is greater than that of *eidei mesirah*. Even according to the view that they sign on the *maneh* (the monetary obligation) in a document, their observation during the instruction and writing—when they are only *eidei mesirah*—is consistent with that status. This is particularly relevant in this case, as there is room to say that *eidei chasimah* are required *mid'oraisa* (by Torah law) since one can say the husband desires it; similar to the case of one who immerses for *terumah* and is [thereby] impure for *kodshim*.

2) We write the *get* in the name of the husband, lest one forget to transfer ownership to her before the delivery. In this case, too, there is this concern if they give **one** *get* **twice**, in addition to the concern that she might not return it to the *shaliach* properly after the first delivery—especially since she has only just received the *get* as a complete gift (unlike taking the *lulav*, where one gives it initially on condition that it be returned).

3) If you were to say that the primary act is the first delivery, and the second time is only *miderabanan* (by Rabbinic decree) because of the condition, etc.—it turns out that by the first delivery, this *get* document has already fulfilled its purpose. It requires study and investigation whether it is possible to use it **a second time** for the purpose of a divorce. (Another similar inquiry: can one divorce with one *get* twice, for example, if he divorced her and then remarried her on the same day?) A distant analogy: *kodshim* (sacrificial items), once their mitzvah has been performed, are no longer subject to the laws of *me'ilah* (misuse of sacred property).

4) If you were to say that the primary act is the second delivery—then when they give the *get* the first time and the *shaliach* says she should be divorced by it without the *eidei chasimah*, etc.—it can be compared to one who says, "This is like a shard," etc., although one can find a distinction, as is understood. One could elaborate on every detail mentioned above, but time does not permit, and I only intended to offer a note.

I will conclude, as I began, with thanks for your gift and with best wishes.

## 339

B"H, 4 Mar-Cheshvan, 5710

To the esteemed Rabbi and Chossid, G-d-fearing and distinguished, etc., Moreinu HaRav Y. 
May he live.

Greetings and blessings,

My letters from before Rosh Hashanah surely arrived on time, and I am writing now regarding the present matter.

They are currently occupied in [our] location with the construction of a rainwater mikveh, and they have asked if there are any specific customs or details that anash are particularly careful about or strive to beautify. Since I know that you were among those involved in such matters in Rostov, and that you heard several points and instructions regarding this from my father-in-law, the Rebbe,

I would be very grateful if you would write to me about this in as much detail as possible. It would be even better if you could include the reasoning for the sake of completeness, if it is known to you.

It is unnecessary to explain that this request of mine also applies to instructions regarding other matters of Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim, Yoreh Deah, etc., that you have received, but it is especially urgent regarding the mikveh, as mentioned above.

With thanks in advance, and wishing you a complete recovery and all the best,

**M. Schneerson**

## 540

B"H, 8 Mar-Cheshvan, 5710

To the esteemed Rabbi and Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, etc.,
Rabbi A. [Abramson], may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 26 Tishrei:

I am not aware of any special stringencies in the construction of a mikveh that only anash are particular about, as I am not familiar with the customs of others in this regard.

I have written regarding this to the esteemed Rabbi Yaakov Landau, who was involved in the construction of the mikveh in Rostov under the instructions of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. If he informs me of any new developments in this matter, I will, of course, inform you. It is surely unnecessary to state that one must be meticulous that the rainwater reservoir itself **always** contains a generous measure of 40 se'ah. The aperture for *hashakah* (connection) must be above this measure. The mikveh in which one immerses must be connected to the reservoir at the time of immersion. The pipes through which the rainwater flows into the reservoir must not be curved. Regarding what you wrote in your letter about "removing the water from the **reservoir** via a pump," in my opinion, this is not correct, as the leniency of the Chasam Sofer in this matter has **not** been accepted by us—to initially render the mikveh kosher via *hashakah* to the reservoir, and then to empty the reservoir, refill it, and render it kosher via *hashakah* to the mikveh (unless your intention is that one would subsequently wait until rainwater collects in the **reservoir** once again). It is well known that we strive to construct a mikveh in a manner that satisfies many different opinions.

As a note: when a woman of short stature comes to immerse, we remove a portion of the mikveh water—naturally, only from the amount exceeding the generous measure. Therefore, in many places, the pump is installed not in the mikveh itself, but in a water collection area connected to the mikveh by an aperture. In that case, one must be careful that they do not remove the water—meaning, the pump must not operate **during** the immersion—because this would cause the water to become *zochalin* (flowing water), as is obvious.

The funds were received on time.

I conclude with regards to your family, may they live, with best wishes for all that is good.

**M. Schneerson**

## 541

8 Marcheshvan, 5710

To the venerable Gaon, the G-d-fearing, learned, and elder scholar sitting in the Yeshiva, etc.,
Rabbi Y. [Steif], shlita,

Shalom u’Bracha!

I am writing to acknowledge receipt of your books on the Torah, on Bereishis and Noach. As discussed, a separate package has been sent to you containing our recent publications: the *Sefer Taamei HaMitzvos* by the Tzemach Tzedek and the *Kuntresim* 69–72 by the Rebbe Rayatz.

It is surely unnecessary to ask that if you find additional copies of your books, you send them via those who do His will. It is understood that further books published by us will be sent to you; thank you in advance for the past and for the future.

As a token of appreciation, immediately upon receiving the books, I leafed through the pages, and I would like to offer a few notes, even though I am stating them in haste, as time does not permit me to elaborate as required.

1) At the beginning of your book: Regarding the *Masorah* of the large *Aleph-Beis*, concerning what you wrote in *Menoras Shlomo* that contradicts the *Masorah* of *Divrei Hayamim*, and you write that the *Menoras Shlomo* did not have our *Masorah* before him. The *Sefer Menoras Shlomo* is not in my possession, but in my humble opinion, it is clear that there are two approaches regarding the large *Aleph-Beis* (and likewise the small *Aleph-Beis*): a) That the aforementioned *Aleph-Beis* is found in the *Mikra* **alone**. b) That the aforementioned *Aleph-Beis* is dispersed throughout the **entire** *Tanach*. Perhaps it can be suggested that this depends on the two opinions regarding whether, had our ancestors not sinned, they would have been given only the *Mikra* and the Book of *Yehoshua* (*Nedarim* 22b), or [that even so] all **twenty-four** books would have been given (see *Iyun Yaakov* on the *Ein Yaakov* there). The Book of *Yehoshua* is not relevant to the case at hand, as it contains no unusual letters (see *Minchas Shai*, *Yehoshua* 14:11).

2) There, in the section *Limudei HaShem*, subsection 1: That at the beginning of every *maaseh* (act of creation), the heavens and the earth were created. And in subsections 2 and 3: That *chomer hayuli* (prime matter) was created, and that time is a created entity. If so, this contradicts what you wrote in subsection 1, for it is obvious that the order of precedence is: time, *chomer hayuli*, heavens and earth. And if the precision is specifically regarding the beginning of every **act**, then the four elements preceded the earth, as written in the *Ramban*. It should be noted that the *Ramban* wrote that there were two types of *chomer hayuli*, one for the heavens and one for the earth, and his opinion is cited in *Etz Chaim*, *Shaar Kitzur Aviya*, beginning of chapter 10.

See also *Rambam*, *Hilchos Yesodei HaTorah*, chapter 3. Now, aside from the great difficulty in saying that the **first** creation consisted of **two** things, it is difficult to understand how to reconcile this with what is explained in *Etz Chaim*, *Shaar Drushei Aviya*, chapter 1, that the *mashal* (analogy) for *chomer hayuli* is the *Sefirah* of *Keter*, and it explains *chomer hayuli* only regarding the earth.

However, it is known that some say the *chomer hayuli* is **one** for both the heavens and the earth. It is possible that this depends on the two opinions in *Bereishis Rabbah*, chapter 12:11 (see there in *Yefeh Toar* and *Moreh Nevuchim*, part 2, chapter 26), and I will not elaborate further.

3) There: That time is created (*mechudash*); and you bring the well-known difficulty from our Sages that there was an order of times prior to this. Now, all the resolutions offered for this do not explain the change in the terminology "order of times."

This is well resolved and explained in the *Sefer HaMitzvos* of the Tzemach Tzedek, *Mitzvas Emunas Elokus*, chapter 11. According to what is explained there, it is understood that even time that is not measurable is created and brought into being. *D’lecha* (a word to the wise).

4) At the end of part 2: The discussion regarding whether a *Ben Noach* is obligated in *Kiddush HaShem*, and your proof from the trial of Avraham Avinu in *Ur Kasdim*. This is discussed at length by the *Maharitm* at the end of *Parshas Toldos* and in *Parshas Derachim*, *Derech HaAcharim*, section 2.

5) There: The discussion regarding whether a *Ben Noach* is commanded regarding the honor of parents. See *Menachos* (56b): "Ten commandments... seven that the *Bnei Noach* accepted, and they added to them laws, Shabbos, and the honor of parents..." [The Gemara asks:] "They added to the laws?"—this implies that the *Bnei Noach* were not commanded regarding this.

Regarding the *Midrash Rabbah* cited there: "To you I exempt you from the honor of parents, but I do not exempt others from the honor of parents"—seemingly, the conclusion "and I do not exempt others" is otherwise incomprehensible, for why would one think to say that He would exempt **others** when He begins with "To you I exempt you"? Is it conceivable that the mitzvah of honoring parents would be nullified entirely? It seems to me the interpretation is: "To you I exempt you" entirely, and consequently, you do not need to return to Charan after fulfilling the command to you, etc.

"But I do not exempt others"—even if it is similar to your case, where one leaves his father based on a command, nevertheless, if he delays after fulfilling the command, he will be punished for it, because he was not exempted from the honor of parents; rather, for the moment, the honor was deferred due to another matter. This hints at the matter of Yaakov, as discussed at the end of the first chapter of *Megillah*. Based on the above, the fact that Avraham Avinu left Charan the second time, even though he had already fulfilled the command of "Lech Lecha" upon his first departure, is also resolved. Nevertheless, there is no proof from this statement of our Sages regarding the obligations of a *Ben Noach* in general, as it speaks of the Patriarchs. A mitzvah that depends on logic—whether a *Ben Noach* is warned regarding it—see *Sdei Chemed*, *Pe'as HaSadeh*, *Klalim* 3, end of subsection 27.

Signing with wishes for all the best,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

By the way, Ginzburg, whom you mention in your introduction, was an apostate and for some time even a missionary. I only mention this to note it.

## 542

B"H, 9 Mar-Cheshvan, 5710

To the esteemed Rabbi, the Chassid, the G-d-fearing man, etc.,
Rabbi A. Chen, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I received your letter with a slight delay.

I presented the contents of your letter to my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe shlita, regarding B.A., my cousin, may she live. In the coming days, he will exert effort in this matter and will also be in contact with Rabbi Burak.

Regarding the writing to Mr. ..., I do not know what to discuss here.

The perspective of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe shlita, on the question of religious education in general—that only those who are observant of Torah and mitzvos are trustworthy in this matter, and only they have the authority to express an opinion on it, etc.—is well known, both from his oral statements and from his written words for some time now. What would another letter add?

His perspective regarding any specific institution or office depends on knowing the nature of that institution, its method of operation, its goal, and the actual, responsible management, etc. If one is to convey this perspective to others, along with demands, one must be certain, to a certain degree at least, of the nature of the management that will be in place tomorrow. And what could I say about the "Center for Religious Culture," the institution whose management was under your direction at the beginning of the month of Tishrei 5710, and which, for the time being, has managed to allocate a sum from the institution's funds for the shiurim of Rabbi Goldschmidt and the Gurim? But what is the goal of the institution? Is it a branch or under the authority of the Minister of Education, or the Minister of Religions, partisan or non-partisan? Who has the final say in its affairs, its operations, etc.? Regarding all this and the like, I know nothing. If so, what can I advise? Furthermore, the time and place of the matter in question is in Eretz Yisrael, and in the year 5710—that is, in a time and place of confusion of values, especially the spiritual values of the religious masses, and the greatest confusion in our time.

Therefore, my suggestion, if the question of the letter is still relevant, is that you should turn directly to my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe shlita, describe to him everything you find necessary regarding the nature of the aforementioned institution, and clarify the point of the letter you are requesting to be written. I am certain that he, too, in my place, would do the same, and therefore he will not mind this.

I conclude with regards to my aunt, may she live (I wrote to her before Rosh Hashanah, and my letter surely arrived), to B.A., may she live, and to the entire family, may they live, with wishes for all the best.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Per your request, I am enclosing the draft and your letter.

## 143

B"H, 14th of Mar-Cheshvan, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., Moreinu Harav M. [Dubinsky] shlita,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your comments regarding the Tanya in your letter of the 4th of Cheshvan:

a) In the *Hagahos* to Tanya (as well as in other *seforim*), there appear matters that do not necessarily pertain to understanding what is explained in the main text; they are merely proofs for what is written in the text, a resolution to a difficulty regarding what is written in the text, or the like. I do not know where you derived that they are exclusively for Kabbalistic matters. See the *Hagahos* in chapters 1, 24, 35, 36, 37, 39, 42, and 52.

b) In the *Hagahos* to *Perek Mem-Beis* (Chapter 42), a new and secondary matter is explained that is not discussed at all in the main text. The main text establishes the general rule that for everyone, it is a "small matter" to know Hashem, and one is obligated to strive in this; the explanation there is only how this leads to *yirah*. In the *Hagahos*, it explains that even if there is a situation where one does not know the King at all, nevertheless, awe and fear will fall upon him.

c) You raise a difficulty that in *Perek Mem-Beis*, the parable does not resemble the subject matter, for in the parable, the garments reveal the matter of the King, whereas in the subject matter, they conceal and contradict the matter of *Malchus*.

I am surprised by this, for the Alter Rebbe himself notes this and writes, "Rather, the main thing is to be established, etc., that everything he sees, etc., are the external garments of the King, etc."

Signing with regards to you and your family, and wishing you all the best,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 144

17 Mar-Cheshvan, 5710

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter arrived in due time. My response has been delayed due to the multitude of my preoccupations. Regarding your questions:

a) Eating the apple on the first night of Rosh Hashanah: This is done at the beginning of the meal, after eating the *Hamotzi*. Eating the new fruit on the second night is done before washing hands for the meal. See *Kuntres Shulchan Shabbos*, p. 30. The distinction is simple: Regarding the apple (and even if it were not dipped in honey), since there is no practical difference, it is better to eat it after washing hands, in order to keep the *Kiddush* as close to the meal as possible. The new fruit is eaten before washing hands in order to keep it close to the *Shehecheyanu* blessing recited upon it.

b) Reciting the *Yehi Ratzon* over the apple: This is done immediately after the *Borei Pri HaEtz* blessing, before **beginning** to eat. This follows the precise wording in the *Siddur Admur HaZaken*: "And afterwards he shall say...", which differs from what is written in the *Shulchan Aruch HaRav*.

c) In *Shulchan Aruch HaRav*, *Orach Chaim* 608, it writes "milk and butter foods" and cites the *Magen Avraham*. However, the *Magen Avraham* states "milk and *chama* (hot/cream) foods" (based on Rambam, *Hilchos Avodas Yom HaKippurim* 1:6). You suggest in your letter that perhaps in *Shulchan Aruch HaRav* it should also be "*chama*" instead of "*chem'ah*" (butter). Furthermore, who mentions "butter" in the *Shas* or *Poskim* in this context? This is a beautiful and acceptable observation. Another proof for this correction is: why specify butter when it is already included in "milk foods"? Just as it does not specify cheese (which is explicitly mentioned in *Yoma* 18a) for this very reason. Nevertheless, in all the editions of *Shulchan Aruch HaRav* that I have seen (Kapust 5586, Zhitomir 5627, Stettin 5622, Warsaw 5634, Jozefow 5635, Vilna 5665), it says "*chem'ah*" (butter). This is also how it is transcribed in *Nesiv HaChaim*. Perhaps one could say that *Admur HaZaken*’s intent was to forbid butter because, although it is included in milk foods, he specifies it because it is not considered a food in its own right (if the intent is butter), or because it is a liquid (if the intent is cream). But the opinion of *Admur HaZaken* remains difficult, as he said "milk" generally, not specifically "hot." It is worth noting the words of our Sages (*Nazir* 23b), "A transgression for the sake of Heaven... she had seven relations..."—that Yael did this to weaken his strength, as Rashi explains. It could be said that this is why "He asked for **milk**... she brought forth **butter**" (*Shoftim* 5:25). The commentaries on *Nach* explain that this is because milk makes one sleepy (though the reason for her bringing butter remains difficult, as per *Yoma* 18a). This is according to the opinion that Sisera did not touch Yael. See *Radak* on *Shoftim* (4:18 and 5:27), as the Midrashim are divided on this.

d) Regarding what *Admur HaZaken* writes in *Shulchan Aruch Orach Chaim* 639:17, that one must repeat the blessing in the *Sukkah* if one did not return immediately, but rather after **"an hour or two."** Yet in 640:27, regarding *Tefillin*, he writes "after a long time, such as two or three hours." You ask for the reason for the difference between *Sukkah* and *Tefillin*. Also, if one hour (or two) is sufficient, why add "and two (or three)" later?

The *Ketzos HaShulchan* by Rabbi Chaim Avraham Naeh addresses this in the laws of *Tefillin* in *Badei HaShulchan* (63:63). He cites Rashi in *Yoma* (30a), s.v. *Notel*, which states that once one has delayed for an hour or two, one has diverted one's attention from the meal. The *Siddur Yaavetz* writes regarding *Tefillin* that one returns to recite the blessing after two or three hours. The *Ketzos HaShulchan* concludes that there is no logical reason to distinguish between *Tefillin* and *Sukkah*; even if two hours is considered a "long time," then three hours is certainly so, and the matter remains a *tzarich iyun*.

What appears to me in this matter—prefaced by magnifying the difficulty, as we find four different categories: 1) *Sukkah*: one or two hours. 2) *Tefillin*: two or three hours. 3) *Tzitzis*: after **several** hours (*Shulchan Aruch* of our Rebbe, 8:23, and in his *Siddur*; in *Badei HaShulchan* 7:17, he notes that two or three hours is called a "long time" in 640:27, but the change in *Admur HaZaken*'s language clearly implies that the measurements are not the same). 4) Fragrance: according to the *Taz*—once a day; according to the *Magen Avraham*—immediately (our Rebbe's conclusion is not stated, as the chapter on *Rei'ach* is missing from his *Shulchan Aruch*). What is the reason for these differences?

It seems to me that the reason is that we find the measurement of the interruption is based on the **duration of the matter itself.** Just as with *Tefillah* and *Krias Shema*, where the duration must be the time it takes to recite the entire passage (see *Shulchan Aruch Admur HaZaken* 65:1). This is the measurement for **all the above.** Now, the essence of the **Sukkah** is eating (see *Taz* 639:20). The time for **Tefillin** for most people (which is the subject of the discussion, as they are not wearing them all day and are currently removing them, not due to duress) is the time of *Tefillah* (see *Shulchan Aruch* of our Rebbe, end of 27 and 65:20, and 37:end).

**Tzitzis** (referring to *Tallis Gadol*, which is our subject): its time is not fixed, but it lasts much longer than *Tefillin*, as one puts it on before *Tefillin*, it does not require a clean body, and it does not have the same caution regarding *hesech hada'as* (distraction) (see *Shulchan Aruch* 37).

**Fragrance**: It has no substance, and therefore one could say that since a person has separated from it and gone outside, the entire matter has ceased, and one recites a new blessing upon re-entering. Or, conversely, since fragrance has no fixed "obligatory" time, time does not cause an interruption at all.

Now, the duration of a meal **in the usual manner** can be said to be one hour. Support for this is found in the words of our Sages (*Berachos* 55a) that the meal (which is frequent, twice a day) is analogous to the offering of a sacrifice. It is stated (*Pesachim* 55a) that the offering of the *Tamid* (which is frequent, twice a day) lasts for one hour. One can also say that what our Sages said (*Shabbos* 10a), "The first hour is for eating," means: during the first hour and throughout the **entire duration** of the first hour. Note the reference in *Bava Metzia* (83b) to "four hours," which Rashi explains is the time for the meal. This measurement is the duration of a normal meal, but for one who wishes to lengthen his life, our Sages said he should prolong his time at the table (*Berachos* ibid.). Since it was extended from one hour, it was fixed at two, **for those who prolong their meal.** It is possible that this is the basis for Rashi's words (*Yoma* ibid., also copied in Rashi on the *Rif*, end of chapter 4 of *Berachos*). The time for *Tefillah* (including putting on and removing *Tefillin*, saying *Mah Tovu*, etc.) is **more** than an hour and a half in the usual manner (see *Iggeres HaKodesh* 1).
