## 730

B.H., 21 Sivan, 5710

To the venerable Rabbi and Chossid, distinguished for his Torah and fear of Heaven, etc.,
Rabbi Shneur Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and Blessings!

I gratefully acknowledge receipt of your letter dated 9 Sivan, in which you report in detail on the situation there.

It gave me great pleasure to hear your news regarding the public shiurim in general, and the study of Chassidus in particular, and especially the holy writings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, the Nasi. You will surely strengthen and exert yourselves in this even more, and may Hashem be your help. You will surely let me know which masechta you are studying, and you should also hold a siyum with the appropriate publicity in order to strengthen the matter and increase the number of students, etc.

It is surprising that you do not mention the study of Tanya.

You are surely making an effort to include in these studies those who, for the time being, are not yet among anash; it is well known how much my father-in-law, the Rebbe, was "boiling" with this.

Regarding talking during davening, it would be appropriate to post on the wall of the beis medrash the Iggeres HaKodesh, section 26, as well as a copy of the "Moda'ah Rabbah" of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, regarding listening to the reading of the Torah. This is surely available among anash in Tel Aviv or Jerusalem (it was also printed in the first collection of the letters of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, published by Rabbi A. Paris).

Regarding the study of Mishnayos by heart—you are surely aware of the letters of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, on this subject, which were printed in his second collection of letters.

— As to why specifically Mishnayos by heart, etc., I have explained my thoughts on this in the fourth *Kovetz Lubavitch*. —

Regarding the mehudar mikvaos in the village, I would be very grateful if you would write to me how they were constructed—whether they are connected to a spring or rainwater, whether they use *hashakah* or another method—and how they are maintained when they need to be cleaned, etc. Please let me know if you have any tradition regarding this, in addition to what the venerable Rabbi Y. Landa, may he live, has already written to me.

Regarding his seventieth birthday, for long life and good years—I wish him that he should merit the fulfillment of the verse, "They shall still bring forth fruit in old age; they shall be full of sap and flourishing," and consequently, he will be occupied with "declaring that Hashem is upright," etc., as explained in *Likutei Torah*, Parshas Re'eh, in the discourse beginning "Ki Tishma," etc., "The Upright."

Since I have mentioned this, our Sages of blessed memory state (Avos, end of chapter 5—and the well-known difficulty is noted regarding what is written in the writings of the Arizal, cited in *Maamarei Admur HaEmtza'i*, end of section 7, that fifty is for "old age" [ziknah] and sixty is for "gray hair" [seivah]) that sixty is for "old age" and seventy is for "gray hair." There is a practical difference as to whether one is considered a "sage" or "distinguished," and whether this obligates one to stand or show honor (see Kiddushin 32b, Tur Shulchan Aruch Yoreh Deah 244, and *Shaar HaMitzvos* of the Arizal, Parshas Kedoshim. But this is not the place to elaborate). See also *Likutei Torah* at the end of the explanation of "I am black," that "seivah" (gray hair) is in Atik Anpin, the place of great mercy, and "He will find abundant kindness."

With regards to the entire group, may they live.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 731

731

B"H, 22 Sivan, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' B. Sh.
Shaliach

Peace and Blessing!

Enclosed herewith are copies of the letters of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory (eight sheets). After they have been transcribed in due course, they should certainly be publicized to the masses, with the omission of names and the like, and the merit of the many depends on you.

Please inform me of the health of Mrs. Miriam Rachel, may she live. Also, please inform me what the final outcome was regarding the affairs of Mr. Neiwirth, may he live, and thank him for his efforts in all of this.

I am sure you will soon bring me good news that you have begun to fulfill the essence of your shlichus. See Likutei Torah, the maamar beginning with the words "Vayikra," Chapter 3. The power of the Sender—my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—will accompany you in all your endeavors, that they may be met with great success in every detail.

With blessings and regards to the entire group, may they live.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

All the kuntresim—they have surely reached you.

***

From the secretariat copy.

**R' B. Sh.:** Shemtov. Additional letters to him—see above, 730, and the references in the notes there.

## 332

B"H, 22 Sivan, 5710

To the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing, etc. ... may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh:

Mazal Tov, Mazal Tov on the engagement that took place with the young woman, may she live. May their building be an everlasting edifice founded upon the Torah and its mitzvos, in accordance with the will of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Generally, the times for weddings in these months are in Elul or in Kislev. In this specific case, however, it depends more on the preparations on the part of the bride, may she live.

Regarding the wearing of Rabbeinu Tam tefillin: I know from bochurim who asked my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, whether they should wear Rabbeinu Tam tefillin, and he replied: "A good thing." If so, why should one delay putting them on for any amount of time, since he has already decided to wear them? See Shaar HaKollel, Chapter 11, Section 30, in the name of Sefer Etzei Eden; Ateres Zekenim, Section 34, in the name of Zohar Chadash; and Os Chaim by the Gaon of Munkatch, Section 34, Subsection 3. Furthermore, the author of Piskei HaSiddur, Rabbi Naeh, has already written that he has not heard a reason why the majority of bochurim only begin to wear Rabbeinu Tam tefillin after marriage.

With blessings and wishes for a Mazal Tov,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***332***

From the Secretariat copy.

## 733

B”H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Tammuz, 5710
Brooklyn

To the distinguished Rabbi and Chossid, G-d-fearing and learned, etc., Moreinu HaRav Y.

Peace and blessing!

Regarding the branches of the avodah of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, the Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch and Machne Israel held a special place for him. He felt a unique responsibility toward them, personally and privately taking upon himself the effort to secure the necessary means for the expenses involved in their work—both in its public aspects and in the parts that he managed, and which continue to be managed, in a confidential manner.

It is clear and simple that the intent, desire, and will of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, is that all these areas of work should continue as before—meaning not only in their current quantity and quality, but like any living work that grows, develops, and expands.

But now, this financial responsibility for these areas of work is loaded upon the shoulders of all of us—each and every one of his Chassidim and those connected to him—to be the vessels and channels through which my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, will continue the flow of his influence, and with even greater strength and intensity. For a tzaddik who has departed is found in all worlds, including this world of action, even more than during his lifetime.

I therefore suggest that you speak with anash, may they live, in your area and organize a local committee, jointly for Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch and Machne Israel, which should, as soon as possible, begin practical work suited to the local conditions to provide **on a regular basis** a specific portion of the expenses for these areas of work.

Please provide the names of the committee members so that they may be mentioned at the tziyun of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for blessing and great success in their work on the committee in particular, and in their affairs in general. If possible, please send them in a way that they can be mentioned at the tziyun on the Chag HaGeulah, the 12th of Tammuz, which is the birthday of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

- (See Yerushalmi, Rosh Hashanah 3:8; Shabbos 156a, Rashi and Tosafos there. And note Likutei Torah, beginning of Parshas Haazinu; Mayim Rabim 5636, p. 104; Maamar "V'Zos HaTerumah" 5640. And this is not the place to elaborate.) -

For then there **can** be, and consequently there **must** be, a connection and a walking in his paths and in the activities that he himself performed, as mentioned above, with greater strength and intensity.

Hoping to hear good news from you and your colleagues, may they live, regarding what is being done in the above matter at the earliest opportunity.

Signing with respect and with the blessing of all good,

**M. Schneerson**

***

733

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 18, p. 472, and completed based on a photocopy of the letter.
The letter was sent to several individuals.
**This financial responsibility:** See above, letter 729, and the references in the notes there.

## 374

B”H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Tammuz, 5710
Brooklyn

To the members of the Presidium of the National Convention of Agudas Israel of America

Shalom and Bracha!

In response to your letter of the 20th of Sivan, 5710:

Every question in the life of the Jewish people, whether minor or major, demands serious attention from any convention called to discuss matters of Torah and Yiddishkeit. However, there are times when questions arise that involve *pikuach nefashos*—whether in a physical or spiritual sense, affecting individuals or the collective—and these naturally demand special attention and the concentration of all efforts with extra alacrity for the work of rescue.

Such a question is currently the issue of the education of the children immigrating to our Holy Land, and their rescue from heresy and spiritual destruction, *chas v’shalom*.

My revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, a leader of Klal Yisrael above and beyond any party lines, whose entire life’s work was dedicated with *mesiras nefesh* for decades to transcending partisan divides—he would, in every instance of danger to the Jewish people, strive to utilize every circle through which it was possible to improve the situation. In his time, he began the effort to concentrate all forces for the rescue of these children.

And although the fruits of this work are already visible, the situation of the children is still quite dangerous, and the question of their education and guidance remains in all its severity.

Therefore, every gathering of Jews who care for the welfare of Klal Yisrael must be a platform from which a voice is heard on high—a voice of strong demand for the resolution of this distressing issue as soon as possible, and for the adoption of resolutions to seize every possible means to remove all obstacles and stumbling blocks from any side that prevent and deny these children their right to be educated on the knees of Torah and Yiddishkeit.

However, even in this country, the question of rescuing the youth demands great and expansive work. One of its branches is the battle against the indifference of the public, which has already become accustomed to a situation where only a small percentage of Jewish boys and girls receive a kosher education, and at most, people sigh over this terrible state of affairs and content themselves with that sigh. Even though the work of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, in this field was also crowned with great success, the road to reaching the desired goal is still long, and great strength and efforts are required to place education in the United States on a proper foundation.

I wish to hope that the National Convention will place these two questions—the question of the education of the children immigrating to our Holy Land, and the question of the education of the youth in the United States who are, for the time being, still far from traditional Yiddishkeit—in their proper place on its agenda, and that we will hear its voice and feel its influence through actual deeds, for the deed is the essential thing.

With respect and blessing to all the participants, that they may succeed in this gathering to act in strengthening the foundations of the religion and in spreading Torah with *yiras shamayim*.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 374

B”H, Rosh Chodesh Tammuz, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R’ M. [Lipsker] shlita,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of the 17th of Sivan:

Do not feel concerned about writing at length, for I am interested in all the details regarding the general situation and your activities in particular. I am certain that you will continue to keep me informed of everything in detail on a regular basis.

Our friend R’ B.G. shlita informed me that he has sent you additional funds. Since you will surely meet face-to-face, you will be able to discuss matters regarding the future as well. It is a pity that obtaining the visa for R’ B.G. shlita is taking so long...

While visiting the Tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, yesterday, I mentioned you for success in your avodah in North Africa. It is a certainty that the shepherds of Yisrael do not separate from their flock, and he continues to serve from there as well, to draw down blessing and success in all the handiwork of those who are connected to him—especially that they should fulfill their shlichus to the fullest extent possible, with success in both material and spiritual matters.

With blessings for great success,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 375

B"H,

Many people seek to describe and explain the virtues and greatness of the Nesi'im of Chabad in general, and of the Nasi of our generation, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in particular, regarding various matters: that he was a man of mesiras nefesh, a gaon, a man of refined middos, a tzaddik, a man of ruach hakodesh, one who experienced miracles, and so on and so forth.

These praises are all the more significant when defined according to the teachings of Chassidus—what constitutes mesiras nefesh, what constitutes a gaon, and so on.

Yet, with all of this, the essential point is missing. Furthermore, this point is not only essential in its own right, but it is especially important because it is the most relevant, particularly to us—the community of his Chassidim and those bound to him. And that is: the fact that he is the Nasi, and the Nasi of Chabad.

For a Nasi in general is called the "head of the thousands of Yisrael" ; he is the level of the head and the brain in relation to them, and from him they draw their nourishment and vitality. Through attachment to him, they are connected and unified with their source, high above.

There are several types of Nesi'im : those whose influence is in a manner of pnimiyus (internalization), and those whose influence is in a manner of makif (encompassing). Within this itself, there are distinctions: whether they influenced through the revealed Torah or the hidden Torah, or both together; whether they taught the paths of avodah and Chassidus; whether they drew down physical blessings, and so on.

There are those who possessed several of these aspects, or even all of them.

This has been the nature of the leadership of the Chabad Nesi'im from the Alter Rebbe until my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, inclusive: they encompassed all types and distinctions. They influenced through pnimiyus and makif, through Torah, avodah, and gemilus chasadim, in both spiritual and physical matters. Consequently, their connection with those who belong to them encompassed all 613 limbs of the soul and body of those who are connected.

Each and every one of us must know—that is, deepen our understanding and fix our thoughts upon this—that he is indeed the Nasi and the head. From him and through him come all influences in both physical and spiritual matters, and through the hiskashrus to him (and he has already instructed in his letters how and through what one becomes connected), we are connected and unified with the root, and the root of the root, up to the highest heights, etc.

**Menachem Schneerson**

3 Tammuz, 5710
Brooklyn, N.Y.

## 336

On this occasion, I would like to clarify our custom in response to the questions of many:

When Rosh Chodesh Menachem-Av falls on Shabbos—as it does this year—should the Haftorah be *Hashamayim Kisi* or *Shim'u Devar Hashem*?

I am transcribing here a passage from the *reshimos* of the year 5690 (1930), when Rosh Chodesh Menachem-Av also fell on Shabbos. It reads as follows:

Motzaei Shabbos Kodesh, 9 Menachem-Av, 5690, Berlin.

I was with my father-in-law, the Rebbe, who was lying on the sofa. He spoke about the custom regarding which Haftorah to recite on Shabbos Rosh Chodesh Menachem-Av: My father [the Rebbe Rashab] once said *Hashamayim Kisi*. A few years later, when Rosh Chodesh Menachem-Av fell on Shabbos again, he said *Shim'u Devar Hashem*, and he remarked then that he regretted having said *Hashamayim Kisi* some years prior.

See the discussion regarding this in Shulchan Aruch Orach Chayim, section 425, and the commentaries there; Shaarei Ephraim, gate 10, section 22.

**M. S.**

***

336

Printed in Sefer HaMaamarim 5710, p. 266 [date of the introduction to the Kuntres: 3 Tammuz]. Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 337.

**Berlin... I was with:** See the introduction to Igros Kodesh of the Rebbe Rayatz, Vol. 2, p. 24.

## 377

B"H, 3 Tammuz, 5717

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. (may he live),

Greetings and blessings!

Enclosed is the Kuntres for the upcoming Chag HaGeulah, 12-13 Tammuz. You will surely merit to share it with everyone in your surroundings, without distinction of type, in accordance with the words of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in his letter (printed in Kuntres 59, p. 27) regarding this Geulah, that "The Holy One, blessed be He, redeemed not only me on 12 Tammuz, but also all those who love our holy Torah, those who observe mitzvos, and even those who are merely called by the name 'Jew'."

This includes all types of Jewish men and women: those who possess Torah and mitzvos, those who possess either Torah or mitzvos, and even those who possess neither Torah nor mitzvos, but who are **called** by the name "Jew."

As is known, a "nickname" (kinui) is something—a name—that obscures and covers the essence of the person being called by this name. It is possible that it is not his essential name, nor an abbreviation of his name, nor an interpretation of his name, nor even in the language in which he is named; it might not even be a nickname derived from his own self, but rather a name he is called by only because of his family (see Vayikra Rabbah 30:12; Tikkunei Zohar, p. 15a; Meorei Ohr, entry *Kaf*; Shulchan Aruch, Even HaEzer, 129:16 and commentaries; Kav V'Naki, Rule 38; Sdei Chemed, Asifat Dinim, Ma'areches Get, 7:6; Maamar 19 Kislev 5717, Kuntres 73, Chapter 2).

And you should not only merit to share this Kuntres with the many, but, following the requirement of our Sages in the Mechilta on the verse "Set them before them," you should also explain to each and every person that the main point is to bring the study and the lesson into action and deed, for each person according to their own capacity: in the *mochin*, in the *midos*, etc.

For in this, too, we receive vitality and strength from the head of the generation, the Nasi of the generation, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. As written in the introduction to the Kuntres, we receive nourishment and vitality from the Nasi for all our affairs, from all the qualities and virtues attributed to him: *Soneh Pshuto* (Keter), *Gaon* (mochin), *Baal Midos* (Chagas Nehi, the essence of the *midos*), *Tzaddik* (Yesod), *Ruach Hakodesh* (Malchus), one who is learned in miracles, and much, much more (from his *Chidushei Torah*).

**The ability for all this has been given to us, the desire for all this we possess, and when there is the will—there is nothing that can stand before the will.**

With blessings for a Chag HaGeulah and best wishes,

**M. Schneerson**

* * *

Your *pidyon nefesh* has been received. It will be read, G-d willing, at the Ohel on the upcoming Chag HaGeulah.

It would be good if you would report on the progress of the students in Torah and *avodah* at regular intervals.

I am sure you will report on the improvement in the health of your wife (may she live).

## 378

B"H, 3 Tammuz, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc. ... may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter:

Regarding your travel, I am sure your brother, may he live, has already conveyed to you that since you have already done so in accordance with the opinion of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—and I believe he instructed you at that time to endeavor in the places you visit to work on matters of strengthening Yiddishkeit, proper education, and the like—then if you are traveling this time with the same intent, the agreement of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, from back then applies to this as well.

You and your family, may they live, have been mentioned at the resting place of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and the Holy One, blessed be He, fulfills the blessings of the Tzaddikim. May everything be in order, in its proper time and season, and surely you will keep us informed from time to time of your good welfare.

Regarding what you wrote, that to your regret you have not established a set time for learning: Why the regret? It would be far better to simply establish a set time for learning. Then you would gain twofold: the benefit of the established learning, and the absence of the regret. For a person is not permitted to cause himself distress, and perhaps, in any case, each of you can learn individually.

With blessings and regards to ... may he live,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 379

B"H, 3 Tammuz, 5709

To the avreich, the G-d-fearing, etc., [Name]... may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to his letter:

May it be Hashem’s will that he be a fitting vessel for the fulfillment of all the blessings that the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, bestowed upon him, in their entirety, both materially and spiritually.

And now, especially after he has completed nineteen years and is beginning his twentieth year, this is the time of preparation for attaining those levels of a "twenty-year-old" mentioned in Chassidus (Likutei Torah, Bamidbar, discourse beginning *Miben Esrim*).

With blessings for a complete and immediate recovery,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***379***

From the secretariat’s copy.

## 440

B"H, 7 Tammuz, 5710

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished [name], may he be blessed, [name] HaKohen,

Greetings and blessings!

I am enclosing here the *kuntres* that has just been published for the upcoming *Chag HaGeulah*, 12 Tammuz. I am certain that beyond your own study of it, you will also share the merit with many others, for "the merit of the many depends upon him."

It is still difficult to write about the *histalkus* of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Yet, one must always keep in mind the increased responsibility this has placed upon each of us—especially upon those who were privileged to see him many times, and who were privileged that he saw them many times and thought of them many times.

For these individuals, this memory must evoke an intensification of their spiritual powers with greater strength and vigor, particularly in the area of being *maavir al midosav* (forgiving and overlooking personal slights), even if one calculates that they are entirely in the right, as the Rebbe writes in the *maamar* for Shabbos Shuvah 5710 (*Kuntres 70*).

One must always remember that one is a "Rebbe’s person," and ask oneself: Is the Rebbe satisfied with me acting in this way? Do I have enough *iskafya* and *ishapcha* as befits a person of the Rebbe?

...And if *Kohanim* are prone to being *kapdanim* (strict/demanding), they are, after all, descendants of Aharon HaKohen, whose essence was "loving peace and pursuing peace." This is especially true for *Kohanim* who are students of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, who always demanded *ahavas Yisrael* and that one should see in the other only their virtues (see also the enclosed *kuntres* in the *sicha*).

I believe it is unnecessary to elaborate further on this, and I look forward to hearing soon that there is peace with you in all matters.

With blessings of all the best, awaiting your letter bringing good news,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***440***

From the secretariat’s copy.

## 341

B"H, 11 Tammuz, Hashem Yisbarach

Received your letter of 20 Tammuz. G-d willing, I will inform you tomorrow at the Tziyun regarding the establishment of the committee through your efforts, and I will mention your names there.

With blessings for the Chag HaGeulah and best wishes to all the committee members and all those influenced by them.

**M. Schneerson**

As for spending much time now on perfecting your English — it is not worthwhile for you. And according to the saying of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: "As much as the carriage (matters of Torah and Mitzvos) is pulled to the horse, it is time for the horse to go to the carriage."

#### ***341***

Written as an addendum to letter 337 above, dated 11 Tammuz.

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 21, p. 540, and completed based on a photocopy of the letter.

**The committee:** Apparently the committee for the benefit of the Melach (Center for Spreading Judaism) — in continuation of letter 333 above.

**Perfecting your English:** To facilitate his Shlichus work in spreading Yiddishkeit to wider circles.

## 482

B”H, 14 Tammuz, 5712

To the illustrious Rabbi, the G-d-fearing man, a man of Torah and lineage, a man of many accomplishments and a master of good deeds, who sits in judgment and leads his community toward calm waters, our master, Rabbi Refael Baruch, may he live, leader of the holy community of the city of Meknes, whose influence extends throughout the entire country,

Peace and abundant blessing!

I have received your telegram—signed by your honor and by our friend, the illustrious Rabbi, the G-d-fearing man, a man of many accomplishments in his life’s work, etc., Rabbi M. Lipsker, may he live—conveying blessings for the Chag HaGeulah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Regarding the blessing that "whoever blesses is blessed" with the blessing of Hashem, whose addition is greater than the essence—which my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory (the father of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in his will *Chinuch L’Naar*) explained as drawing down from the level of Ohr Ein Sof that is above Seder Hishtalshelus—

This means that beyond the fact that everything existing in the root above is drawn down below, as all obstacles and delays are removed,

There is an additional aspect: a new will is created, and they decree for him an addition of life, blessing, and peace in all his affairs, Amen, may it be His will.

You also informed me of the joyous news that you will do everything in your power to obtain a visa to your country for the illustrious Rabbi, the G-d-fearing man, etc., one of the veteran students and emissary of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, Rabbi Binyamin Gorodetzky, may he live.

It is unnecessary to explain to your honor and those like you how urgent the priority of this matter is.

If, in every matter of mitzvah, the Alter Rebbe elaborates on the great virtue of *zerizus* (promptness), and emphasizes that "the *zerizus* of our father Avraham, peace be upon him, is what stands for us and our children forever, for the Akeidah itself is not considered such a great test in comparison to the stature of our father Avraham, peace be upon him" (*Iggeres HaKodesh*, section 21),

Then all the more so is the virtue and necessity of *zerizus* great in a matter concerning *chinuch*, and specifically the *chinuch* of the children of Yisrael. This is especially true in these times of trouble and distress, in the *ikvesa d’meshicha* generation, where "there is no day..." and the darkness is doubled and redoubled, and the exile grows ever darker—according to the explanation of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that before the dawn, the darkness of the night is at its greatest. Only the Torah is our life, and its study with the greatest possible expansion, both in quantity and quality—especially the study of the *pnimiyus haTorah*, the "light" within the Torah and its *Zohar*—is the elixir of life that will strengthen all those who study, engage, and strive in this, until Hashem, in His kindness and mercy, breaks the yoke of exile, and Yisrael is redeemed through *tzedakah*. This includes the *tzedakah* of reviving the souls of the poor, for "there is no poor person except..." (and, because of the darkness of the night, some even mistakenly say that this is not poverty). May He lead us upright to our land through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, speedily in our days.

To conclude with a *devar Torah*: Regarding the matter of *tzedakah* at night, since the subject has come up, let us say a word about it, as I was asked some time ago regarding what is written in the sefer *Nagid U’Mitzvah* (Shacharis prayer) that "the night is not a suitable time for *tzedakah*." Seemingly, the opposite is true: since the night is a matter of *gevuros*, it would be appropriate to increase *tzedakah* in order to sweeten them... "she gives food to her household."

See also the end of the sefer *Shaarei Tefillah* by Rabbi Yaakov Rokach (Livorno, 5630).

May your peace and your Torah increase, and may you live long years at your post, the holy post, in much goodness.

Wishing you all the best and awaiting good news,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 743

B"H, 19 Tammuz, 5710

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Boruch, may he live.

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of 8 Sivan and 2 Beha’aloscha—my reply was delayed due to the great pressures and matters pertaining to 12-13 Tammuz.

In the circles in which you find yourself, you have very good opportunities to make yourself useful, with the help of Hashem Yisbarach, by fulfilling the shlichus for which Divine Providence has led you there.

One must not feel discouraged when it seems that the number of those who allow themselves to be influenced is small, or that the impact on these individuals is weaker than one would like.

**Regarding quantity,** one must remember that every single individual is an entire world, and it is worthwhile for a whole world to dedicate itself to saving even one single Yid—in gashmius, and all the more so in ruchnius.

Beyond that, the positive influence received by the recipient does not remain with him alone; directly or indirectly, he later influences others for the good within his own circle, whether narrow or broad. Just as every limb is part of the body as a whole, so too is an individual part of the entire tzibbur.

And with the spiritual revitalization of one individual, the well-being of the entire tzibbur is strengthened as a result.

**Regarding quality,** one cannot possibly imagine the greatness of the good one does for a person when helping him rise even a little bit higher.

Aside from the inestimable value of performing even one fewer aveirah and one more mitzvah—for an aveirah leads to another aveirah, and a mitzvah leads to a mitzvah—with every mitzvah, a person becomes more capable of understanding a true word and having the strength to bring that word into action. To help one Yid put on a yarmulke, another tefillin, a third tzitzis, a fourth to be aroused toward ahavas re'im and good character traits, a fifth to observe taharas hamishpachah, a sixth to teach his children Torah, and so on.

You surely also meet Yidden whom the Almighty has helped so that they are able to broadly support financially the work of Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch and Machne Israel, which my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, founded and upon whom he placed the broad task of doing everything possible to bring back all those who have wandered and are weak in general, and the youth in particular, to Yiddishkeit. By giving Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch and Machne Israel the ability, for example, to publish and distribute as much printed material as possible in various languages, one becomes a partner in performing this tremendous rescue work that the donor himself is not in a position to do. It goes without saying that this zechus makes him more worthy of being able to more easily receive the spiritual influence that he himself needs.

It is difficult to enumerate in a letter all the details of your opportunities to make yourself as useful as possible, but the main thing is, as my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, used to say: "One must speak less and do more."

All of this concerns your work with **others.** However, there is also the work with oneself, where the yetzer hara stands in the way most powerfully—but regarding that, another time.

Mr. Harry Katz was written to according to your request.

Awaiting good tidings,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Enclosed is the Kuntres for 12-13 Tammuz.

## 444

B"H, 20 Tammuz, 5710

To the esteemed and G-d-fearing individual, who conducts his business with integrity, etc., [Name]—may he live.

Peace and blessing!

After such a long interval, I was happy to meet with you. This meeting was particularly gratifying to me for several reasons: first, because I met you amidst a gathering of Chassidim; second, because it was on the birthday of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and the day of his liberation—which is a *chag hageulah* for us all. Furthermore, meeting you at the *tziyon* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, where the most sublime *hemshech* resides, was especially meaningful. I also derived great satisfaction from the fact that you guided the *kohanim* Chassidim to the *tziyon*; by enabling others to perform this merit, your own merit is certainly great, and your *hiskashrus* to my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, is the channel through which blessing and success will be bestowed upon you.

I hope that not a day goes by without you setting aside time for the study of *Nigleh* and *Chassidus*. In my opinion, you should undertake, without a vow, to recite *Kapitel* 71 of *Tehillim* every day, at least until the first *yahrzeit* on 10 Shevat 5711. In addition, you should study some topic from the teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—from his *maamarim*, *kuntreisim*, or *sichos*—every *Shabbos* and *Yom Tov*.

Just as water reflects the face, so too will your proper *hiskashrus* to my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, draw down upon you an abundance of blessings to succeed in both material and spiritual matters.

Please convey my regards to your brother—may he live—at an appropriate opportunity.

With wishes for all the best,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Enclosed is the *kuntres* published for the birthday of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. In a separate package, I am sending you *Sefer HaSichos* and *Sefer HaMaamarim*.

***

*From the Secretariat copy.*

**That you guided the kohanim Chassidim:** By creating a partition between the *kohanim* and the graves.

**Recite Kapitel 71 every day:** See *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 2, p. 6. See also the letters below: 449, 450, 452, 457, 460, 467, 470, 488, 496, 516, 534, 538, 547-8.

## 391

B"H, 21 Tammuz, 5711

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R' Tzvi, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter was received in due time, and the response was delayed due to the matters of 12-13 Tammuz. While at the Ohel on that day, I mentioned you for all that you require.

Regarding your concern about finding a suitable teacher for your Talmud Torah: We have seen tangibly, from the time of the histalkus until now, that the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, bestows success upon those who stand at their posts, fulfilling the shlichus he placed upon them. It only requires hishtadlus on their part, and then the success is extraordinary. Without a doubt, in this matter as well, it will be so. Please inform me of good news regarding this.

Regarding whether to change your place of residence and whether to purchase a home: Since you intend to be here in a few weeks, in my opinion, you should not do the aforementioned for the time being. When you are here, we will speak about it.

May Hashem bestow success and fulfill all the blessings that the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, has blessed you and your entire family, may they live, in their entirety.

With best wishes for all good and regards to all those who seek your welfare,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Your letter of 20 Tammuz has just been received. If it is indeed the case that it is impossible to remain in the apartment in the shul building, and the price of the house being offered to you will be no more than ten thousand, you should purchase it. May it be with good fortune and success, and you will surely inform me of the day of the purchase, which should be before the month of Menachem Av.

## 346

B"H, 21 Tammuz, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., R' Y. [may he live],

Greetings and blessings,

I have received your letter, and I thank you for the information contained therein.

May it be the will of Hashem that your stay, together with your family [may they live], in the lush pastures brings the desired benefit, quite literally, in the healing and strengthening of the body. As is the saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in the name of his father, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: "How precious it [the body] is, that for its sake so much Torah, etc., is poured out."

See also Rambam, Hilchos Deos, Chapter 4: That the body be healthy and whole is among the ways of Hashem, etc.

From this we can learn, by way of a *kal v’chomer*—and even a *kal v’chomer* of a *kal v’chomer*—how much more critical it is that the *neshamah* be healthy. And if every year one must strive for this, how much more so must one strengthen this with added courage during times when one is occupied with and focused specifically on the healing of the body, so that one does not, *chas v’shalom*, arrive at the state where "the strength of the body is the weakness of the *neshamah*" (Zohar I, 180b). See also Shabbos (147b), regarding the water of Dimus, etc.

I am not writing this as a sermon, but rather to awaken you to the matter—a form of *is’hapcha*—to utilize the days and the process of physical healing to strengthen the *neshamah*. That is to say, to add a special *shiur* of study for the days of vacation, and to seek opportunities to inspire others who have come to rest in your area and its surroundings, toward Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilas chasadim*, each according to his situation. Sometimes it is also worthwhile to explain to them that we do not know the secrets of Divine Providence, and perhaps the purpose of all of you coming to such-and-such a place is to add precious stones to the crown of the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He.

My father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, has already promised that it is a certainty that *ta'amulah* (outreach) does not return empty-handed.

And He is faithful and reliable.

With wishes for a healthy summer in body and soul, and regards to your family,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***346***

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 8, p. 369, and completed according to a photocopy of the letter.

The letter was sent to several people, between the dates of 21 Tammuz – 4 Menachem Av.

**As is the saying:** See also above, Letter 345. Below, Letter 397.

## 287

B"H, 21 Tammuz

To the esteemed and distinguished activist, engaged in communal needs with faith, etc., etc.,
Rabbi Y. [Gripel], may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

I hereby wish to express my heartfelt mazal tov regarding the resolutions of Young Israel concerning the rescue of children in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established. There is no doubt that a large part of this is due to your efforts, may you live and be well, and your reward will be doubled from Heaven. Furthermore, the reward of a mitzvah is the mitzvah itself.

However, the essential part is still missing: the actual deed, not merely the discourse in words alone.

Enclosed are copies of my letters regarding this matter. Surely, as you are on the scene, you will also exert your influence to expedite the aforementioned as much as possible.

Signing with wishes for all the best and success in your work,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

**287**

From the secretariat copy.

**Rabbi Y.:** Gripel.

**My letters regarding this:** See above, letter 274.

## 248

B"H, 21 Tammuz, 5710

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished... shaliach,

Shalom u’Bracha!

Your letter of 13 Tammuz was received, but the Kuntres for Chag HaGeulah had already been sent to your address in... and surely it has finally reached you by now.

The Pidyon Nefesh from them was received earlier and was read at the Tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, on 12 Tammuz, and subsequently in his chamber, where people would enter for yechidus and to submit a Pidyon Nefesh. He surely bestowed his blessings, and may the Ribono shel Olam fulfill them completely. I trust you will keep me informed from time to time regarding the good health of your wife, may she live and be well, and that she is following the instructions of the doctors regarding rest and the like.

Regarding your question about going out wearing a sheitel but without a head covering: without entering into the essence of the matter, why make such a change, or anything similar, in the middle of a pregnancy? The main thing now is that the pregnancy should proceed properly and without pain, and that the birth should take place at its proper time, easily, with a healthy and viable child. Afterwards, you may ask about the aforementioned matter.

With wishes for all the best and regards to the entire group, may they live and be well,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***248***

From the secretariat copy.

## 349

B"H, 25 Tammuz, 5711

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. (may he live),

Peace and blessing!

Your letter, along with the *pidyon nefesh*, was received in due time. As you requested, I read it at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), and in his chamber, where one enters for *yechidus* and to submit a *pidyon nefesh*.

A *tzaddik* bestows blessings, and Hashem fulfills the blessings of the *tzaddik*—who is found in this world even more than during his lifetime—regarding their fulfillment.

It is my hope that there is no need for comment or illumination regarding the matter of *hiskashrus* to the *Nasi*, my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), for *hiskashrus* is achieved through studying his Torah and walking in the paths he instructed.

Surely you have set times for studying the Torah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), as found in his *maamarim*, *sichos*, and letters. And how good it is if this study is done in a group, where each person strengthens and awakens the other.

It is understood that this does not exempt one from the other *shiurim* that every individual is obligated to maintain. Among these are the *shiurim* that are equal for every soul as a daily requirement: *Chumash* with *Rashi*, *Tehillim* as divided for the days of the month, and *Tanya* as divided for the days of the year by my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory).

With best wishes,

**M. Schneerson**

(The response to your telegram—it surely arrived.)

* * *

G-d willing, I will mention your names—in the chamber and at the *tziyun*—as well as your aunt (may she live) for all that is needed, as you requested in your letter.

- And surely you are saying, G-d willing, every day the Rebbe’s *kapitel* of *Tehillim* (it is now *kapitel* 71), at least until 10 Shevat 5711. See the *sicha* in the *kuntres* of 12 Tammuz 5711, section 11.

With best wishes and a *mazal tov* blessing on bringing your son (may he live) into the covenant of our father Avraham, and may you raise him, along with your other children (may they live), to Torah, the *chuppah*, and good deeds.

## 550

B"H, 25 Tammuz, 5710

To the avreich, the G-d-fearing, the esteemed [Name]... may he live,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter:

May it be the will [of Hashem] that you be a fitting vessel for the fulfillment of all the blessings that my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, bestowed upon you, in both material and spiritual matters.

You have surely seen the excerpt in *Hayom Yom* for 11 Nissan regarding conduct on one's birthday.

And upon reaching the age of twenty, which is the concept of *mochin d'Abba* (see *Likutei Torah*, *Bamidbar*, the *maamar* beginning "Miben esrim"), it is appropriate that you begin, *bli neder*, to don *tefillin d'Rabbenu Tam*.

And you should certainly recite every day, *bli neder*, the Rebbe’s *kapitel* of *Tehillim*, which is now *kapitel* 71.

Signing with blessing,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***550***

From the secretariat’s copy.

**Tefillin d'Rabbenu Tam:** See also above, letter 532.

**Kapitel 71:** See also above, letter 544, and the references in the notes there.

## 551

B"H, 26 Tammuz, 5711

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished... may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter:

Thank you for sending the excerpt from your article regarding... and for your question as to whether there is anything that should be changed, etc.

In my opinion, there are at least two points to consider:

1) It is evident in the writing that you exercised great caution so that it would not be apparent at all that the author is also a Chassid of Lubavitch. It is a pity to go to such lengths, firstly because the time has already passed when there was any basis for the concern that knowing this would create difficulties in your affairs; now, the opposite is true. Secondly, it is already known that you are a Chassid of Chabad, and therefore, the effort to conceal it is of no use—and in fact, quite the contrary.

2) The greatest satisfaction of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, is that all his activities should be living, enduring, and constantly growing. Therefore, every article and piece of news from a good friend should leave the reader with the impression that the work is continuing and developing, rather than the impression of a mere eulogy.

Surely your mother, may she live, has already found relief, and may her recovery continue to improve. May you be able to share good news regarding this.

I will conclude with wishes and blessings that you and your family, may they live—each and every one of them—should have a healthy and peaceful summer, wherever you may be, in all respects.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***551***

From the Secretariat copy.

## 452

B"H, 28 Tammuz, 5710

To the avreich, our teacher and rabbi [Name]—may he live and be well,

Greetings and blessings!

Your father—may he live and be well—wrote that on 11 Tammuz you celebrated your Bar Mitzvah and requested a blessing. I hereby express my blessings that, in accordance with the blessings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe—of blessed memory—you should be a yarei shamayim, a Chassid, and a lamdan, and that your parents—may they live and be well—should see from you and from their other children—may they live and be well—much nachas, Chassidishe nachas, in a state of expansion.

My father-in-law, the Rebbe—of blessed memory—related that for Bar Mitzvah maamarim, the Nesi'im used to begin with the words "Naaseh Adam" ("Let us make man") (and when the maamar was given to be copied, they sometimes omitted the beginning).

This means that a Bar Mitzvah must know that he is tasked with attaining the status of "Adam," which is the most exalted of the four titles: Adam, Ish, Gever, and Enosh (see Hayom Yom, 4 Elul 5703; D"H Vayavo Amalek 5709, Kuntres 62, Chapter 2). One must work on this immediately and not postpone it for a later time; then, one is assisted from Above to become an "Adam" (see Sefer HaToldot, p. 64).

I trust that you recited a maamar of Chassidus on the day of your Bar Mitzvah, and that you recite the Rebbe’s kapitel of Tehillim every day, which is now 71. You should continue to do so, without a neder, at least until the upcoming Rosh Hashanah 5711.

Signed with blessings,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

452

From the secretariat’s copy.

A similar version was sent to several who reached the age of Bar Mitzvah, and one was copied as an example.

Sefer HaToldot: Admur Maharash, published by the Rebbe (Kehot, 5707).

Kapitel... 71: See also above, Letter 444, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 353

B"H, Erev Rosh Chodesh Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished, etc., Moreinu Harav Nissan, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letters from the 10th and 24th of Tammuz, along with the lists of the students, the matters pertaining to Melach and Machneh Israel, and the *pidyon nefesh*. Your second letter was received on Thursday, and as I was at the *tziyon* at that time, I read the *pidyon nefesh* and the lists there. Furthermore, the telegram regarding your son, may he live, has just been received, and I read it in the chamber of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, where one enters for *yechidus* and to submit *pidyonos*. Surely, the tzaddik, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, is a source of blessing, and the Holy One, blessed be He, fulfills his blessings completely, for each and every person according to their needs. Surely you will not refrain from sharing good news regarding the improvement of the condition of your daughter and son, may they live, going from good to better.

The package for Kazliner has been sent.

I was very pleased to hear that you have already begun the actual implementation regarding Melach and Machneh Israel, as is known from what is written in *Iggeres HaKodesh*, Epistle 21, regarding *zerizus*. However, it would be well to publicize the two conditions for this: a) that there be **no** deficiency whatsoever in the matter of *Nifneh* [the *maamad*] as a result, since it appears that those involved in both are the same people; b) that they should not overextend themselves in their assessment of this.

Regarding success in your work—the work of Heaven—this work itself carries success within it. This is in line with what is explained in *Zohar* Vol. III (98a)—and as was said on the night of Shavuos—"Who prepares her ornaments and illuminates her crown and establishes her order, etc." This is your work with the students, the youth of Israel, and "He loved him." Especially since my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, holds your hand and guides you. One only needs extra caution not to interject one's own desires, and to recall the *Likkutei Torah*, *Shir HaShirim*, the second *maamar* beginning "Tze'ena U'Re'ena," Chapter 4, that even after the level of "doing His will as your own," one must "nullify your will."

With regards to the entire group, may they live.

**M. Schneerson**

***

**353**
From a photocopy of the letter.

**Moreinu Harav Nissan:** Moreinu Harav Nissan Nemanov. Additional letters to him appear above, 440, and in the references noted in the footnotes there.

**The lists of the students:** At the Tomchei Temimim yeshiva in France.

**Regarding Melach and Machneh Israel:** See letter 373 above.

**Nifneh:** A *maamad* [financial support campaign].

## 354

B"H, 2 Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished...

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 6 Tammuz, which was received with some delay:

...You surely have a set schedule for learning Nigleh and Chassidus, and you also participate in group study; it is surprising that you do not mention anything regarding this. If you had written about this, then, according to the saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in the name of his father, the Rebbe, "Two souls are better than one," which is also understandable to the intellect. Yet you omit this and ask specifically about the matters detailed in Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 22, and the like.

Regarding what you write about being lonely, etc., the Alter Rebbe explains at length and clearly that a Tzaddik is found in this world of action **more** than during his lifetime, provided that one follows the paths he instructed, etc., in order to strengthen the hiskashrus. Consequently, you will not be lonely, and the bitterness of heart that you write about will also depart, for you will be a vessel for the blessings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, who blessed you that the Ribono shel Olam should fulfill them in their entirety.

Signing with blessings,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

**354**

From the secretariat copy.

**Saying:** See Igros Kodesh Vol. 2, Letter 383.

**In Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 22:** "Where have you found this custom in any of the books of the sages of Israel, both early and later, that it should be a custom and a standard practice to ask for advice on physical matters, as if it were a law, regarding what to do in the affairs of the physical world."

## 355

B"H, 3 Menachem Av, 5711

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R' Yisroel Zev, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter, along with the enclosure, was received in due time. I read it, and also [brought it] this past Thursday to the resting place of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. He surely blessed each and every one of you with whatever you require, according to your respective needs, and may Hashem fulfill His blessings in their entirety and in a revealed manner.

Regarding your prayer to be worthy vessels for the fulfillment of your desires:

One must not fall into *marah shechorah* (melancholy) or excessive doubt, for the *yetzer* uses this as well.

It has already been discussed at a *farbrengen* here that one must not forget that the entire concept of creation (and I refer here even to creation in a general sense, and also the **totality** of the *Seder Hishtalshelus*) came about because it is the nature of the Good to do good. It is understood that the nature of the Cause is influential and evident within the creation. This is regarding creation as it is in itself. And this is also true regarding what we add to creation through our *avodah*, for all *hashpa'os* come through the leader of the generation, who is the head of the generation, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. He related in a *sicha* on Simchas Torah 5693, and I quote: "Everything that Father told me in the year 5680, I accepted upon myself without a vow, only on the condition of kindness and mercy. My goodness costs me dearly, etc., not to touch, etc., and not to distance, etc." End quote.

Since all of this is ours, we must approach our *avodah* with joy and absolute confidence that Hashem will have mercy and assist us, and that we are being sanctified greatly from Above—provided that we hold on (*anhaltin zich*) to the rope of *hiskashrus* to the *tzaddik* who is found now even more [powerfully] in this world of action.

With blessings to the entire group, may they live,

**M. Schneerson**

## 536

B"H, 3 Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., Moreinu Harav Y.Y. shlita,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter:

While at the Tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, this past Thursday, I mentioned you and your family, may they live.

Regarding your question as to whether you should become fully proficient in the craft of *shechitah* in all its details: in my opinion, it is very appropriate.

As for what you write, that regarding a position as a *shochet* "it is not yet a thought," know that who among us knows what exists in the realm of thought as hidden powers and influences, and especially what has arisen in thought? See *Iggeres HaKodesh*, Epistle 27, regarding "with what does one earn a living, etc., and when will the Kingdom of Heaven return, etc.—behold, they are equated."

Surely you will be able to arrange it in such a way that it will not be in conflict with the other matters of your *avodah*.

Regarding your question, "if one may skip one's *kavua itim* (fixed times for Torah study) for the sake of fundraising":

You did not specify whether this is a *kavua itim* alone or with others (in which case it also weakens the others).

In any case, one must assume that in most instances, this is nothing more than an illusion with which the *yetzer* deceives one, [suggesting] that "one must skip." And if it is truly necessary, then one can make up for the missed *shiurim* at night.

Surely the words of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, at the conclusion of his letter to you from the week of his *histalkus*, are engraved in the letters of your memory, where he blesses you "to succeed in the work of my *shlichus* in both material and spiritual matters." Therefore, be strong in the fulfillment of this *shlichus* of yours, for you will surely see extraordinary success in it. You need only do what is incumbent upon you with joy and keep the channel of *hiskashrus* open.

With blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

Enclosed are the six letters you sent. Thank you.

## 557

557

B”H, 3 Menachem Av, 5711

To the esteemed Chossid, the distinguished scholar, etc., Rabbi M.Z. [Greenglass], may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I have received your two letters, along with the sheets regarding the daily halachos, etc., from Rabbi Y.H. HaLevi, and the sefer *Mekor Chaim* to be returned in due time. I thank you for the aforementioned.

Regarding the matters of *melachah* and *machaneh*—if this could cause any deficiency in his *avodah* at his post, it is obvious that he must be careful about this.

Regarding the *Likutei Dinim* for daily use—in general, in my opinion, it is difficult to establish hard-and-fast rules as to what is most necessary to include in such a compilation. Surely, not all locations are the same, either. But on the other hand, even if it is not organized according to a systematic method, it brings benefit, as it prevents one from committing some of the prohibitions mentioned therein and arouses one to perform a matter that one is obligated to do.

I have asked Rabbi R.M.A. Hodakov to review the aforementioned in order to provide notes, and they are enclosed.

In my opinion, the *marei mekomos* should be at the bottom so as not to confuse the simple reader. The name could be, as a mere suggestion off the cuff: *Kovetz Likutei Dinim. Kovetz Alef*, or "Necessary Instructions for Every Day, First Issue," and the like.

One of my acquaintances pointed out to me some time ago that there is a need for this awareness among *anash* as well regarding the laws of *shmiras halashon*, and perhaps it is worthwhile to include something on this, at least briefly.

Regarding what you wrote—that because he had emotional distress he is not involving himself in...—is it possible that he weighs these two things as equal? A temporary emotional distress of an individual, versus damage (in his opinion) that affects several years or the entire lifetime of the student and of many others? I am doubly astonished at him.

Regarding the question of the kindergarten, I spoke here at length with the guests from Montreal, and surely they conveyed the matters as they are. The crux of the matter is that this is where Lubavitch and the Hungarians differ: the latter held to the method of isolation, while Lubavitch is not so. It is obvious that this approach does not mean total connection in all matters, but the general movement is to "take the other person along with you," even if, because of this, one is forced to walk slowly. And that which the upper waters were separated from the lower waters is called *machlokes* (dispute), and it is impossible to write then "that it was good." See the language of our Sages (*Bereishis Rabbah* 4:6): A *machlokes* that is for the sake of the world’s rectification and its settlement (**the Torah** testifies that it is for the rectification of the world), and yet, there is no "that it was good" in it.

And the rule is known that a person is willing to commit a minor prohibition in order to save [others] from a major prohibition (*Eruvin* 32b and *Tosafos* there; *Sdei Chemed*, *Klalim* 6, 26). And the definition of a *Chossid* is one who places himself in danger to save his fellow. There is much to elaborate on this. I told them that **after** kindergarten hours, they should arrange a room [for Torah study], and the like.

And they should do the same with other matters that can be done without forcing the children of Canada.

...Regarding the sefer *Nishmas Chaim*, I have not heard about this. In general, it is a great merit that he strengthened the belief in *gilgul* (reincarnation), but in his book, straw is mixed with the wheat, as is obvious to one who examines his book.

I have the sefer *Likutei Maharich*, and I enjoyed several citations from it, sources for customs, and the like.

A pamphlet in Yiddish regarding *taharas hamishpachah* was printed some time ago in Warsaw, I believe (by the Agudas HaRabbonim), and you will certainly be able to find a copy to print from it (with changes according to the conditions of the place and the Hungarian people).

Wishing you the best in all matters and proper health for your household, and please inform me from time to time of these matters.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 598

B"H, 3 Menachem Av, 5711

To the esteemed and distinguished [name], may he be blessed...

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter, and in accordance with your request, while at the Tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, I mentioned you regarding your livelihood wherever you may be; the Ribono shel Olam can certainly provide livelihood in Montreal as well.

Surely, as a student of Tomchei Temimim, you have your shiurim in Nigleh and Chassidus, and you also study the Chassidus of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, at fixed times. It is also worthwhile to recite, bli neder, the Rebbe’s Kapitel Tehillim, chapter 71, every day until the upcoming Rosh Hashanah. This serves as a vessel for the fulfillment of the blessings he bestowed upon you in their entirety.

With blessings,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***598***

From the Secretariat’s copy.

**Chapter 71:** See also the aforementioned letter 594, and the references in the notes there. And there—until 10 Shevat 5711.

## 359

359

B”H, 3 Menachem Av, 5711

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R’ S. שי'

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter of 21 Tammuz. While I was at the Tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, on Thursday, I mentioned your names, each according to their needs.

Regarding your question about a transfer, I see no reason for alarm.

It would be best to speak with R’ B.G. שי' after his return from Morocco regarding the possibility of transferring there, in a manner that would allow you to continue the work there that my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, entrusted to you and gave you the strength for. If that is not a possibility, you should look into a transfer to Australia.

"The steps of man are established by Hashem," and He will guide you and arrange for you a place and a manner that is suitable for you and your family שי' in both spiritual and material matters. You will then be able to engage in *hafatzas hamaayanos chutzah* (spreading the wellsprings outward), and thereby hasten our redemption and the redemption of all of *Klal Yisrael*. May my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, lead us all, together with our families שי', upright (see *Sanhedrin 100b*, *Dah V’asisa Chash* 5716) to our land.

With blessings and regards,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

359

From the Secretariat archives.

**R’ S.:** Libarov. Additional letters to him – see below 379, 623.

**R’ B.G.:** R’ Binyamin Gorodetzky.

**His return from Morocco:** See above letter 320, and the references in the notes there.

**To transfer there:** To engage in the work of education, as above in letter 259, and the references in the notes there.

He did indeed travel there. – See below letter 623.

## 660

B”H, 3 Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed and G-d-fearing [individual], etc.

Greetings and blessings!

You have surely received my letter from 6 Tammuz, and I hope you will respond to the questions contained within it.

Your son, may he live and be well, informed me that your daughter... is at times not feeling well. Surely she has consulted a doctor and is following his instructions. Since she lights candles every Erev Shabbos and Yom Tov, you should also ensure that, at the very least until she turns eighteen, she should place three coins into a *tzedakah* *pushke* for *R’ Meir Baal HaNes* before lighting the candles. It would also be good if you would recite Chapter 71 of *Tehillim* every day until *Rosh Hashanah*.

Your son, may he live and be well, also informed me regarding his aunt... that she is not in the best of health. She should also be told the aforementioned regarding following the instructions of a specialist doctor, and to place three coins into a *pushke* for *R’ Meir Baal HaNes* before lighting candles, and at least once a week she should provide a meal for a Jew (a guest or a poor person).

Healing is performed by the *Ribono shel Olam*; He brings it about, in part, through a doctor. However, the **primary** medicine for Jews that He has ordained is *tzedakah*, *maasim tovim*, and a chapter of *Tehillim*. I hope to hear from you that your health has improved and continues to get better.

**On behalf of Rabbi Menachem**

**Schneerson**

Rabbi A. Quint, Secretary

#### ***660***

From the secretariat copy.

**Chapter 71:** See above, Letter 644, and the references in the notes there.

## 601

B”H, 5 Menachem Av, the Hillula of the Arizal, 5711

To the esteemed members of the Committee for the Support of Merkos L’Inyonei Chinuch, Machne Israel, and Kehot, the G-d-fearing men who occupy themselves with the needs of the community, and who are bound to my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—which is the most important of all—may Hashem grant them life.

Peace and blessing!

I was delighted to hear that, with the help of Hashem, you have established a committee for the support of Merkos L’Inyonei Chinuch, Machne Israel, and Kehot, and I am certain that you have already begun practical activities.

I am sending my blessing to the members of the committee who have gathered to strengthen themselves in the merit of the avodah of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—which is to secure the means to strengthen and expand the work of the aforementioned institutions. Through this, you will also strengthen your hiskashrus with my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, who, from the day he founded these institutions, carried the world upon his shoulders. Now, it is the duty and the privilege of all those bound to him to participate with him in this good and boundless inheritance that he has bequeathed to us.

When you contemplate this duty and privilege well, your desire—and even your ability—will be strengthened, and the desire of Hashem will succeed in your hands.

Please inform me of the names of the committee members—if any have been added—so that I may mention them at the Tziyun of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

With wishes for all the best to the entire group, may they live, awaiting good tidings.

**M. Schneerson**

***

601

From a photocopy of the letter. See also the subsequent letter.

The Committee for the Support: See letter 629 above, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 602

B"H, 5 Menachem Av, 5712 (the Hilula of the Arizal)
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed members of the committee for the support of Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch, Machne Israel, and Kehot, the G-d-fearing men who occupy themselves with the needs of the community, and who are bound to my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—which is the most important of all—may Hashem grant them life.

Peace and blessing!

It was with pleasure that I read in your letter the news regarding your good work in supporting the institutions of Merkos, Machne Israel, and Kehot. Surely, as with all matters of holiness, your activities and their results will continue to grow and increase, until they become "exceedingly great" (see the Maamar of Chai Elul, *d'h Hashkifa*, Chapter 8, and in *Likutei Torah* noted in the footnotes there regarding the virtue of "exceedingly great").

Time does not permit me to write at length, so I will suffice with a few lines, and a word to the wise is sufficient.

There are several modes of connection—one person to another, a student to his teacher, a Chossid to his Rebbe—and there are various levels within this.

Connection in general is achieved by following in the ways of the one to whom one is connected, and this is divided into three types, from below to above, corresponding to the nature of the ways of the one to whom one is connecting:

(a) He requests or instructs others to act—the connection of the one who connects is achieved by fulfilling this command and instruction.

(b) He acts in partnership with others—the connection of the one who connects is achieved by also participating in this.

(c) He acts himself—the connection of the one who connects is achieved by replacing him in his activities when the opportunity is given to him.

In my letter of Erev Rosh Chodesh Tammuz, I came to awaken the Chassidim who are connected or belong to my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that now the opportunity has been given for a connection on the third level—a connection of their nefesh, ru'ach, neshamah, chayah, and yechidah to the Nasi and head of our generation, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

It is self-understood that I do not intend, Heaven forbid, to diminish the great value of the other institutions of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and certainly not to diminish, Heaven forbid, the matter of the *maamad*. It is well known that all matters of holiness complete one another and do not, Heaven forbid, detract from one another.

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* and success in your holy work, which is also among the broad vessels for the drawing down and receiving of all material and spiritual good for each and every one, for all that they require.

I await good tidings.

**M. Schneerson**

## 603

B"H, 6 Menachem Av, 5713

To the avreich, the G-d-fearing, etc., R' ... may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to his letter:

May it be the will [of Hashem] that he be a fitting vessel for the fulfillment of all the blessings with which the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, has blessed him and continues to bless him, in their entirety, both materially and spiritually. And may he succeed in his personal avodah and in his avodah with others.

Especially since with this birthday, his twenty-fourth year begins, in which, according to all opinions, the days of youth are completed (see Kiddushin 30a; Hilchos Talmud Torah of the Alter Rebbe, Chapter 1, end of Section 6).

Signing with blessing,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***603***

From a photocopy of the letter.

**According to all opinions:** See letter 596 above.

## 664

B"H, 6 Menachem Av, 5711

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R' Shneur Zalman, may he live:

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 25 Tammuz:

I see no basis for the panic to leave your current location at this time. Since my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, wrote to you at the time—as you quote in your aforementioned letter—that "for the time being, he should remain in his place and continue his holy work with the students, may they live," you should be where the students are. Surely, the blessing of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, will be fulfilled, that Hashem will strengthen your health and the health of your wife, may she live, and grant you success in all your endeavors.

When one can walk under the Rebbe’s responsibility and with his strength, why must one intervene with human actions and schemes?

May Hashem turn these days into joy and gladness in our immediate future, and may He redeem us with a true redemption through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, speedily and in our days, Amen.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***664***

From the secretariat’s copy.

**R' Shneur Zalman:** Levitin. Another letter to him — see above, 507.

## 655

B"H, 7 Menachem Av, 5711

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished... may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

Your letter dated 4 Menachem Av has just been received, along with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed therein. In the meantime, my letters from the beginning of the month of Menachem Av have surely reached you.

Regarding the welfare of your son, may he live: I am surprised at *anash*, may they live, who "are stringent" in interpreting everything that happens to them, even though they know the saying of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in the name of several of the Chabad *Nesi'im*: "Think good and it will be good." See also *Horiyos* (12a): "And it is of no consequence, etc."

I have just read the aforementioned *pidyon nefesh* in the *heichal* of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and he will surely bless you, and his blessings will be fulfilled in their entirety.

Signing with blessings and awaiting good news from you,

**M. Schneerson**

#### ***655***

From a photocopy of the letter.

**Saying:** See *Igros Kodesh* of the Rebbe, Vol. 7, Letter 1990, in the name of the *Tzemach Tzedek*. See also below, Letter 675.

## 606

B"H, 7 Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc. ... may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Monday:

Naturally, the preferred method from the perspective of Halacha is that they take the seed from the woman after the *tashmish*, though it must be done in close proximity to the time of the act, and the doctor will certainly instruct how to do this. Analyses have already been performed in this manner and were successful.

The Gaon of Rogatchov, although generally stringent in these matters, also permits in his *Shu"t* (*Tzafnas Paneach*, Part 1, end of Chapter 9) a method where a very tight sheath is placed upon the organ so that there is no air at all between the sheath and the organ, in which case the seed remains in the sheath.

May the *Ribono shel Olam* help that everything should proceed properly and with success.

I read your *pidyon nefesh* in the chamber of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, where one enters for *yechidus* and to submit *pidyonos*. He will certainly bless you and your wife—may she live—with what you both need, and specifically with *zera chaya v'kayama*, and the *Hakadosh Baruch Hu* fulfills the blessings of the *tzaddikim* to their fullest extent. And when Hashem gladdens your hearts, you will surely share the good news.

With blessings for the above and for the complete and true *Geulah*.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***606***

From the secretariat copy. For its content, see letter 480 below.

**The Gaon of Rogatchov:** See letter 625 above.

I received your telegram in due time, and as you requested, I read the *pidyon* in the room of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, where one enters for *yechidus* and to submit *pidyonos*.

And a *tzaddik*—our *Nasi*, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—bestows blessings, and the *Ribono shel Olam* fulfills his blessings, both materially and spiritually, in their entirety.

It is appropriate that you recite Psalm 71 every day, *bli neder*, at least until the coming *Rosh Hashanah*.

With blessings, awaiting good news,
