## 386

B”H, 21 Menachem Av, Hashem Yisbarach

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [may he live],

Peace and blessing!

I received your letter from Erev Shabbos Kodesh Va’eschanan, along with the enclosure regarding the situation at Beis Chayeinu. The receipt is enclosed.

I will read the pidyon, as you requested, in the chamber of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, where one enters to submit pidyonos and for yechidus; I will also read it at an appropriate time at the Tziyun.

Surely our Nasi, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, will grant his blessing that everything should proceed in its time, properly and with ease, and may Hashem fulfill His blessings in their entirety, both materially and spiritually. I am certain you will share good news.

With blessing,

**Menachem Schneerson**

Regarding what you asked previously concerning the question of how it is possible to have worlds without number, and I replied that this is due to the power of the Ein Sof within them, for whom there are no impossibilities: You raise the difficulty that if so, the proof that time was created—which is demonstrated in Derech Mitzvosecha—falls away, as it argues that it is impossible for many limited divisions to result in the unlimited. You wonder why we cannot say that in the case of time, too, this occurs due to the power of the Ein Sof within it, and how do we know that time was created?

The general answer to this and similar matters is that, in truth, it **could** be said regarding **every** single thing in which the power of the Ein Sof is revealed—that it is the "impossible of impossibilities," and therefore the thing does not fall under any law or logical proof. However, the Torah states that the world was created in a manner that conforms to the laws of logic, as is proven by the fact that all the laws of the Torah necessitate truth and logical proof. The **only** exceptions to this rule are those matters regarding which it is **explicitly** stated that they are not subject to the laws of logic. Therefore, regarding the worlds, where it is stated in many places in the words of our Sages that they are without number, we are forced to say that this is due to the power of the Ein Sof within them.

This is not the case with other matters where this is not explicitly stated. Especially when one comes to prove through **logic** that time was created, we are permitted to accept the logical proof. For in any event: if it is from the perspective of faith, there is no need for proof that time was created; and if one wishes to prove this through logic, we provide him with the proof that logic itself says this. One could elaborate on this, but this brief explanation will surely suffice for you.

I would like to know what is happening with Mr. ... and I thank you for your trouble in informing me.

**The Above.**

## 287

B"H, 21 Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned, etc., R’ D.B. shlita,

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter of 14 Menachem Av, and I hope that your father, shlita, as well as your brother-in-law, sister, and son, shlita, have surely settled in well.

As you requested, I mentioned you in the *heichal* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *kein yehi ratzon*, regarding your scheduled return trip for Friday, Erev Shabbos, 5 Elul. Surely, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, *kein yehi ratzon*, will bless you that the journey should proceed properly.

I received a letter from a bookseller, Mordechai Dovid Blum in Yerushalayim (Meah Shearim market, Beis HaRav Charlap), who writes that he has some *bichlach* (booklets) of *kisvei* *DA"CH* and wishes to sell them to *Kehot*. If you happen to be in Yerushalayim, please be so kind as to check whether they are original manuscripts in the Rebbe’s handwriting or merely copies, and also if you can ascertain whether these writings have already been printed or not. If the price is very low, you may purchase them in any case. It is certainly advisable that Mr. Blum not know that you are acting on instructions from here, as that would raise the price. Thank you in advance for your trouble.

Signing with blessings for a proper journey and regards to your father, shlita, and his family, shlita,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***287***

From the secretariat copy.

**R’ D.B.:** Haskind, who traveled to Eretz Yisrael to meet his father, Daniel, who had arrived in Eretz Yisrael from China. **His journey... 5 Elul:** See letter 284 above.

## 288

288

B"H, 21 Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and learned... may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 16 Menachem Av regarding the health of your wife, may she live.

It is incomprehensible why you write about reasons why she cannot conceive, when in your earlier letter you mentioned that in Elul 5709 she was indeed pregnant. It can certainly be the same now.

Therefore, it seems to me that she should not do things that are dangerous, as you describe. She should consult once more with an expert doctor regarding what she should do, and surely he will find remedies in a calm manner, and through a certain doctor and a certain medication, everything will pass, and the blessing of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—that Hashem should gladden their hearts with living and enduring children—will be fulfilled.

Regarding what you write, that your wife, may she live, has become nervous, etc., because of all this:

You must convey to her that the Ribono shel Olam leads the world in the best way possible; He knows what is best, and He has written in the Torah that children are a blessing.

Consequently, this comes to Yidden, and if at times the blessing is delayed, we must know that we all have a great Rebbe—my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—and he will ultimately bring about all his blessings for all those who are connected to him.

However, one must maintain that hiskashrus with him. If one begins to rethink the fulfillment of his blessing and becomes nervous about it, this is a sign of weakness, chas v'shalom, in bitachon and in hiskashrus. In particular, if this also affects one's health, it is certainly nothing more than a suggestion of the yetzer hara.

Both you and she must be strong in your bitachon in Hashem and in the Moshe Rabbeinu of our generation, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and this in itself helps to draw down his holy blessing for living and enduring children and for all good things, sooner.

Surely, your wife, may she live, should—without a vow—give tzedakah to the Kupas Rabbi Meir Baal HaNes when she lights Shabbos candles, and you should say—without a vow—the Rebbe’s chapter of Tehillim every day, which is chapter 71.

When your wife, may she live, becomes pregnant, b'sha'ah tovah u'mutzlach, you will likely not publicize it for the first while, but you should immediately inform the Rebbe at the Tziyun.

Signing with a blessing,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 1929

B"H, 23 Menachem Av, 5689

To the esteemed and distinguished scholar, etc., Rabbi D. shlita,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Erev Shabbos Nachamu, which brings the news of your settling into a suitable position:

I thank Hashem for the good news, and may Hashem grant that you always bring tidings of only good, both regarding your personal affairs and those of your surroundings.

For the Rambam rules (Hilchos Deos, Chapter 6, Halacha 1) that it is the nature of a person to be drawn in their opinions and actions after their friends and associates, etc.; therefore, a person must associate with the righteous and sit with the wise, etc.

However, this refers to the essence of a person as a recipient, which is their starting point. Immediately, they must also become a *mashpia*, as demanded by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, in his *sicha* printed in the *Kuntres* of 12 Tammuz, 5689, section 3: "Live yourself, and make others live."

And since this is demanded of us, we have certainly been given the ability to do so. And when there is the will, each and every one of us will illuminate our surroundings—and there is no light other than Torah and *mitzvos*, as it is written: "For a *mitzvah* is a lamp, and Torah is light."

With blessings and regards,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

1929

Printed in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 23, p. 4, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**Rabbi D.:** Hellman.

**In the Kuntres:** *Sefer HaMaamarim 5710*, p. 262.

## 390

B"H, 23 Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., our teacher Rabbi... may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Friday, Erev Shabbos Va'eschanan:

I was happy to hear of the improvement in the health of your wife, may she live. Surely you are following the instructions of the expert doctors who examined her, and her condition will certainly continue to improve. Please share good news regarding this.

It is surprising that you do not mention anything regarding your *kavuah* study of the Torah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, which I prompted you about in my previous letter. This is the *keli* that a Chossid must choose for his *hiskashrus* with his Rebbe—my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. This is in accordance with what is explained in *Chassidus* regarding the virtue of the limbs, which are not merely vessels that receive, but also vessels that draw down—drawing down his blessings in all that is needed, in both *gashmius* and *ruchnius*.

With blessings for success in your *avodas hakodesh*, awaiting good news from you,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***390***

From the secretariat copy.

## 291

B"H, 23 Menachem Av, 5711

To the esteemed and distinguished scholar, etc., Rabbi D.M., may he live,
Shochet and Bodek

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter, and I was happy to hear that the condition of your wife, may she live, is improving. May you continue to report only good and kindness, amen, may it be His will.

Regarding what you noted in the *hemshech* "Mayim Rabim" 5636:

1) In chapter 33, where a distinction is made between the initial creation and the renewal of the world, and the proof brought from the Midrash Rabbah (Bereishis Rabbah, ch. 64:4): "From then until now, what is He doing, etc."

You raise a difficulty, arguing that the words cannot be taken literally—that the Holy One, blessed be He, was occupied with the act of creation for six days, etc. However, your difficulty does not weaken the proof. The proof is that, regardless of how one interprets the words in the Midrash Rabbah, it is **necessary** there that there is a distinction between the creation during the six days of creation and the creation thereafter. A brief explanation of the Midrash could be: The question is, "The Holy One, blessed be He (who is *Sovev Kol Almin*, and who extends and is revealed in a manner of *Memale Kol Almin*, meaning in place and time—in this) created His world for how many days (does this revelation extend)?" He replied, "Six days; from then until now, what is He doing (and in what way is this light revealed)?" Regarding the basis for explaining the name *Kudsha Brich Hu*, see *Likutei Torah*, Tazria 22c, and elsewhere.

2) In chapter 34, where a parable is brought that from the *Ein Sof* a boundary is formed, like a spring that flows without cessation, yet they fill from it into measured vessels. You raise a difficulty that the *nimshal* (the concept being explained)—where the *shefa* (flow) changes the recipient—is not similar to the parable, because the vessel is not changed by the water drawn into it. You suggest that the second parable, of the flow of wisdom from a great sage to a small student, is more appropriate.

Actually, the first parable is also brought elsewhere in *Chassidus* (see *Biurei HaZohar*, Vayishlach) with a bit more explanation, adding that because the flow of water is incessant, it would wash away the vessels. It is explained in *Chassidus* (*HaYom Yom*, Rosh Hashanah 5706) that this occurs in one of three different ways: a) The vessels would break entirely. b) At the very least, they would be nullified from the status of a "vessel," whose purpose is to receive, and here the water would not remain within it due to the force of the flow, and would flow back out. c) Even from the outset, it would not enter the vessel at all, due to the *Ein Sof* in the flow. Thus, even in this parable, the recipient is changed. However, in a spring where the flow is drop by drop, naturally none of the three aforementioned scenarios apply.

One might add a bit of clarification by first addressing the question: Why were two parables necessary, and what does one add to the other? For surely it is not an empty matter.

The matter, in brief: At first glance, the concept of *Bli Gvul* (or something higher, the concept of *Ein Sof*) must be *Ein Sof*, since the source is **not** limited—both in the essence of the flow and in the thing that is flowing.

In truth, this is not the case in both instances.

Regarding this, the two parables were brought: The parable of the spring—where the flow is without cessation, but the thing that is flowing is limited (drops). And the parable of the sage—where the flow **in revelation** has ceased, but the intellect that is flowing is *Ein Sof* **relative** to the recipient, for "a person does not grasp the mind of his teacher until forty years have passed," and only after forty years, having grown in the interim, does he understand the depth of the wisdom that was imparted to him **forty years prior**.

In the *nimshal*, one could say, perhaps, that this is the distinction between the "host of the heavens," which exist in permanence and have no offspring—the flow has ceased, but the *chayus* (vitality) that was flowed is *Ein Sof* (as explained in chapter 33)—and the "host of the earth," which do not exist in permanence but have offspring—the *chayus* that is flowed is limited, but the flow does not cease.

One could elaborate on this, but for the wise, the above is sufficient.

See also *Sefer HaChakirah* by the Tzemach Tzedek, Part 1, at the beginning.

With blessings for the healing of body and soul alike, for you and your household, may they live,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

## 1932

By the Grace of Hashem, 24 Menachem Av, 5712

To the esteemed and distinguished Rabbi, a man of action, etc., Moreinu Harav Shneur Zalman, may he live,
Shaliach,

Peace and blessing!

In response to your prompt letter (though the date of writing was not indicated),

I was very pleased to hear of your positive activities in the places you visited, as detailed in your letter. Certainly, in addition to what is revealed and accomplished **during** your visit to a city or town, many matters are being brought about without being known at this time, and there will be many results and fruits from the seeds sown during the visit itself. Holiness produces fruits, and the fruits of its fruits, until the end of all the world (*le’olam*—from the root *he’lem* and *hester*, concealment), as per the words of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in the *sicha* of 12 Tammuz, end of section 1: "Holiness is *Ein Sof*."

You are certainly also working and acting in your travels regarding the *nifneh* (status/standing), which, aside from being part of your *shlichus*, is known and visibly evident that anything that has a foothold in the physical realm as well, is enduring. It is known that this is also one of the reasons why the prophets would sometimes precede their prophecy with some physical action, as is mentioned in many places in the *Neviim Acharonim*.

It is surprising that in the *shiurim* you mentioned seeing in the *beis medrash*, there are few *shiurim* for learning *halacha l’maaseh* regarding the **common** laws.

Regarding your question—since my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, wrote to you that you "should not get involved in this," that is certainly the correct path.

As for your question whether to begin now by visiting the towns on the way here, and to spend the month of Tishrei here and in the vicinity of New York:

In my opinion, it is highly necessary that precisely this year you utilize the end of Elul and the month of Tishrei to strengthen your standing and position in your camp and its surroundings. These times are periods of *hisorerus*, even according to the custom of this country—days of *taamulah*, days of *farbrengen*, etc., etc. And it would be best if you could arrange for yourself, in good time, a detailed program on how to utilize (*oysnutzen*) **all** the aforementioned days in the most beneficial and appropriate manner.

Regarding your previous question about establishing a *beis medrash* with *Nusach Ari* in your camp—while the matter itself is correct, you yourself, due to your responsibilities and *avodah*, should not and cannot be tied to one *beis medrash*, for your net must be spread over **the entire** city and its surroundings.

I am searching for a suitable person to send to your camp, who will be the rabbi and teach *shiurim*, etc., in said *beis medrash*, and naturally, also assist in its founding. You, sir, will be the one standing **above** him. However, for the time being, I have not found one. I will continue to search.

*Bli neder*, I will mention you at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for success in your holy work, as you requested. This is also the channel for *hashpaos* in *gashmius* and *ruchnius* in all that is needed.

I sign with a blessing for success in your general and specific matters, for you and your family, may they live.

**M. Schneerson**

Regarding the *dvar Torah* from the *farbrengen*—you have surely already spoken with Rabbi M.A. Hodakov, may he live.

## 393

B"H, 26 Menachem Av, 5713

To the distinguished Rabbi and Chassid, G-d-fearing and learned, etc.,
Rabbi Schneur Zalman, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I received your detailed letter from the month of Tammuz with a slight delay. Thank you for the information regarding the scheduling of your study sessions, which you described in detail.

As you requested, I am enclosing here some points regarding the *siyum* on Maseches Kiddushin that you are studying in public. I am certain that if you have any comments on this, you will let me know.

Regarding your inquiry as to whether I have notes from previous Chassidim explaining the Tanya: surely you have a copy of what is in the possession of the distinguished Rabbi and Chassid, etc., Rabbi M. Axelrod, as well as what was copied by the Chassid, etc., Rabbi A. Paris.

The report I heard—though I do not know if it is accurate—is not good: that in the village where the Chassid, etc., Rabbi R... resides, whom the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, appointed to [a position] among them, he is being hindered in his duties for various reasons. I do not wish to delve into the facts of the matter, but on the face of it, because of our *hiskashrus* to our Nasi, the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, it is incumbent upon each and every one of us to make every effort not to deviate from anything he commanded, in all its details—especially in matters that concern the public and matters that are to be publicized. Certainly, even if there are claims and arguments regarding the interference, it is possible to find ways to impose penalties or the like, but one should not alter the appointments made by the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory. Naturally, I am writing all of this as a possibility, without knowing the details of the matter and without wishing to involve myself in them, especially since I am not on-site. I rely on your understanding that the matter will be arranged in the best possible way for all parties.

I hope to hear good news from you in the future regarding everything that is happening for the good, and I conclude with blessings and regards to the entire group, may they live.

**Menachem Schneerson**

Regarding what you wrote about the *mikveh*: You mentioned that you made an additional *otzar* on the side so that there would also be *zriah*. According to the method of the Rebbe (Rashab), which is a *mikveh* **on top of** the *otzar*, and he wanted the water of the lower one to rise above the floor of the upper one (according to the words of Rabbi Y.L. Landau), this is *zriah* in the most effective manner, such that no *hashakah* is required at all according to all opinions, obviously. Even if they do not rise above the floor, one should arrange it so that the *sha'uvim* (drawn water) fall **directly** into the opening leading to the lower *otzar*—which constitutes *zriah*. If so, what does the *otzar* on the side add? (Unless it is merely as a precaution, in case the lower *otzar* becomes compromised).

## 394

By the Grace of Hashem, 26 Menachem Av

To the esteemed Rabbi, the Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu Harav Shlomo Chaim, may he be a Shaliach.

Peace and Blessing!

I received your letter of the 17th of Menachem Av, and as you requested, I will read the *pidyonos* enclosed therein at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Thank you for the good news regarding the improvement in your wife’s health, may she live. But consider this: before the surgery, you wrote several times with *pidyonos* that the surgery should go well. Now that Hashem has fulfilled the blessings of the *tzaddik*—my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—and the surgery was successful, you did not even mention it, and now you write again only when you are distressed (regarding her general state of health). In my opinion, the correct order is: first, to give thanks to Hashem for His great kindness in the past, and then to pray, together with complete *bitachon* that Hashem will continue His blessings and that her condition will steadily improve. The power of *emunah* and *bitachon* is great, capable of drawing down all influences in a revealed and manifest good.

Regarding what you wrote about the *seder* of learning *Chassidus* in the three classes, etc.: I have already stated several times that at least for this year, every single person—and especially the *temimim*—must **set** a time to study the teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. In the institutions, this must also be part of the **curriculum** of every single class.

Concerning the joint committee for *Melach*, *Machaneh*, and *Kehot* that you mention is still "pending": it is already well-known that the purpose of all human *avodah* is to transform things that are "pending"—or, in other words, matters of *reshus* (optional)—into a matter of Torah and *mitzvah*. This is explained in many places in the teachings of *Chassidus* and the *sichos*. Especially in this case, as this is also one of the ways of *hiskashrus* to our *Nasi*, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Surely, for someone like you, further elaboration is unnecessary. My suggestion is that you announce the names of those volunteering a monthly contribution, along with their names and their mothers' names, and the amount of their contribution, and they will be mentioned at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Incidentally, you included two pearls in your letter: a saying of the Rebbe (Rashab), of blessed memory, and a saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. There is no doubt that many more of the holy *maamarim* of the *Nesi'ei Chabad* are stored in your memory. I have already written several times that my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, very much desired that all *sichos*, sayings, and the like be preserved by having each person write them down, so that they may all be collected by the editorial board of "Otzar HaChassidim" at *Kehot*. Surely, in time, this will be of benefit to many. It is surprising that you are not participating in the work of gathering these pearls. However, relying on the saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that "it is never too late," I hope that at the very least, you will now begin this with the appropriate vigor and reveal the treasures hidden in your memory.

I sign with a blessing, and regards to the entire group, may they live.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 5712

By the Grace of Hashem, 26 Menachem Av, 5712

To the esteemed avreich, the chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, etc., R’ Sh. shlita,

Peace and blessing!

I received your two letters from Rosh Chodesh and the 8th of Tammuz. Following the report from the esteemed chossid and rav, etc., R’ B.G. shlita, regarding the matters in Africa, it is my opinion that it is appropriate for you to take upon yourself the work in the field of chinuch in our mosdos there. Even though it is still difficult to determine the details of the work there, as it is a new endeavor, one can nevertheless hope for a broad field in the area of strengthening Torah and Yiddishkeit, and consequently, for exceptional success through the kochos of our Nasi, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Fortunate is the portion of all those *who will be on his wagon*. It is self-understood that I mean that this is also for your own benefit and the benefit of your wife, may she live and be well, and for establishing a happy life.

I am not writing this—as you phrased it in your letter to me—"as private individuals." For Temimim in particular, and those connected to my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in general, are not private individuals. Rather, each and every one of them is a candle to illuminate their entire environment, living personalities to bring life and to actually revive one more, two more, three more, etc., and their fruits produce fruits, and the fruits of their fruits, until the end of the entire world—the *lelam* (concealment) and *hester* (hiddenness). And in addition to the reward of the mitzvah, the mitzvah itself, in that they are fulfilling their purpose in life, is in and of itself a vessel that receives and **draws down** the blessings of the Tzaddik—who is found now in this world even more than during his lifetime—in both gashmius and ruchnius, in everything that is needed.

Regarding the details of your work—and surely your wife, may she live and be well, will also participate in this as time goes on—we will speak further when the time for your successful journey draws closer. However, since obtaining the documents takes a certain amount of time, it is good that you submit the application for this as soon as possible. Surely there are methods and formats that shorten the time and the bureaucratic procedures until the documents are submitted, and you should inform yourself about this accurately.

With blessings for success in your private life and in your communal work,

**M. Schneerson**

***

5712

From a photocopy of the letter.

R’ Sh.: Matossov. Additional letters to him—above 587, and in the references in the notes there. R’ B.G. shlita: R’ Binyamin Gorodetzky.

The matters in Africa: See above, letter 559, and the references in the notes there. He did indeed travel there—see below, letters 826, 892.

## 726

B"H, 26 Menachem Av, 5710

To the distinguished Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc. ... may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, 15 Menachem Av, thank you for the good news regarding the improvement in the health of... may he live. I am certain that you will inform me very soon of further improvement in his health, until he is restored to full strength.

Certainly, he sleeps with a yarmulke on his head, and you tell him—in terms **appropriate** for his age—about our Nasi, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. You should also tell him that even now, the *tzaddik* who has departed is found in all worlds—including this world of action—even more than during his lifetime, and he bestows blessings upon all who are *mekushar* to him, especially upon young boys and girls, as is explained in *Chassidus* regarding the concept that "Israel is a **youth** (*na'ar*), and I loved him."

Thank you for the fact that through your efforts, many of our brethren, may they be blessed, have begun reciting the 71st chapter of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory. The merit of the many is dependent upon you.

Awaiting good news from you very soon, and signing with a blessing,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### 726

From the Secretariat copy.

**Chapter 71:** See above, Letter 744, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 397

B"H, 26 Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed and G-d-fearing [name], may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter from Erev Shabbos Nachamu, and I praise Hashem for the news that your health has improved. It will surely continue to improve further, as is the case with all matters of holiness—for the creation of a Yid’s body is also a matter of holiness. As my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, said several times in the name of his father, the Rebbe (Rashab), of blessed memory: How precious this [the body] is, for the sake of which so many Torah and mitzvos were given—which must always ascend higher and higher.

So too must it be with the health of a Yid; it should not, G-d forbid, distract him from Torah and avodah.

A special thank you for the news regarding the regular study of Chassidus, with farbrengens, as well as for the news regarding the studies in Beis Sarah and Beis Rivkah. It is very appropriate that the students traveling to America should have the address of Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch. However, one must also ensure that the students traveling to other countries maintain their connection, and through this, receive the necessary encouragement and strengthening in their path within their new circumstances.

I will read the pidyon nefesh, as requested, at the tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May it be His will that her journey takes place at the proper time and in a successful manner.

Signing with blessings and regards to the entire group, may they live,

**Menachem Schneerson**

***

From the secretariat copy.

**Said in the name of his father:** See also above, letters 385, 386.

## 398

B"H, 27 Menachem Av, 5708

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu Harav R' A., may he live, Shaliach.

Peace and blessing!

I assume you have received my previous letters, and it is surprising that I have not received a response to them, especially regarding the state of health of... may she live.

I also wish to urge you that regarding the Vaad HaMelach, it is appropriate to send here the names of all those who have pledged a monthly contribution to Melach, Machaneh, and Kehos—their names and their mothers' names, along with the amount of the contribution—so that they may be mentioned at the Tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, before the upcoming Rosh Hashanah.

Please be so kind as to inform me of the state of health of the esteemed Rav, etc., etc., Moreinu Harav R' Yehoshua, may he live, Merari...

Awaiting good news and signing with a blessing.

#### ***398***

From a copy.

**Moreinu Harav R' A.:** Paris. Additional letters to him—see above 315, and the references in the notes there. **Vaad HaMelach:** See above, letter 373.

## 399

B"H, 27 Menachem Av, 5709

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., Rabbi B. (may he live),

Greetings and blessings!

I have received with thanks your letters of 10 and 19 Tammuz, along with the enclosed copy of the letter from my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), dated 1 Cheshvan 5699, as well as the list of maamarim of Chassidus that you have in your possession.

Thank you for what has been done, and a request for the future: if you have any additional letters of substance, please send them here so they may be copied, as this will surely be of benefit to the many, and the merit of the many depends upon it.

Regarding the list of maamarim you sent, I do not have the following:

*Ki Tomru Mah Nochal* **5658**
*Ki Ka’asher HaShamayim HaChadashim* **Rosh Chodesh Elul 5681**
*Shoftim V’Shotrim* **Shoftim 5681**
*Ki Karov Eilecha* **Nitzavim 5681**
*Shir HaMaalos MiMaamakim* **Shabbos Teshuvah 5682**
*K’Nesher Ya’ir Kino* **Ha’azinu 5682**
*B’Sha’ah SheHikdimu* **5684**
*VaYomer... Lech* **5686**
*V’Lo Zachar* **Vayeshev 5688**

I trust that you have fixed times for the study of the Torah of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), in addition to your other shiurim.

As you requested, I mentioned you at the Ohel of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), and a Tzaddik blesses, and Hashem fulfills his blessings in their entirety.

Signing with blessings to you and your family (may they live),

**Menachem Schneerson**

Regarding what you wrote concerning the aforementioned letter about the *Ofanim*, *Seraphim*, and their root in *Tohu* — see the *maamar* entitled *Mi Kamocha* of *Acharon Shel Pesach* 5684, where it is explained at greater length.

## 400

B"H, 28 Menachem Av, 5710

To the esteemed Rabbi, the G-d-fearing man, etc., Moreinu Harav [Name] may he live,

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter of the 2nd of Eikev, in which you ask: since my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, wrote to you that "it is proper to make efforts to travel to the U.S., and may Hashem help you with a proper journey and good settlement," and you write that obtaining the papers is proceeding slowly, and the current situation is uncertain, and you ask for advice on what to do—

In my opinion, it is proper that your heart be confident that the promise of the Tzaddik, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, will be fulfilled, and Hashem will certainly help you with a proper journey to your desired destination in success. This is provided that you remain strong in your bitachon, your emunah, and your hiskashrus to the Tzaddik of the generation, our Nasi, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

Regarding your question concerning the semen analysis: the test is performed by taking the semen from the womb as soon as possible after the act of intimacy, and bringing it as quickly as possible to the place where it is tested. The basis for this permission is found in Responsa Tzemach Tzedek, Even HaEzer, end of section 70, which permits the use of a *moch* (contraceptive) in a place of [need], etc., and all the more so in the aforementioned manner. This is contrary to those who are stringent in this matter (cited in Sdei Chemed, Asifat Dinim, Ma'arechet Ishut, section 1; and in Pe'at HaSadeh there). You should certainly ask the doctor first how to preserve the semen in a way that it will be suitable for testing.

Regarding what you write about the ailments you are experiencing: surely this is nothing more than *marah shechorah* (melancholy). The advice for this is known, based on what is explained in Torah Or by the Alter Rebbe, Parshas Toldos, in the explanation of the *maamar* beginning "Mayim Rabim," that for *marah shechorah*, it is good to utilize it for the purpose of *hasmadah* (diligence) in learning. This is, of course, on the condition that it **does not weaken your health**, G-d forbid. When I am at the Tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, I will read the *pidyonos* mentioned in your letter, and the Tzaddik will certainly bless, and Hashem will fulfill the blessings of the Tzaddik—who is found in this world even more than during his lifetime—for their fulfillment, both physically and spiritually.

I sign with a blessing and await good news from you.

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***400***

From the secretariat copy.

**Semen analysis:** See also above, Letter 606.

**Marah shechorah... hasmadah:** See also above, Letter 672.

## Tisa

B”H, 28 Menachem Av, 5711

To my *mechutan*, the esteemed Chossid, the great Torah scholar, the G-d-fearing man, etc.,
Rabbi Gershon, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Daled Va’eschanan: You need not apologize for what you wrote to me. There was no cause for any *kapida* (displeasure), etc. I am always satisfied to hear what is happening with *anash* (may they live). If the news is good, it brings satisfaction and joy; and even if, Heaven forbid, the opposite is true, the very act of maintaining a connection through letters is strengthening, and it diminishes the feeling of loneliness and low spirits. It is well known what is explained in many books: even if one cannot help in a material sense, a good thought and good wishes from one Jew to another is, in itself, a significant matter.

It is also well known that there is a special quality when one speaks openly in this world about the virtues of his fellow and the needs he lacks; this acts—whether immediately or after some time—to alleviate his situation (see the explanation in the parable of the Rebbe of Paritch, which is brought in *HaTamim*, Vol. 2, and printed in *Kovetz Michtavim*, 1, p. 20).

It is also known that the Baal Shem Tov would strive to cause simple people to speak well of one another, and through this, the *dinim* (judgments) would be sweetened.

I will read your *pidyon nefesh* in the *heichal* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—the place where one enters to submit *pidyonos* and for *yechidus*—as well as at the first opportunity at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

A *tzaddik* blesses, and Hashem fulfills the blessings of the *tzaddik*—who is found in this world even more than during his lifetime—regarding their fulfillment.

Regarding your note on *Likutei Torah*, *Parshas Shelach*, *d.h. Inyan HaNesachim* (p. 42b), where it states: "And likewise for a *niddah* and a *zavah*, their purification is specifically in *mayim chayim* (living waters)." You ask, as this contradicts the *halacha*, which states that a *niddah* and *zavah* do not require *mayim chayim*. You mention that you recall seeing this in Rashi somewhere, though you do not remember the exact location.

You are surely referring to Rashi in *Shabbos* 65b; see the *Tosfos* there. What is written in *Likutei Torah* is based on what is written in the *Zohar*, Vol. 3, p. 97b, which states: "And you shall count for yourselves... 'for yourselves' specifically, just as it is written, 'And she shall count for herself seven days'—'for herself,' for her own sake. So too, 'for yourselves'—for your own sake. And why? In order to be purified in water. There, 'And she shall count for herself seven days'; here, 'seven weeks,' etc., to be purified in the water of that river which flows and issues forth, and is called *mayim chayim*... just as a woman who is pure [immerses] for herself at night, etc." (end quote). This refers to the *niddah*, and the *Zohar* emphasizes that it is called *mayim chayim*. Seemingly, what is the practical difference here? See the *Remez* there regarding the virtue of *mayim chayim* and their level. See also *Tikkunei Zohar*, *Tikkun* 19, near the beginning.

I sign with blessings and regards to the entire group in general, and to your household in particular.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 482

B”H, 3 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Rabbi M. [Teitelbaum], may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 28 Menachem Av, in which you ask regarding the emigration [to the United States]:

It is indeed true, as my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, stated, that since times have changed and circumstances have shifted, each person may inquire again regarding the matter of emigration. Since you ask in your letter—and Rabbi Yitzchak Meir, may he live, also asked on your behalf—he surely informed you of my opinion: if you have the possibility, you should endeavor immediately to obtain the papers to travel to the United States. May Hashem grant that everything should proceed properly and with ease, and that you may arrive at your destination successfully, for "the steps of man are established by Hashem."

As you requested, I will mention you and your family, may they live, when I am at the tziyun of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Surely the tzaddik, who is found in this world now even more than during his lifetime, will bless you, and may Hashem fulfill His blessings—both materially and spiritually—to their fullest measure.

I trust you are also occupied with disseminating the wellsprings outward in an appropriate manner, and I would appreciate it if you would inform me of good news regarding this.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v’chasimah tovah*, and regards to the entire group, may they live.

**Menachem Schneerson**

I have now received the kuntresim you sent: the *maamar* *Kinyan HaChaim*, the *maamar* *Zos Toras HaBayis*, and *Die moralische Bedeutung von Chassidus Chabad*. I trust you will send the rest as well, and I thank you in advance.

## 453

B"H, 3 Elul, 5713

May the mountains bear peace and blessing to the brilliant Gaon, the G-d-fearing man, who sits in judgment, a man of valor and many accomplishments, who leads his congregation with integrity, illuminating and shining, whose speech and insight are excellent, of the holy seed, etc., Rabbi Meir Abuchatzeira, may he live, of the holy community of Midelt, may it be built and established.

After the customary greetings:

It was with pleasure that I heard from our representative, the Chassid and Gaon, a man of many accomplishments in Torah and mitzvos, who encompasses all, who engages in communal needs with great faithfulness, and whose hands are full in strengthening Torah and Judaism, etc., Rabbi Binyamin, may he live, Gardosky, regarding your honor and your activities in disseminating Torah and strengthening Judaism in your holy community and its surroundings, where your influence is widespread. As per your request, which the aforementioned conveyed to me, I mentioned your name, and that of your family, may they live in goodness, while I was at the resting place of the Tzaddik, our Nasi, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. I prayed for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for everything that each one of you requires, according to your respective needs.

Surely the Tzaddik will bless you, for as the Zohar states (Vol. III, 71b), "After he departs, he is found in this world even more than during his lifetime." May Hashem fulfill the blessings of the Tzaddik to their fullest, in both material and spiritual matters.

It is known what is written in the *Iggeres HaKodesh* of the Alter Rebbe (Letter 27) to comfort with double measure the "downtrodden, who sigh and groan" (see *Be'ur HaTorah* by the Tzemach Tzedek, Parshas Shemos, p. 64, regarding the concept of *ne'akah*), that the Tzaddik "leaves life... for all the living, for he is the soul of all the living, who is bound to his soul... in each and every one according to the level of his true connection and his pure, true love."

The book explains the concept of *hishtatchus* (visiting the gravesite), that "even those who did not know or recognize him during his lifetime, but only learned from the holy books that he left behind as a blessing, and who benefit from the light of the radiance of his Torah and are strengthened through this in the *avodah* of Hashem... they, too, are certainly called his students... for they believe in that Tzaddik and receive from him the light of his Torah... the branches are drawn to their root."

My revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, explained in a letter that "the magnitude of the yearning for connection can only be satisfied when one studies the *maamarim* of Chassidus that the Rebbe says and writes, for merely seeing his face is not enough."

And in a second letter, he details: "The questioner asks: 'In what way is there a connection to me, since I do not know him face-to-face?' The true connection is through the study of Torah. When he studies my *maamarim* of Chassidus, reads the *sichos*, connects with the friends of *anash* and the *temimim* students, may they live, in their studies and *farbrengens*, and fulfills my request to recite Tehillim and keep to the study times—this is the connection."

And when we study his Torah and *sichos* and walk in this straight path that he has taught us, then "as water reflects the face, etc., and spirit brings spirit and draws forth spirit, and his spirit stands within us literally... so that even in this world of action, where today is the time to do them, he is found even more." And just as he stood and served here, he stands and serves there as well.

The connection is even greater through participating in the holy work of our Nasi, and I have a strong hope that your honor will give us your assistance in every way possible, in accordance with what you discussed with our representative, the Gaon and Chassid, etc., Rabbi Binyamin, may he live, Gardosky. From this, and from us, the holy and exalted Name will be magnified, *amen, ken yehi ratzon*.

In a separate package, I am sending you the *Tanya* and some of the *kuntreisim* of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. Please be so kind as to acknowledge receipt.

I sign with the blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*, and may your peace and Torah increase. May you merit to lead your congregation on still waters, on the path that ascends to the House of G-d, with tranquility and breadth of mind. I await good tidings, and there is no good other than Torah and mitzvos.

**Menachem Schneerson**

As the Gaon and Chassid, etc., Rabbi Binyamin, may he live, Gardosky, told me, there are several books in his possession regarding the "Torah of Truth" that were authored by your father and grandfather. Surely they contain some of the radiance of our holy Torah, which is "more precious than pearls," etc. I would be very interested in having them in our library, and if you would grant them as a gift to our collection, my gratitude is extended to you in advance.

## 404

[4 Elul 5710]

... Likewise, his letter of the 20th, containing his memoirs, was received some time ago, as well as the aforementioned letters of the 21st and 22nd. He should certainly continue his writings, in accordance with the wish of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

#### 404

Published in the book *LiShma Ozen*, p. 12, and in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 21, p. 559. It is addressed to Moreinu HaRav Shneur Zalman Duchman. Additional letters to him appear above, 414, and in the notes indicated there.

## 5715

5715

B"H, 4 Elul, 5715

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Moreinu HaRav... may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter regarding your son... may he live, who will turn five years old, and whom you are educating to bring into Cheder...

As you requested, I will mention him at the Tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, at the appropriate time.

I send here my blessings that you should merit to raise him for Torah, for the Chuppah, and for good deeds, in a state of abundance. Regarding the statement of the Mishnah, "At five years old, one begins the study of Mikra," and as explained in Likutei Torah (in the explanation of *Lo Tashbit*), that the inner dimension of Torah is included within Mikra (see also the Laws of Torah Study by the Alter Rebbe, Chapter 2, end of section 1): may you raise him for Torah and for the "Light" of the Torah, which is the inner dimension of the Torah. This is the Torah of Chassidus Chabad, which the Nesi'im of Chabad have bequeathed to us, and for us in particular—our Nasi is my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

With blessings for a *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah* for you and for all your family members, may they live.

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### 5715

From the Secretariat copy.

## 456

B.H., 4 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Rabbi Yitzchak Aharon, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

I have received your note with the 20 mezuzos for the standing fund and five for the general fund, and they have been delivered to their intended purpose, with thanks. I am surprised that you limited your letter to two lines, without informing me of what is being done in the area of *hafatzas hamaayanos chutzah* (spreading the wellsprings outward), nor of the activities of the *maayanos* (wellsprings) within.

It is unnecessary to elaborate on the great importance of the standing and support of the institution that my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, valued so highly—to the extent that he himself was involved in finding the necessary means for the work of this institution. I am referring to Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch.

However, it is known that the requirement of the Torah of Chassidus Chabad is that one’s giving and dedication must be with all the powers of the soul: *Chabad, Chagas, Nehim*, and *Mochin*. As is explained in *Tanya*, *tzedakah* is referred to simply as "a mitzvah," because it includes the entire vitality of one's soul. And in *Iggeres HaKodesh*, Epistle 9, it is explained that in the *ikvesa d'meshicha* (the era preceding Moshiach), there is no way to truly cleave to Him and to transform darkness into light, etc., except through the aspect of *asiyah* (action) as well, which is the act of *tzedakah*.

But after all this, a person does not fulfill his obligation with this alone, as is explained in the *Tanya* itself, in Part I, that all the powers of the soul and its "garments" must be filled with Torah and mitzvos.

This is especially true for *talmidei hatmimim*, who are among the elders of *anash* and must serve as an example to others. Consequently, they must be, to the extent possible, a *karcha d’kula bei* (a place that contains everything): if it is *haskalah* (intellectual study) — there must be *haskalah*; if it is *avodah* (divine service) — here is *avodah*; and if it is *maasim b’poel* (practical deeds) — I have *maasim b’poel*.

It is difficult for me to elaborate further, as I know the nature of your soul, and my intention is only to help, not to criticize. But I hope that these lines will suffice.

Surely, there is no need for comment or illumination regarding the matter of *hiskashrus* (connection) to the *Nasi*, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. This *hiskashrus* is achieved through studying his Torah and walking in the paths he taught.

Surely you have set times for studying the Torah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in his *maamarim*, *sichos*, and letters. And how good it would be if this study were done in a group, where one strengthens and awakens the other.

It is understood that this does not exempt one from the other *shiurim* that every individual is obligated to maintain.

Among these are the *shiurim* that are equal for every soul in the *Chok Yomi*: *Chumash* with Rashi, *Tehillim* as divided for the days of the month, and *Tanya* as divided for the days of the year by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

With blessings for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for you and all your household, may they live, and may you use the talent for communal work that you possess to clear the path for the feet of the herald, through the light of the Torah of Chabad.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 547

547

B"H, 8 Elul, 5707

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, learned, and distinguished R' M. [Bistritzky] shlita,

Peace and blessing!

It has been a long time since I have heard from you, and I trust that all is well with you and your entire household.

Enclosed is a letter I have just received from our friend, the great Gaon, the great Chossid, the G-d-fearing, who engages in communal needs with faith, a great scribe, Rabbi Shlomo Yosef Zevin shlita. You have surely heard of him, as he was one of the primary communal activists in Russia, and he is now one of the primary activists in our Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, and the editor of the Talmudic Encyclopedia, etc.

Surely you do not require any prompting regarding the matter mentioned in the enclosed letter, and without a doubt, you will do everything in your power regarding this matter.

I thank you in advance if you would be so kind as to inform me of what you will do in this regard, and also for the trouble of returning the enclosed letter to me after you have made use of it.

I sign with the blessing of a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for you and your entire household.

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***547***

From the secretariat copy.

**R' M.:** Bistritzky.

**Letter... Zevin:** See letter 601 below.

## 708

B"H, 8 Elul, 5708

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 1 Elul, in which you inform me that the Yeshiva administration has arranged for you to study the laws of *shechitah* and you are asking whether to continue with this:

In my opinion, this is the correct path. You should become proficient in the *shechitah* of both poultry and large cattle, and it goes without saying that this includes the study of the laws of *shechitah*. May Hashem grant you success in your studies.

Surely you have established times for studying the Torah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, as found in his *maamarim*, *sichos*, and letters. And how much better it is if this study is done in a group, where one can strengthen and inspire the other.

It is understood that this does not exempt you from the other *shiurim* that every individual is obligated to fulfill.

Among these are the *shiurim* that are incumbent upon everyone as a daily portion: *Chumash* with Rashi, *Tehillim* as divided for the days of the month, and *Tanya* as divided for the days of the year by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. All of these are paths for *hiskashrus* with our *Nasi*, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

With blessings for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

***

From the secretariat copy.

**Shiurim that are incumbent upon everyone:** See above, Letter 689, and the references noted in the footnotes there.

## 559

By the Grace of Hashem, 8 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed and distinguished Chossid, etc., Rabbi R. Sh. Z. (may he live and be well),

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 26 Menachem Av, in which you inform me that your eldest son, Chaim (may he live), has been directed by the Rebbe, my father-in-law, to spend part of his time working in the kindergarten—and you ask whether he should continue in this, as you are distressed by the time he is forced to take away from his studies, and he is not like his peers who are studying in Tomchei Temimim. You add that both you and your son are pained by this, and that your son possesses good faculties, and you ask my opinion as to whether he should nevertheless continue as before, or come to the United States to study in Tomchei Temimim.

In my opinion, at least for the duration of the year until 10 Shevat, the coming year (5711), your son should continue his work in the kindergarten while setting fixed times for Torah study, as the Rebbe, my father-in-law, instructed him, including a special *shiur* in the teachings of our Nasi, the Rebbe, my father-in-law. You should explain to your son, and to yourself as well, that it is in vain to be distressed that part of your son’s time is given to physical work, leaving only a few hours for Torah study.

The statement of our Sages is well known, cited in *Iggeres HaKodesh*, Chapter 22: "A person does not know what he will earn [or: what will be his reward]..." and "When will the kingdom of the House of David return?" The Alter Rebbe concludes that these two are equated. It is known what is written in the *Shelah* that the Torah speaks in the higher realms and hints at the lower realms. This means that even in this statement of our Sages, "A person does not know what he will earn," the intention is also—or primarily—referring to spiritual reward and sustenance. In this, too, they are equated to the matter of the "End" (the coming of Moshiach), regarding which they said, "The heart does not reveal it to the mouth." The explanation given is that this refers also to the "Higher Heart" and the "Higher Mouth," for even to the Higher Mouth the "End" is not revealed. According to the Alter Rebbe, this is likewise the case regarding spiritual reward and sustenance.

And consider this for yourself: Why must one go around with one’s own calculations, thinking about what is better for oneself in spiritual matters—when in spiritual matters, we are fortunate if we even know the reality itself, let alone its essence—at a time when one has a directive from the Nasi of the generation? The Nasi is the "heart of all Israel," which is higher than the "mouth." And furthermore, as is explained in Chassidus, the Nasi—the Rebbe—is the intermediary that connects us with the *Atzmus* and *Mahus* of the *Ein Sof*. Therefore, if he has indicated a place for spiritual sustenance, it is certainly the best thing that could be for the soul and for the health of the body. The health of the soul is attained when it is a vessel to carry out the *kavanah* of the Higher One and the Higher Will.

In this, you will also find a general answer for all of *Anash* (may they live) who are in Australia and do not cease to complain about their lot, as it seems to them that it is a bitter fate to be far from a certain place or from certain people. This is nothing but a tactic of the *yetzer* to confuse them in their *avodah* and prevent them from fulfilling the task entrusted to them by the Rebbe, my father-in-law. He is the "general soul," and according to what is explained in *Toras HaChassidus* regarding the level of the "general" that is above the "particulars," this applies even as they are included within the general.

The saying of the Rebbe (Rashab) is well known: "Temimim are candles to illuminate." The implication of this statement is that this is their essence. From this we learn several things, two of which are: a) One who lights a candle from a candle loses nothing from the candle from which it was lit; b) Since the function of this candle is to illuminate, and it fulfills its function by illuminating, this is the greatest elevation possible for a created being—to fulfill the command of the Creator, through which it connects with Him. This is the meaning of *mitzvah*: *tzavta* (connection) and *chibur* (joining), as is explained in *Toras HaChassidus* in many places.

It is quite understandable, the loneliness one feels in Australia, especially when remembering the environment they were in when they were all together in a large congregation, like the sun shining at noon—for then, "what use is a candle in the daylight?" But it seems this was only a matter of preparation, in order to gather the strength to illuminate the darkness and make their current location and country a place of light—this is Torah, and specifically with the "light" within it, which is the teaching of *Toras HaChassidus*, that the *maayanos* (fountains) shall spread outward to all corners of the world. Then we are promised that the kingdom of the House of David will return, as is explained at length in the *sicha* of Simchas Torah 5690. Consequently, this is the greatest reward that can be, both in spiritual and in physical matters.

And as the aforementioned statement of the Alter Rebbe says, the reward and the return of the kingdom of the House of David are equated to one another.

I sign with the blessing of *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah* for you, for your son, for your entire household, and for the entire group. Please convey the content of this letter to the other *Anash* who complain about the task entrusted to them by the Rebbe, my father-in-law. Thank you in advance for your trouble.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 510

B"H, 8 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, the learned Rabbi, G-d-fearing, etc., R' Dovid Moshe, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

Your two letters, from the 1st of Parshas Re'eh and the 2nd of Rosh Chodesh Elul, along with their enclosures, have been received. It is regrettable that you continue to be so involved in the entry into the Rabbinical Center and in facilitating the matters that lead to this. Once again, I urge you to expedite this as much as possible, and I am certain you will share good news regarding the results of your efforts in this matter.

I was pleased to read in your letter that your situation in the shul has improved. You are surely utilizing the days of the month of Elul, and especially the upcoming month of Tishrei, to solidify the idea that you are indeed the Rabbi of the shul, through appropriate speeches, etc., etc.

Thank you for your trouble in sending me the letters of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, which were in the possession of the esteemed R' Moshe Hakohen Sheyevitz. After copies are made, the originals will, of course, be returned to him. I will surely write him a special letter of thanks.

I was pleased to hear that Mr. Shmuel Katz has already been to the shul. Thank you for your trouble in forwarding his donation to the Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch; we are sending him a receipt to his address, along with a letter, a copy of which is enclosed.

You did well to accept the offer of [name] to officiate at the wedding of his son. Since there are still a few months until the wedding, you should look for ways to speak—either yourself or through his acquaintances—to the hearts of the groom and the bride regarding the laws of Taharas Hamishpacha. This affects them and the health of their descendants until the end of all generations, both physically and spiritually. You should surely begin gently, for the words of the wise are heard when spoken gently.

Awaiting good news from you, and signing with a blessing for a *Kesiva V'Chasima Tova* for success in your holy work as a Rabbi and leader of your congregation, to which you were appointed by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and may you be a fitting vessel for this,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 1911

B"H, 9 Elul, 5711

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., [Name], may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 20 Menachem Av, in which you inform me that you and your wife, may she live, have decided to join the group traveling to our Holy Land, may it be rebuilt and established, and you add that if a position in mathematics should become available to you, you feel you should not decline it, and you request a blessing for this:

May it be the will of Hashem that you be a vessel for the blessings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and may the journey proceed in order and may you arrive at your destination successfully. "From Hashem are the steps of man established, and He delights in his way."

The saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, is well known; he said to one of those traveling to Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established: "You must make Eretz Yisrael into an *Eretz HaKodesh* (a Holy Land)." We are not privy to the secret of the *birurim* (refinements), and it is known from our Sages that "a person does not know in what he will profit," which refers also to spiritual sustenance. However, on the face of it, to make Eretz Yisrael into an *Eretz HaKodesh* by means of being a mathematics teacher is a very long road, and Mashiach Tzidkeinu is already standing behind our wall, waiting for the completion of the *birurim* of the *ikvesa d'Meshicha* (the footsteps of Mashiach).

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*, and for a journey to your destination that is proper, for you and your wife, may she live,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***1911***

From the secretariat copy.

**Saying:** See also the letter below, 1936.

## 412

B"H, 9 Elul, 5710

Mrs. ... may she live,

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter, where you mention that doctors maintain that you need an operation and you are requesting a blessing:

You are surely fulfilling the custom of Israel to give to tzedakah in the pushka of Rabbeinu Meir Baal HaNes before licht bentchen.

Presumably, the doctors you have consulted are specialists, and this is the opinion of at least two of them.

If this is the case, may the Ribono shel Olam help that the operation should proceed as it ought to, and may you be able to report good news that you have been blessed with healthy and enduring offspring.

With blessings for a complete recovery and a *ksiva v’chasima tova*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

I would be interested to know about the composition of your family, may they live, and what they are occupied with.

#### ***412***

From the secretariat’s copy.

## 513

B”H, 9 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing, and distinguished [name omitted]...

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 26 Menachem Av, with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed, I will, as you requested, read the *pidyon nefesh* at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

I am surprised that you have not yet replied to my question regarding whether you have set times for Torah study in general, and for the teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in particular. It is a well-known saying of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, that when there are "trouble-Chassidim"—meaning, when some unfortunate event occurs, *chas v’shalom*, they remember the Rebbe and Chassidus and write a *pidyon*—but when there is relief, and "each man sits under his vine and under his fig tree," etc.

As you are someone who has merited, in a way that transcends nature, to see the holy countenance of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, I do not understand why it is necessary to prompt you regarding the necessity of studying his teachings. And even after being prompted, you still deliberate whether to accept the suggestion or whether you have more important matters than that.

It is simple to understand that I do not intend to stir up anything that is the opposite of mercy, *chas v’shalom*. However, I am doubly astonished by those who have the choice of being "Polish-style Chassidim," "trouble-Chassidim," or "women-style Chassidim," versus being Chassidim in the sense that all the *Nesi’im* of Chabad Chassidus have demanded of us—those who have tasted the sweetness of "the light that is good." Yet, despite this, one first becomes a *baal habayis*, then becomes "busy," and then says that a single line of Chassidus is enough, and that from him, no more should be demanded.

I am certain you will share good news. It is known from our Sages in *Kiddushin* that true goodness is when it is not only good for people, but also good for *Hashem*—for one’s own self is also included among "people"—and there is no "good" other than Torah and *mitzvos*, as explained in the "light of Torah," which returns one to the good, regardless of the standing or situation in which one finds oneself.

Signing with the blessing of *Kesivah v’Chasimah Tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

How is your family, *sheyichyu*?

## 1954

B"H, 9 Elul, 1954

To the esteemed avreich, the chossid, etc., [Name] shlita,

Shalom u’vracha!

In response to your letter, noting that today you have completed twenty-two years—may you have long, good years ahead:

May it be Hashem’s will that you be a fitting and expansive vessel for the drawing down and reception of the blessings of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in a revealed manner.

Surely you have seen the excerpt in *Hayom Yom* for 11 Nissan regarding the conduct for one’s birthday.

It is appropriate that you study during the day the *maamar* of Chai Elul 1949 and the *sicha* of Chai Elul found in *Sefer HaSichos*.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***1954***

From the secretariat’s copy.

## 5715

5715

B”H, 11 Elul, 5715

To the esteemed and distinguished, etc., may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of Friday, Erev Shabbos Parshas Re’eh, I was pleased to read of the *kvi’as itim* for Torah study that you have established, both in *nigleh* and in *Chassidus*. Surely you will remain steadfast in this, for these are the broad vessels for drawing down and receiving the influence of all good, both materially and spiritually.

You mention in your letter that your younger brother is currently in Eretz Yisrael, may it be rebuilt and established. It would be appropriate for you to suggest that he connect there with *anash*, may they live, who are in Yerushalayim, may it be rebuilt and established. In a manner appropriate to his spirit, explain to him the great necessity—especially in these generations, the *ikvesa d’Meshicha*—to study the teachings of *Chassidus*, which is the *ilana d’chayei* (tree of life), as the *Zohar* promises: "Through it, Yisrael will emerge from exile with kindness and mercy." Surely, with the proper effort, you will succeed in having your brother, may he live, begin this study.

Thank you for the news regarding your relative, may he live, who has become engaged in a good and auspicious hour to a suitable match.

May it be Hashem’s will that the wedding take place at the appropriate time and that they build a home in Yisrael upon the foundations of Torah and *mitzvos*.

At the first opportunity, I will mention him at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, as you requested, for a suitable match, and surely you will share good news regarding this.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v’Chasimah Tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***5715***

From the Secretariat’s copy.

## 5716

11 Elul, 5716

To the Tamim, the G-d-fearing, etc., [Name]... may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

I was distressed to hear that you are not in the best of health, and some say that from time to time you have been feeling down. This was a great wonder to me, for it is well-known, and explained in many places in the teachings of Chassidus, that a Chassid must feel the truth—(which is, in truth, the case for all created beings; the distinction of Chassidim, and especially Tamimim, is that they must **feel** this as well, not merely understand it intellectually).

This is the paradox that exists within every created being: on one hand—from the perspective of its own self—it is "not truly real," but on the other hand—from the perspective of the fact that it is a creation of the Essence and Being of the Ein Sof, blessed be He, which is its entire essence—the created being is also "all-powerful."

If for ordinary Jews and Chassidim this is difficult to feel, it must nonetheless be a matter of absolute simplicity for Tamimim who are connected to their Nasi, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

For each of them must surely know and feel that "a Tzaddik who has departed is found in all worlds"—and as the Alter Rebbe adds: "even in this physical world—more than during his lifetime," for the limitations of place have been removed. Consequently, everyone must feel that the Rebbe is present with him.

As for dwelling on one's own standing and situation, there are special times when one should do so, but the rest of the time it is better to think about the Rebbe—how he is always with his connected followers and how he guides them at every single step.

This thought alone, even without specific *hisbonenus*, should effect a strengthening of all the powers of the soul to utilize them in accordance with the will of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

And if any extraneous thoughts contrary to the above should enter, one must know that it is a tactic of the *yetzer*, which seeks various ways to distract one from Torah and *avodah*.

The conclusion from this is that you must be strong in your trust in the blessings of the Tzaddik, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, who stands over you and guides you on the straight path. Provided that you do not become discouraged, and you recite Chapter 71 of Tehillim every day—and the main thing is that it should be absolutely settled in your mind that the Rebbe is with you, and you can rely on him that everything will be good. For the Essence and Being of the Ein Sof, blessed be He, is the ultimate good, and the Rebbe is the intermediary who connects us with the Essence and Being of the Ein Sof, blessed be He, and carries out the will that everything should be good—and ultimately, that it should be in a way that is good, visibly and openly...

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*, awaiting good news,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 5717

5717

B"H, 11 Elul, 5717

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R' Yisroel Noach, may he live;
R' Peretz, may he live;
And R' Nissan, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter from the 25th of Menachem Av, regarding... and asking for my opinion:

In my opinion, you should all gather together on one of the days during this week or next week. Beforehand, you should study—whether each of you individually or all together—from the Chassidic teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and recite his Kapital. Then, you should consult together regarding the matters of... may he live. Surely, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, will ensure that you arrive at the correct decision, as is known from the story of the Tzemach Tzedek with R' Aharon Belinitzer.

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*,

**M. Schneerson**

This letter of mine was sent to the address that was on the envelope.

## 1958

1958

B"H, 11 Elul, 5718

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., R' Yosef, may he live:

Greetings and blessings!

... Thank you for informing me that the *Roshei Yeshiva* and *Mashgichim* at Tomchei Temimim have established fixed times for studying the Torah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. It is surprising, however, that they do not also have fixed times on *Shabbos Kodesh*, from which all the days of the week are blessed, both in spirituality and in physicality.

With blessings for a *Kesiva V'chasima Tova*,
