#### 1958

From the Secretariat copy.

**R' Yosef:** Goldberg, Rosh Yeshiva of Tomchei Temimim in Brunoy, France.

## 519

B"H, 11 Elul, 5719

Peace and blessing!

Regarding your question about people giving me *pidyonos* to be read at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory: Firstly, this question should be asked of those who give them, not of me. Secondly, this also occurred during the lifetime of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, in this world, when *pidyonos* were submitted to him. Thirdly, regarding your concern that by doing this I am exempting the giver from thoughts of *teshuvah* and shedding tears—this has never crossed my mind. In fact, I often explain this clearly to the giver. Sometimes, I link the reading of the *pidyon* to a commitment from the giver to engage in some matter of Torah and *mitzvos*. Thank G-d, I have already seen actual fruits from many who have fulfilled and continue to fulfill their commitments.

...In all the letters you write, I have not seen any mention of your involvement in influencing others to draw them closer to the teachings of Chassidus and to a connection with our *Nasi*, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. This is comparable to a king who assigned a specific task to one of his servants, yet the servant busies himself with a different task, which surely causes him to diminish the time and attention he devotes to fulfilling the task the king assigned him.

My father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, said that the *Temimim* are "candles to illuminate." This means that their specific task, which their Rebbe assigned to them—and even according to the *nigleh* aspect of Torah, "whoever teaches his fellow’s son Torah, Scripture considers it as if he birthed him," and especially in the case of a *Chossid* and a Rebbe, where it is not merely "as if" in a figurative sense, but he truly birthed him, and the Rebbe said that his children must be "candles to illuminate"—simply put: wherever he finds himself, he must see to it that it becomes illuminated. For this is the entire essence and purpose of his soul, for which it descended from the highest heights to the deepest depths...

There are thousands of Jews who know nothing about Chassidus or about the Rebbe, and those who do know, do not know as they ought to know. Yet, a Jew from the older *Temimim*, who possesses so many stories and traditions and matters of Chassidus, remains within his own four cubits and thinks to himself, "What do I have to do with them?" And if the Rebbe, of blessed memory, said that he must be a "candle to illuminate," he will put that off for another time.

Now is the month of Elul, and just as throughout the year I do not like to ask difficult questions, all the more so do I not wish to be lengthy now in the month of Elul.

Nevertheless, I would suggest, with a request for your forgiveness, that you establish a fixed time each day for the teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and a fixed time a few times a week to study these with others. You should seek ways, with appropriate effort, to draw Jews closer to Torah and *mitzvos* in general, and to the teachings of Chassidus of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in particular. And if you involve yourself in this, I calculate that the Rebbe will help you, and you will be successful in carrying out the will of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 5720

5720

By the Grace of Hashem, 11 Elul, 5720

To the esteemed, G-d-fearing man, who conducts his business with integrity, etc., R’ Y. HaKohen, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Rosh Chodesh Elul, along with the enclosure, I hereby acknowledge receipt of the check for the printing of the Kuntres for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah. One mitzvah leads to another; may you go from strength to strength in the performance of tzedakah, according to your current means, and even more so.

For with the Ribono shel Olam, there are two paths: One path is that He first gives money, and then sees how much one sets aside from it for tzedakah, proportionally. The second path is that one gives to tzedakah more than one thinks one can afford, and the Ribono shel Olam does not remain a debtor; He subsequently provides, calculated in many multiples if one has given maaser, or with abundance in many multiples if one has given a chomesh. Consequently, it turns out that for every dollar one gives beyond what **appears** to be within one’s means, the Ribono shel Olam gives, in addition to that dollar, many more, as is well known in many seforim.

Therefore, reading in your letter that you do not want to miss the opportunity to give a little tzedakah, especially in the month of Elul, and seeing the amount of your check, I am surprised that you have "pity" on the Ribono shel Olam. Why does it bother you that your check might be such that the Ribono shel Olam would grant you income and parnassah incomparably greater than what you estimated for yourself with the tzedakah of this check?

I hope you will not take offense, and that you will also remember that the Rebbe always wanted to make you a wealthy man. One of the ways to achieve this is *aser te'aser*—giving tzedakah as **the Rebbe wanted.** When one does not bargain, the Ribono shel Olam does not bargain either, as explained in Iggeres HaKodesh, Epistle 13. Even one who gives a chomesh is still within the category of *tzimtzum* and limitation in the avodah of Hashem. However, one who acts from the *midah* of *chesed* and *hispatshut* (expansion) in the avodah of Hashem (which is especially relevant to Kohanim, for a Kohen is a man of *chesed*), and there is no restraint to the spirit of his generosity, whether in tzedakah or in Torah study, etc.—then, the Alter Rebbe concludes, the Ribono shel Olam conducts Himself with him also with His great *chesed* and without limit or end.

I would be pleased to hear good news regarding what is happening in your camp in general, and also what is going on with your brothers, may they live. I hope to hear from you that there is a revival in your business. I am also interested to know what is happening regarding your case and the proposal concerning a building.

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*, awaiting good news,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 5721

5721

B"H, 12 Elul, 5721

The brilliant Rabbi and Chassid, G-d-fearing and distinguished, etc.,
Rabbi S.Y. Zevin, may he live

Peace and blessing!

I received your letter dated Thursday, 27 Menachem Av, regarding the request for *hachnasas kallah*. I immediately spoke with the G-d-fearing and distinguished, etc., Mr. M. Bistritzky. His response is that, apparently, there was a misunderstanding regarding this matter, as he had no dealings with the Poles. However, since he had heard about Rabbi Z.K., and believes he may have even seen him once or twice in Belgium, he volunteered two payments of 18 dollars, and the check is in my possession.

Now, since I do not know the details of the situation, I have not yet cashed the check. Please be so kind as to inform me if you would like me to send you the money and, if so, in what manner, or if I should return the check to the donor.

You need not have apologized for troubling me with this request. Rest assured, once and for all, that I am happy for an opportunity such as this to do a favor for anyone, and especially [for one such as you], etc. May the Ribono shel Olam grant me the merit to be able to easily accomplish what is requested of me.

Enclosed is a *kuntres* for Chai Elul, which has just been published, along with a copy of a letter written to him.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v'chasimah tovah*, and good health for your honor and for your wife, may she live, including this, of course,

**Menachem Schneerson**

B"H,

I hereby join those who are performing the mitzvah of participating in the special campaign for "Beis Rivkah" to clear its debts. It is my hope that all of anash—may they live—will do the same, for an institution founded by my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe—of blessed memory—is precious in his eyes. He will surely arouse mercy upon them and their families—may they live—that they be blessed with everything they need, in both material and spiritual matters.

**M. Schneerson**

## 5723

5723

B"H, 12 Elul, 5723

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Rabbi Saadia, may he live,
Shaliach

Peace and Blessing!

In response to your letter from the 2nd of Rosh Chodesh Elul, enclosing the list of contributors: 1) for the construction of the Ohel HaKodosh, 2) for the needs of the camp and Kehot, 3) for the payment of debts.

Thank you for your efforts in all of this. A mitzvah leads to another mitzvah; may you increase your strength in your efforts regarding all of the above. Explain to all the contributors, regarding this and similar matters, that this giving signifies and constitutes a reception in broader vessels for the blessings of all good, both materially and spiritually, which our Nasi, my sainted father-in-law, the Rebbe, may his merit shield us—who is present in this world more than during his lifetime—has bestowed, is bestowing, and will bestow upon them. And as all limitations have been removed, the concealments and hiddenness will also continue to diminish, until the light that is good is revealed in tangible sight.

See the *kuntres* enclosed herewith, which is the *kuntres* for the upcoming 18th of Elul, regarding the matter of seeing in a tangible way, for that also encompasses the physicality of both the one who sees and the one who is seen.

It is known in the *maamarim* of Chassidus that discuss the *Levush Malchus* and their explanations regarding the essence and being of the Ein Sof, B"H, which is present here in physicality.

Regarding the matters of Morocco, you will surely begin by submitting all the papers necessary to obtain the entry permit. May it be His will that everything should be with success: the obtaining of the papers, the journey itself, and most importantly—the *avodah* there on the spot, that it should be in accordance with the will of my sainted father-in-law, the Rebbe, may his merit shield us, and to complete the supreme intent for each and every one of us in our respective areas.

Thank you for informing me of the fixed times for study at the shul at 25 and 15 Rue des Rosiers. Likewise regarding the studies in the gatherings of Anash; surely there is in every place, according to its nature, also a fixed time for the study of the Torah of my sainted father-in-law, the Rebbe, may his merit shield us.

With the blessing of *Kesivah V'Chasimah Tovah* for you, for all your family members, may they live, and for the entire group, may they live.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 5724

5724

B"H, 12 Elul, 5724

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., R’... may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from the first day of Rosh Chodesh Elul, as you requested, I will mention you and your family, may they live, at the Ohel and in the sanctuary of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for each and every one according to their needs.

Regarding your question concerning parnassah: certainly, one must make natural vessels, as explained in Sefer HaMitzvos of the Tzemach Tzedek (page 106a) and in Kuntres U’Maayan (Maamar 17). When one makes vessels in the natural way, Hashem will grant His blessing for what is needed for you and your family, may they live.

As for what you write regarding the etrog trade, may it be His will that if you engage in this, it should be with success; however, this is merely a temporary business, and you must search for a permanent source of parnassah, for "you have labored—and you have found."

Surely you have fixed times for studying the Torah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, which is a vessel for drawing down and receiving his blessings in both material and spiritual matters.

With blessings for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***5724***

From the secretariat copy.

## 255

255

B"H, 12 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing and refined, R' Shlomo, may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letters of the 4th and 5th of Elul, in which you inform me that you have set the date of the wedding for the 18th of Elul, may it come upon us for good:

May it be Hashem’s will that your wedding take place at a propitious and successful time, that the union be a beautiful one, and that you build an eternal edifice upon the foundations of Torah and mitzvos as they are explained in the teachings of Chassidus, and may you be blessed with a righteous generation.

With blessings of Mazal Tov, Mazal Tov to you and to your bride, Mrs. Pessia, may she live.

And with blessings of *Kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**M. Schneerson**

Regarding your question concerning the wedding ring: The custom of the Rebbe’s household is to use a gold ring, and this is also how I have seen *anash* and the Chassidim conduct themselves.

It is worth noting what is written in the sefer *Taamei HaMinhagim*, quoting the sefer *Chupas Chasanim*, that the ring is placed on the finger because in the Psalm [19:6], "And he is like a bridegroom..." [followed by] "more to be desired are they than gold," the word "gold" (*mi-zahav*) corresponds to the finger (*etzba*); see there.

426

## 227

B"H, 12 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed and G-d-fearing, etc., R' Dovid, may he live:

Shalom and blessing!

The esteemed Chossid, the G-d-fearing, etc., Rabbi Yisroel Jacobson, has passed on your names to me, as well as the two checks for *Maamad* and for the Yeshiva. The receipts for them have been given to Rabbi Y.D. [Jacobson].

As you requested, I will mention the names that were submitted at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, at the appropriate time, and he will surely bestow his blessing. I hope that you and your family, may they live, will be proper *keilim* to receive the Rebbe’s blessings in both material and spiritual matters. One of the ways to prepare these *keilim* is by observing the *shiurim* that the Rebbe, of blessed memory, instituted: every day, a *parsha* of *Chumash* with *Rashi* from the weekly *sedra*; *Tehillim* after *Shacharis* as divided for the days of the month; and *Tanya* as the Rebbe, of blessed memory, divided it for the days of the year.

I assume that you will certainly fulfill these *shiurim*, and I would be pleased if you would inform me of this.

Presumably, you give *tzedakah* from time to time—and not only in material terms, but also in spiritual terms; that is, to see to it that you influence your acquaintances to bring them closer to *Torah*, *mitzvos*, and *yiras shamayim*. When one truly desires this, the *Ribono shel Olam* helps one find the right words and ways to exert a positive influence upon the other. This also provides *chizuk* to oneself, as one becomes stronger personally, and *chizuk* in the study of *Torah* and the fulfillment of *mitzvos* also comes to the household—which is the will of our *Nasi*, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v'Chasimah Tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 5708

B"H,

... The significance of the day of 18 Elul has been explained in the sichos of my sainted father-in-law, the Rebbe, as the birthday of the Baal Shem Tov (in the year 5458), the day his holy teacher and mentor, Achiyah HaShiloni, revealed himself to him (in the year 5484), the day of the Baal Shem Tov’s own revelation (in the year 5494), the birthday of the Alter Rebbe (in the year 5505), and the day that marks the "beginning of the avodah for the coming new year."

Regarding the third day of Selichos, my sainted father-in-law, the Rebbe, related that this day "is the day designated and set aside for the glory of the Alter Rebbe for all generations, to be recited only before his chosen son, the Mitteler Rebbe. When my grandfather, the holy Tzemach Tzedek, was about ten years old, he also recited it before him—a maamar Chassidus in private—and he instructed him that no one should know of this. My grandfather, the holy Tzemach Tzedek, would recite a maamar of the Alter Rebbe on the third day of Selichos before my grandfather, the holy Maharash, and he instructed him that no one should know of this. My grandfather, the holy Maharash, would recite a drush on the third day of Selichos before my father, the holy Rebbe. And on the third day of Selichos 5655, my father, the holy Rebbe, saw fit to recite this drush before me, and he instructed me regarding the secret until the time he would command that it be revealed."

* * *

Regarding the avodah of the days from 18 Elul and onward, my sainted father-in-law, the Rebbe, explained that "the month of Elul is the time for the revelations of 'I am to my beloved and my beloved is to me.' The preparation for these revelations corresponds to the essence and nature of the revelation... Teshuvah and a broken heart during the twelve days from 18 Elul until Rosh Hashanah.

The first night of Rosh Hashanah is the essential revelation of 'I am to my beloved and my beloved is to me,' with a mesiras nefesh and kabbalas ol following the preparation. These twelve days must be a cheshbon hanefesh for the twelve months of the year—a day for each month. 18 Elul is the day for the cheshbon hanefesh of the preceding month of Tishrei. Erev Rosh Hashanah is the day set aside for the cheshbon hanefesh of the month of Elul, which is the final preparation for the avodah of Rosh Hashanah, to receive a kesivah v'chasimah tovah for the study of Torah, the fulfillment of mitzvos, and avodah shebalev, with a good and sweet year in material matters."

* * *

For many years, the matter of 18 Elul and the matter of the third day of Selichos were hidden, but now permission has been granted to reveal them, and my sainted father-in-law, the Rebbe, publicized them to the ends of the earth. Even though, on the face of it, one might ask, "Are we worthy of this generation?"—the very fact that we are in such a generation is the reason. Because of the concealments and darkness of the footsteps of Moshiach, which have grown so intense, "they are opening the treasures of precious wealth that had been hidden and sealed from the eyes of all who see" until now.

And especially now, after his passing—each and every one of us must strengthen ourselves and connect to the *ilana d'chayei* (Tree of Life) and to our Nasi, my sainted father-in-law, the Rebbe, with greater intensity and strength.

And with the help of the treasures he opened and entrusted to us—to remove the concealments and darkness—this is also the preparation and readiness to receive a kesivah v'chasimah tovah.

**Menachem Schneerson**

Erev Shabbos Kodesh, 13 Elul, 5708
Brooklyn, N.Y.

## 629

B"H, Erev Shabbos Kodesh, 13 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, etc., Moreinu Harav Y. [may he live and be well],

Greetings and blessings!

Enclosed is the Kuntres for the upcoming 18 Elul. I am certain that you will merit to share it with the many, to bring vitality to yourself and to them, in accordance with the two adages regarding the day of 18 Elul: 1) To bring a *chayus* into Elul, 2) To give a *chayus* in the *avodah* of "I am to my beloved and my beloved is to me."

For this is the day when the luminaries were suspended: the birthday of the Baal Shem Tov and the birthday of the Alter Rebbe.

They introduced an internal *chayus* into the *avodah* of "I am to my beloved and my beloved is to me," and they sacrificed themselves so that both those who are learned in Torah and simple people alike would have an *hemshech* of internal *chayus* and be able to live with internal *chayus*.

My revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, explained (copied at length in the introduction to the Kuntres) that from 18 Elul, the special preparation for Rosh Hashanah begins. He adds in another place*, that this is also when the three months of preparation begin for the Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus on the holy day of 19 Kislev,

The founders of which are: the Baal Shem Tov — founder of the general teaching of Chassidus; the Alter Rebbe — founder of the teaching of Chabad Chassidus,

And its bearers and masters are the Nesi'im who fill their place, up to and including my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

On this bright day, the day of 18 Elul, everyone to whom the words of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and his explanation of the significance of this day have reached, will surely begin, with internal *chayus*, to conduct a *cheshbon hanefesh*—a true accounting of the soul—regarding everything that has passed for him or her during the previous year, with regret for the past and a resolution for the future, and a good preparation for the general Rosh Hashanah and the Rosh Hashanah of Chassidus,

All of which is, according to the words of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, a preparation to receive a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* in both material and spiritual matters.

With wishes for success in your *avodah* to bring merit to the many, and with blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*,

**M. Schneerson**

***

Regarding your question as to whether to daven at daybreak when one has been awake all night, or to sleep a little beforehand so that one can prepare to meet his G-d—it depends on how much one can strengthen himself so that sleep does not overcome him. I have not heard a specific instruction on this, but in my opinion, since there is a requirement of *kavanah* in *tefillah*, the absence of which invalidates the *tefillah*, it is **generally** better to adopt the second approach, especially for Chassidim and Anash who have heard or studied the subject of *tefillah*.

***

I was alarmed to hear that you are considering attending some college or the like.

It is my hope and my firm belief that you will abandon this idea entirely and put my mind at ease with good news regarding this.

## 730

B"H, 14 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., [Name], may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I received your letters along with the *pidyon nefesh* enclosed within them. I read the *pidyon nefesh* in the chamber of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, where one enters to submit *pidyonos* and for *yechidus*. I will also read them at the appropriate time at the *tziyon*. A *tzaddik* who has departed is found in this world more than during his lifetime, and he will bestow his blessing, and Hashem will fulfill it completely, both physically and spiritually.

Regarding what you write about several boys and girls—you should certainly provide their names and their mothers' names—whom you are encouraging to continue their studies in the *yeshivah*: You must explain, calmly and gently, to the parents that a kosher education does not mean that one is making the boys and girls into *rabbonim* and *rabbaniyos*. Rather, in our current times, it is a literal rescue from assimilation, *chas v'shalom*, among the nations. Regardless of the parents' current standing or situation, it is clear that both the mother and the father desire that their children remain Jews. In order to have a strong assurance that in ten or twenty years the sons and daughters will still be **connected** to Yiddishkeit, one must pour into them everything possible of Yiddishkeit, and both the mother and the father must pray to the *Ribono shel Olam* that, regardless of all the *nisyonos* that children face, they should remain Jews—and good Jews—both *bein adam laMakom*, *bein adam l'aviv v'l'imo* (which is certainly relevant to parents), and *bein adam l'chaveiro*.

Regarding what you write about the skin irritation that arose on your left hand, on the outer side of the palm: I hope that by now you have already been healed. Whatever it was, it was surely caused by some emotional agitation. Once you divert your mind from these two things, the irritation will surely pass entirely. In any case, it is worthwhile—especially now, in the month of Elul—to have your *tefillin* checked, particularly the *tefillin* of the hand, as well as the length of the straps and especially their width.

I trust that you arrange, from time to time, a *farbrengen* between yourself and the others who work with you in the *yeshivah*—a *farbrengen* as the Rebbe wanted and wants now: with love of peers, with a strengthening of Torah and *mitzvos* in general, and with *ahavas Yisrael*, *ahavas haTorah*, and *ahavas Hashem* in particular. Please, I urge you to inform me of good news regarding this.

Enclosed is the *kuntres* for 18 Elul, which has just been published. I am certain you will use it to benefit the many in the most appropriate manner.

With regards to all members of your household, may they live, each one individually, and with a blessing for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 371

B"H, 16 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, learned, etc., Rabbi A., may he live.

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 7 Elul:

1) Regarding what you wrote concerning the form of the inverted *yud* written in the *tzadik*, and your request for a halachic clarification on this matter: Many have already delved into this and gathered the various opinions. See *Mishnas Avraham*, section 23, subsections 86-88; *Meir Einei Sofrim*, section 67; *Me'asef Lechol Hamachanos*, section 36; and refer to them there.

2) Regarding the practical application for *tefillin*: I am astonished as to who exists in our generation, this orphaned generation, who would dare to disagree in practice with the AriZal, who ruled that one must write *tefillin* with an inverted *yud*. I can call upon faithful witnesses that this is indeed the opinion of the AriZal, namely: *Mishnas Chassidim*, the *Sefer Tikkun Tefillin*, Chapter 1, the *Mitzat Shmurim*, etc., and last but not least, the Alter Rebbe. These words of the AriZal have been cited as halacha by many *poskim* and in the *Shulchan Aruch* of the Alter Rebbe, section 36.

3) Regarding the practical application for a **Sefer Torah**: The aforementioned ruling regarding *tefillin* proves that the form of the letter *tzadik* requires an inverted *yud*. Since no change to this is mentioned in the *Shas*, then according to the well-known *Shu"t Noda B'Yehuda*, it is obvious that such a *tzadik* is kosher in every place, and a *Sefer Torah* is included in this.

4) As to how one should write **l'chatchilah** in a *Sefer Torah*: You have already copied in your letter a letter from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, stating that our custom is to write in a *Sefer Torah* with an inverted *yud* as well. See *Mishnas Avraham* (loc. cit.), where he notes that he saw this in the *Sifrei Torah* of great and expert scribes from many generations, and in the *Sifrei Torah* of the experts of his own generation; only those who have recently arrived are the ones who change it, etc. (This is contrary to the opinion of *Shu"t Hashiv Moshe*, Yoreh Deah, section 51, which holds that *l'chatchilah* one should write in a *Sefer Torah* with a straight *yud*.)

5) Regarding the opinion of the Gra on this matter: You quote in your letter what is written in the book *Tosefet Maaseh Rav*, that the Gra disqualified "the *tzadikim* that are bent and curved at their backs, and he said that an error occurred in the writings of the AriZal."

Now, I do not have the aforementioned book. But where is an "inverted *yud*" hinted at in that language? However, in the book *Kasuv L'Chaim* by the scribe R. Avraham Chaim of Minsk (Vilna, 1858), he writes that he heard from Rabbi R. Elazar Moshe of Monastyrshchina, in the name of the Gra, that the version in the *Zohar Chadash* (2b) is *l'astachra* (to turn around) and not *l'achorei* (to the back). The author concludes that we hold according to the opinion of the *Beis Shlomo*, the *Beis Yosef*, and the Gra, of blessed memory, for halacha l'maaseh.

Of course, it is a very weak foundation to determine based on this that the opinion of the Gra is the opposite of the writings of the AriZal—especially since the *tikkun* (correction) in the *Zohar* there does not hold up well at all, for the *Zohar* continues there: "And the faces are not turned face-to-face... it is destined to saw for you and to make for you face-to-face," etc. From all this, it is proven that beforehand it is the opposite of "faces," which is "back," and not *l'astachra*.

6) The form of the *tzadik* is mentioned in *Chassidus*, as I have noted in *Likutei Torah*, at the beginning of the *biur* on *Hechalotzu* (where the language of the *Zohar* "to the back" is also cited), and in the *maamar* "Netah Es Yadecha" of 5663.

7) Thank you for your comment regarding the *Shabbos* blessings. Several Chassidim from Poland have already informed me that in every place in Galicia, etc., they call it by this name.

8) Likewise, thank you for your comment regarding the arrangement of the place in the *beis medrash* and the like, so that anyone who wishes can find *seforim* of *Chassidus* there to study, at least on the spot. If everyone on the spot would choose by majority opinion a specific place and inform me of it, we would send there from all our publications.

9) Enclosed is the *Kuntres* for 18 Elul, which has just been published, along with a letter to Levi for him.

10) As you requested, I will mention you and your household, may they live, at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* and all good things.

I sign with the blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*,

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

Of course, we are limited regarding the letter from Riga. I have no information from his uncle, R. M. A., may he live; the address of his *shul* there is: [...]

## 5732

16 Elul, 5732

Greetings and Blessings,

I received your two letters, neither of which was dated, in due time. Due to a heavy workload, my response has been delayed until now.

Regarding the situation you describe concerning your relationship with your second wife: after reviewing all your arguments and the events that have occurred in the disputes between you, in my opinion, these are matters that have no real foundation and are nothing more than mere verbal friction.

Surely, among the contributing factors is the interference of other people and matters of *lashon hara* and the like. Consequently, in my opinion, you should have sought advice on how to bring your hearts closer to one another. If it is possible, it would be best to move your residence so as not to be together with those whom one might suspect of being talebearers and exaggerators. Then, one can hope that little by little you will draw closer to each other, and the conflicts will diminish until there is peace and tranquility between you.

It is understood that in accordance with the statement of our Sages that a woman’s tears are readily available, your words should be spoken gently, in which case they will be heard more easily. A soft answer turns away wrath, even when the wrath has a foundation—and especially in your case, where it has no foundation other than emotional agitation and mere verbal outbursts.

Our Sages said, "A second marriage is according to one's deeds." Beyond the simple interpretation of this, one can also learn from it that the relationship between the couple depends much more on one's deeds than in a first marriage, since, fundamentally, the marriage itself is brought about by one's deeds.

I am enclosing here the *Kuntres* for *Chai Elul*, and you should look at the end of the *Maamar* "Lecha Amar Libi." As the *Likutei Torah* cited there explains, in the month of Elul we meet the King in the field. It is well known that the "field" is a place that is not a human settlement; every person possesses the levels of city, field, and desert. In the field, anyone who wishes is permitted and able to go out to greet the King, and the King receives everyone with a shining countenance and shows a smiling face to all.

Through an *is'arusa d'letata* (an awakening from below) comes an *is'arusa d'le'eila* (an awakening from above). Especially in Elul—the acronym for *Ani L'dodi* (this is the beginning, and consequently) *V'dodi Li*—each and every one of us must conduct ourselves in our surroundings toward everyone who draws near to us in such a way that even if it seems to him—and even if there is a basis for this—that his fellow is a "man of the field," he must nevertheless receive him with a shining countenance and show him a smiling face. Just as the month of Elul is a time auspicious for such conduct between Hashem and Yisrael, so too, *alma tata'ah k'gavna d'alma ila'ah* (the lower world is like the higher world), and it is also a time auspicious for such conduct in this lower world between a person and his fellow, and between a man and his wife.

I trust you have set times for studying the teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, as this is a vessel for drawing down and receiving his blessings in both material and spiritual matters.

As you requested, I will mention you, your wife, and your family—may they live—at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. A *tzaddik* who has departed is found in this world more than during his lifetime, and he will surely grant his blessing for everything you need, and may the *Ribono shel Olam* fulfill His blessings completely.

I will conclude with the blessing of *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah*, and I look forward to hearing good news that there is peace and tranquility in your borders, for peace is the vessel that holds blessing in the world.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 733

B”H, 16 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., [Name], may he live.

Peace and blessing!

I have received your two letters from [the week of] Parshas Re’eh and Parshas Ki Seitzei. In accordance with your request, I will read your *pidyonos* at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and he will surely draw down blessing and success upon you and all those you mention in your letter.

Regarding your question concerning the transfer, and your reference to the letter from my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, regarding your travel—I do not understand why you are asking again, and what can I add to my previous letter in which I already wrote about this? In any event, whatever you decide, may it be with success in both material and spiritual matters.

I will read the *pidyon nefesh* for the teacher from "Beis Sarah" at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and he will surely bestow his blessing for whatever she requires in material and spiritual matters.

Regarding your studies in the trade... which you interrupted, and you ask if it is worthwhile to continue studying during the month of Elul: "And you shall gather in your grain, and your wine, and your oil" is also a matter of Torah and of the section of *Krias Shema*, provided that it is done according to the instructions of the Torah and with *yiras shamayim*. Therefore, it appears to me that you should successfully continue your studies and complete your training in this trade, and may Hashem grant His blessing that you find a livelihood wherever you may be, in a kosher manner, with tranquility and abundance.

With the blessing of *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah*, awaiting good news from you and from all those you mention in your letter,

**Menachem Schneerson**

The book *Sefer HaChassidus* by Avraham Kahana is not worth using, especially now that there are so many *seforim* printed regarding Chassidus and the teachings of Chassidus, which were written by Rebbes and by Chassidim.

#### 733

From the secretariat copy.

## 374

B”H, 16 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., [Name], may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter, written without a date, in which you write that the doctor told your wife, may she live, that she needs to spend a few days in the hospital for examination and testing, and that your wife is fearful and anxious about the hospital due to various concerns (which have no basis),

You must explain to her, in terms appropriate to her current state of mind, that:

The Ribono shel Olam created the world and He leads the world, both in the lower hemisphere where we find ourselves, and in the upper hemisphere where... and you find yourselves. He spoke and it came to be; He commanded and it stood firm. Everything that happens in the world does not happen without the Ribono shel Olam, and everything that the Ribono shel Olam wills, happens. It is just that the Ribono shel Olam desires that we create a vessel within nature—meaning, that matters should be conducted through the ways of nature.

When a Yid, man or woman, does not feel well and a doctor must be called, the meaning is not that the doctor will act as he pleases. Rather, the essence of the matter is that Hashem has chosen this doctor to be His messenger to carry out His mission through him.

And when one has bitachon in the Ribono shel Olam, without doubts, that He leads the world, one is then privileged to see this with physical eyes as well, at every step: how the Ribono shel Olam holds each one of us by the hand and leads us where it is best for us, both in gashmius and in ruchnius.

Consequently, if she goes to the hospital according to the doctor's instructions, she remains entirely under the Ribono shel Olam’s domain. The Ribono shel Olam will watch over her and ensure that things go as is best for her, both for the health of the body and for the health of the soul.

She only needs to be strong in her emunah and bitachon, that what the Rebbe, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, has blessed her with—has blessed her now and will continue to bless her—will certainly be fulfilled: that she should have a refuah, and that you and she will soon be able to report good news, that she is feeling healthy.

Presumably, she places [money] into the charity box of Rabbeinu Meir Baal HaNes on Erev Shabbos and Yom Tov before lighting candles, and you surely recite the Rebbe’s chapter of Tehillim, which is currently chapter 71, at the very least until 10 Shevat 5711. Naturally, all of this is without a neder.

With blessings for a kesivah v’chasimah tovah, awaiting good news,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 355

B"H, 16 Elul, 5710

Mrs. ... may she live,

Greetings and blessings!

I have received your letter of 27 Menachem Av, in which you describe your situation and lament that you are lacking in goodness and piety.

It is stated in Chassidus regarding the month of Elul, and it is also brought in a maamar of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, that in the month of Elul, Hashem is like a king who goes out into the field. There, everyone can approach Him, and there He receives everyone with a welcoming countenance and a smile, regardless of the state of the man or woman who approaches Him.

The lesson to be drawn from this is that in the month of Elul—the final month of the year, when one must make a *cheshbon hanefesh* for the entire past year—a person should not be frightened when they begin to examine their conduct during the time that has passed.

One is told: You should know that even though you find yourself in a field and do not yet know if you are fit to be admitted into a place where proper people reside, you should know that the King of Kings has come out to you in the field. There, you can approach Him directly and present your request, and He will receive you with a welcoming countenance and a smile; therefore, you need not be afraid.

And even if the *cheshbon* you have made of your deeds, words, and thoughts is not a good one, since you are dealing with an *Av Harachaman*—a Father full of mercy—if you only make a resolution that from now on you will do everything you can to be as Hashem desires, you will surely have a *kesivah vachasimah tovah*, a good and sweet year, both for the body and for the *neshamah*.

In particular, a mother who has raised children who walk in the straight path, in the path of Torah and the path of Chassidus, should feel encouraged. This is one of the greatest *zechusim* a woman can have, and it will help you emerge from this situation which is not as it should be, and lead you onto the right path to have *tov gashmi veruchni*.

We must all know that we had a great Rebbe, and we now also have a great Rebbe—the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory—who prayed and interceded, and continues to intercede, for *bracha* and *hatzlachah* for Yidden in general, and for those who are connected to him in particular.

The Rebbe knew, and still knows, the weaknesses of the men and women in the *kehal adas hachassidim*. Yet, despite this, he prayed for them and continues to pray for them, that they should have what they need—*bnei, chayei, umezona revicha*—and that Hashem should forgive their *aveiros*, taking into account the state in which they find themselves and the *nisyonos* of the *yetzer hara*.

Therefore, we must all make a resolution within ourselves that from now on we will become better. This resolution will draw down *bracha* and *hatzlachah* so that we all—and you and your household, may they live, are included in this—should have a *shanah tovah umetukah*. May everyone’s heart’s desires be fulfilled for the good; if one is pious, it will naturally be good, both *b'gashmius* and *b'ruchnius*.

I will mention all those you mentioned in your letter at the appropriate time at the *tziyun* of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, for a blessing and for a *kesivah vachasimah tovah*.

I wish you and all yours a *kesivah vachasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, and you will surely be able to report good news about yourself and all yours.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 476

B"H, 17 Elul, 5716

To the Gathering of Chassidei Chabad
Yeshivas Olei Russia
Shechunas HaRakevet A6
Lod

Shalom u’Bracha!

Surely you have received the telegram sent to you yesterday, and this is its text:

"The national gathering of Chassidei Chabad in Eretz HaKodesh will surely strengthen and increase the 'service of the heart,' which is tefillah, the study of the revealed Torah and Da"Ch, and guidance in the ways of Chassidus according to the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov and the Alter Rebbe—whose lights were kindled on the bright day of Chai Elul—and according to the instructions of our Nasi, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. You will surely fulfill your role as the 'armies of Hashem,' as explained in the continuation of the maamarim of the histalkus, that no obstacles can hinder you from strengthening Torah and Yiddishkeit in general, and proper chinuch in particular—the proper chinuch of the boys and girls who are already here and those currently arriving in Eretz HaKodesh—and that this spiritual rescue is a holy obligation of every man and woman. You will surely strengthen the maintenance of all the institutions of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and the 'desire of Hashem will succeed in their hands.'

With a blessing that you merit to fulfill the demand of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, to 'make Eretz Yisrael, Eretz HaKodesh,' success in your avodah, and a kesivah v’chasimah tovah in both gashmius and ruchnius for all the participants and their families, each and every one individually—

**Menachem Schneerson**"

With the blessing of a kesivah v’chasimah tovah,

**The Secretariat**

***

## 5737

B"H, 17 Elul, 5737

The esteemed avreich, learned and G-d-fearing, etc., [Name]

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 26 Menachem Av, in which you describe your situation and quote what the Rebbe Rayatz, of blessed memory, wrote to you—that you should arrange shiurim in your home and endeavor to increase the number of friends who will participate in the studies—you also write to me that you have been occupied with gathering children and holding Shabbos parties, and sometimes even during the week. You also currently have fixed study sessions, yet you have no satisfaction from this; you feel that something is missing, and you ask for my opinion on the matter.

In my opinion, what is missing is the concept of kabbalas ol, which is the foundation and root of all avodah. To attain kabbalas ol, one does not require any special or profound hisbonenus; one need only visualize that the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, created His world, and subsequently "breathed into his nostrils the soul of life"—which is his soul that enlivens his body—and He stands over him, watching, observing, examining his kidneys and heart, and counting all his deeds, his words, and all his steps (as stated in Tanya, Chapter 42).

To facilitate this avodah for you, Hashem has granted you the merit of being a Chossid—meaning that your soul is a part of the collective soul of the Tzaddik. If anything is lacking in the individual soul, then through the proper hiskashrus, everything that is missing is drawn down from the collective soul. The Rebbe stated explicitly that when he accepted the Nesi'us, he stipulated that it be done with chesed and rachamim. Since then, from the year 5680 until now, he stands and serves, drawing down for all his students, Chassidim, and those connected to him, strength and vitality in avodas Hashem, which includes both the matters between man and Hashem and the matters between man and his fellow.

In addition to all this, the Rebbe himself chose you and entrusted you with a special shlichus. It is known even according to the revealed Torah that "a person's agent is like himself." Consequently, it is understood that in this shlichus, the Rebbe has given you from his own powers. As explained in the maamar of Chai Elul of this year, the word mitzvah implies *tzavta* (connection) and *chibur* (joining). It is explained there at length with a parable: that when a person fulfills and performs the command of a wise man or a king—even if he does not know the content of the matter or grasp its greatness—his joy should be boundless, for through this he unites and connects with the very essence of the wise man and the essence of the king.

Yet, after all this—that you possess all of the above—you complain that you have no satisfaction in this and do not feel vitality, and you only feel that something is missing.

If you will listen to my advice, abandon this excessive preoccupation with your own feelings regarding what you lack and what you have. Instead, contemplate the beautiful portion that has been given to you: that you are among the students and those connected to our Nasi, the Rebbe Rayatz, of blessed memory. Rejoice with great joy in this portion of yours, and translate that joy into concrete, practical actions to fulfill the will of the One who sent you. The powers for this have already been given to you; you lack nothing but the ratzon.

When you nullify your own ratzon before the ratzon of our Nasi—who transmitted to you the ratzon of Hashem—then that very act will become a vessel through which vitality will be drawn down to you in all your activities in fulfilling the will of the Rebbe Rayatz, of blessed memory.

With the blessing of *Kesivah v'chasimah tovah*, awaiting good news from you that you have increased your courage in your avodah, both with yourself and with others, and that you are serving Hashem with joy.

**Menachem Schneerson**

Enclosed is the kuntres for Chai Elul.

## Tishlach

B"H, 17 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I received your letter from some time ago, and now I have received your letter from the 2nd of Elul. As you requested, I will read your *pidyon nefesh* at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. A *tzaddik* who has departed is found in this world more than during his lifetime, and he will surely bestow his blessing, and the *Ribono shel Olam* will fulfill his blessings in both material and spiritual matters.

I trust that your wife, may she live, is being examined from time to time by a specialist doctor, and one should not be discouraged by the fact that the matter has persisted until now. Especially in the year of passing, where on one hand all limitations have been removed, as explained in *Iggeres HaKodesh* section 27 and its commentary, and on the other hand, even according to the revealed Torah, for the entire twelve months there is a special connection between the one who has passed and this world—one must create a *keli* within the natural order. Then, there will be influences of whatever is needed, in both material and spiritual matters, with success that transcends the natural order, provided that the channel of *hiskashrus* remains in great strength and open. The ways of *hiskashrus* have already been explained in the introductions to the *kuntreisim* that I have published over the last few months.

Enclosed is the *kuntres* for Chai Elul, along with the letter attached to it.

It is surely unnecessary to urge you to set fixed times specifically for the teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, to recite chapter 71 of *Tehillim*, and to give *tzedakah* in a manner that will **not** be a burden upon you—for in this, it is not the quantity that matters, but the act itself—**without a vow**, every day, excluding Shabbos and *Yomim Tovim*, to the institutions founded by my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. This corresponds to the three pillars of Torah, *avodah*, and *gemilus chasadim*.

My hope is strong that you will be able to bring me good news, both regarding yourself, your wife, may she live, and your daughters and sons, may they live.

I sign with the blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*, and I await good news,

**Menachem Schneerson**

***

**Tishlach**

Published in *Likkutei Sichos*, Vol. 11, p. 211, and completed according to the secretariat's copy.

**Chapter 71:** See also above, letter 444, and the references in the notes there.

## 339

339

B"H, 17 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., R' Nissan, shlita,

Greetings and blessings!

My previous letters and the kuntresim were surely received in due time.

I am deeply distressed by the news I received that our friend, the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., R' [Name], shlita, has been arrested, and since then I have had no word.

I am certain that you are doing everything possible regarding this matter. Please be kind enough to inform me of what is being done and what the current situation is. My hope is strong that during these days of mercy, and in accordance with the legal ruling that "whoever has a case..." [may it be] in this month of mercy, they will be released and this source of distress will be removed.

I await good news from you in general, and regarding the aforementioned in particular.

I sign with the blessing of *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah*,

**M. Schneerson**

When the aforementioned is released, please convey to them my blessing for a *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah* for a good and sweet year, and that they should thereby fulfill their obligation for the entire year and know no distress, *chas v'shalom*.

And we conclude with revealed and apparent good.

#### ***339***

From a photocopy of the letter.

**R' Nissan:** Moreinu Harav Nissan Nemanov. Additional letters to him appear above, 340, and in the references noted there.

## 5440

B"H, 17 Elul, 5740

To the avreich, the learned and G-d-fearing, etc., [Name] shlita,

Peace and blessing!

It is my hope that you have arrived safely at your destination, and I hereby wish you a peaceful arrival.

True peace is as stated in Maseches Sanhedrin 100a, that Torah brings peace to the world. The Gemara explains this as peace in the Heavenly entourage and in the earthly entourage.

The interpretation according to the teachings of mussar and Chassidus is that every person possesses a nefesh elokis and a nefesh habahamis, a yetzer tov and a yetzer hara, and they are in a state of war: which of them will conquer and capture the "small city"—this body—so that it will listen to its will and act according to its counsel. The difference between them is that the yetzer tov cannot, under any circumstances, agree with the opinion of the yetzer hara, for it is truly a "part of Elokus from above." This is not the case with the yetzer hara; it, too, knows its Master, but the purpose of its coming was to test the person and see: "The words of the Master and the words of the student—whose words should one heed?" And when the yetzer hara sees that despite all its incitements and temptations, the person did not listen to its words but acted according to the words of the yetzer tov, then it, too, rejoices in this. For not only did it fail to cause the person to stumble, but it actually increased the person's reward for having withstood the test and remained faithful to his Creator.

It is understood from this that peace in the earthly entourage—that is, between the yetzer tov and the yetzer hara, between the desires of the body and the desires of the soul—is possible in only one way: through the yetzer tov conquering the yetzer hara. Through this, peace is also created in the Heavenly entourage, where there is also a *sitra d'smala* (the left side), for as our Sages said, the yetzer hara is the Satan, while the *sitra d'yamina* (the right side) is Michael, the minister of Israel, and above him is the Ribono shel Olam, who said, "My son, My firstborn, Israel," and "You are children to Hashem your G-d." Thus, when they see in the Heavenly Court—where there are those who incline to the right and those who incline to the left, and where they judge the person—that the yetzer tov has conquered the yetzer hara, and this has happened many times over until his merits outweigh his sins, then peace is made in the Heavenly entourage. The attribute of judgment ceases its prosecution, Heaven forbid, and even the *katigor* (prosecutor) becomes a *sanigor* (defender), and they decree for the person all that is good, both materially and spiritually.

As you requested, I will mention you at the tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. May the Ribono shel Olam guide you on the straight path, so that you yourself will walk in the way of Torah and mitzvos with yiras shamayim, and influence your surroundings as well, so that throughout the entire environment there will be the "light of Torah and the lamp of mitzvah," for then there will be light in their dwellings in material matters as well.

I hope that from time to time you will study the teachings of Chassidus, and if you encounter any difficulty, you will surely discuss it with the anash in your vicinity. I am confident that you will find pleasure in the light of this Torah—the Torah of the hidden—and that it will give you vitality in your path in life.

I wish you a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, success in your holy work, and I await good news.

**Menachem Schneerson**

Enclosed is the Kuntres for 18 Elul, which has just been published.

## 5741

B"H, 17 Elul, 5741

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., [Name], may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from Tuesday, Parshas Shoftim, informing me of your safe arrival in the city of Cleveland with your family, may they live, and of your activities there and the arrangements made during these first few days:

I was happy to hear that you are approaching the local Jews with Torah and mitzvos in general, and Chassidus in particular. Surely this will serve as a vessel for the drawing down and receiving of material and spiritual blessings as needed for you and your family, may they live.

Regarding your news that you have been accepted as a *shaliach tzibbur* for the *Mussaf* prayers during the upcoming *Yomim Nora'im*: May it be His will that you succeed as a *shaliach tzibbur*, and may your prayers be accepted so that your congregation may be inscribed and sealed for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

You should certainly endeavor to find a suitable *beis medrash* for yourself without any issue of encroaching on others' territory. Naturally, this does not happen overnight; you should look around with patience so that you understand the situation in the city clearly, and then you will surely find what is appropriate for you.

Of course, this does not preclude the matter of *parnassah*, as the spiritual leaders in the *shuls* here in this country are occupied on *Shabbos* and *Yomim Tovim*, but are relatively free during the weekdays.

Enclosed is the *Kuntres* for Chai Elul, along with the letter attached to it.

With blessings for a *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah*, and awaiting good news regarding both the individual and the collective.

**Menachem Schneerson**

You should certainly arrange your affairs with the *Vaad*.

***5741***

From the Secretariat copy.

## 5742

B"H, 17 Elul, 5742

Mrs. ... may she live,

Blessings and peace!

In response to your letter of the 9th of the 8th month, in which you describe your situation and mention that you wrote about this to the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, and he answered you at that time...

You must know that the Rebbe always kept his promises. Especially now, in the year of his passing—when, as the Alter Rebbe writes, the various limitations that existed previously are removed—you must be certain and strong in your emunah and bitachon that the Rebbe’s promise will be fulfilled; it is only a matter of time. It goes without saying...

Regarding what you write about your daughter... may she live, I still remember from my time in Paris, when she conveyed your request through me...

A Siddur was sent to her at that time, and I am certain that, receiving her chinuch in Beis Rivkah, she likely uses that Siddur often, and it is probably worn out by now. I have therefore instructed that a new Siddur be sent to her. May Hashem help that whatever she prays for in this Siddur be acceptable before the Master of all...

In general, these are the days of Elul, and soon the days of Selichos will arrive, followed by the Yomim Noraim and the joyous days of Sukkos and Shemini Atzeres. One must know that this is the case for every Yid, man and woman alike: when one asks the Ribono shel Olam to be merciful and compassionate and to preserve kindness, it awakens the heart and the "pintele Yid" that exists within everyone. At that time, the Ribono shel Olam grants atonement if one has not acted as one should, and He grants a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*. Then come the joyous times, and one merits a *shanah tovah u'mesukah* in both material and spiritual matters.

You likely observe the custom of giving tzedakah to the pushka of Rabbi Meir Baal HaNes before lighting Shabbos and Yom Tov candles, which is a segulah for health and parnassah.

Being at the Tziyun of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, I will mention you, your husband, and your daughter for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*, and I wish that the Rebbe’s promise be fulfilled as soon as possible, and that you have a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year.

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***5742***

From the secretariat's copy.

## 5743

5743

B"H, 17 Elul, 5743

To the esteemed and G-d-fearing [name], may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

I have received your letter and the *pidyon* regarding a blessing for success for you, your wife—may she live—and your two sons—may they live. Without a vow, when I am at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, I will mention all of you with a request for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

Surely you have heard the saying that my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, stated: that a blessing is like rain. First, one must sow the seeds oneself, and then, through the blessing, a single kernel that was sown yields many, many times more.

So it is also when one does, from one's own side, a matter of Torah and *mitzvos* in general, and *tzedakah* in particular. Even though a person acts according to his own limited strengths, he is blessed such that Hashem gives according to His unlimited strengths—health, *parnassah*, and success for you, your wife, and your children, may they live.

I am certain that you will seek, and also find, ways to act—both with yourself and with others—in matters of *tzedakah*, Torah, and *mitzvos*, and this will serve as a fitting *keli* for everything that you and your family need.

I also wish to ask you to convey to your uncle, may he live, that in accordance with your request, I will also mention him at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and pray that he be blessed with a *refuah* soon and a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year.

Enclosed is the receipt for your donation to the *Lishkas Hasha'im* of Machne Israel. From this fund, individuals and institutions are assisted who, for specific reasons, need to be helped without publicity, and *tzedakah* given in secret is, after all, one of the highest forms of *tzedakah*.

With the blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 544

B"H, 17 Elul, 5710

The esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., R' Y.A. (may he live),

Shalom u'Bracha!

In response to your letter of 5 Elul, which briefly describes the progression of your life and your current order of avodah, I was pleased to read that in your new location you are fulfilling the shlichus of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory). Since the powers of the sender are granted to the one sent, there will surely be success.

As is explained in several places in Chassidus, it is through creating a vessel within the natural order that success is drawn down in a manner that transcends the natural order.

Regarding your question as to whether, in the future, if you find someone who can replace you, you should hand over your position and occupy yourself with bringing simple youths closer to Torah and mitzvos: since this is not currently a practical matter, it is a waste of effort to resolve this question now.

Regarding your question about the education of your daughter and whether she should attend school: it is appropriate to endeavor to form a class of several girls and hire teachers for them who will exempt the students from attending another school. Then, there will surely be the proper supervision regarding matters of Torah and yiras shamayim...

As you requested, I will mention you and your family (may they live) at the tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), at the appropriate time.

Enclosed is the Kuntres for Chai Elul, which has just been published, along with the letter attached to it.

I sign with the blessing of a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for you, for all your family (may they live), and for the entire group (may they live).

**Menachem Schneerson**

***

**544**

From the secretariat copy.

**R' Y.A. (may he live):** Pliskin.

**In your new location:** Australia.

## 45

17 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed and distinguished [name], may he live,

Greetings and blessings,

In response to your letter from the first day of Rosh Chodesh Elul, I was pleased to learn that you have completed your course of study, have received your credentials, and have become established in your profession.

It is well known what the Rambam elaborates upon in *Hilchos Deios* regarding the service of "In all your ways know Him"—meaning that whatever your path in life may be, you can certainly serve Hashem in that way. There is no doubt that if you search with the appropriate effort for how to serve Havayah in your specific path, you will find the correct methods.

Regarding what you write—that during your travels you encountered G-d-fearing Jews and learned from them—this is a very good thing, in accordance with the Mishnah, "Who is wise? He who learns from every person" (see the *Zichronos* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for the Baal Shem Tov’s interpretation of this statement). However, this is still not enough. Every person must be a student, but also a teacher to others. There are several ways to do this: either by teaching in the literal sense—speaking with others and teaching them upright conduct in matters between man and Hashem and between man and his fellow—or by teaching others through serving as a model for them in your conduct in general, and in the study of Torah and the fulfillment of mitzvos in particular. That is, when a person sees you and is convinced that your conduct is good, he will declare: "Look at so-and-so, who conducts himself as is fitting for a Jew; everyone must learn from his conduct." I hope that in time this, too, will come to pass, and not only in the second manner. That is, in addition to what you write in your letter—that you teach in Sunday school and work in a religious organization—you will add to your teaching by influencing others in a direct manner, by stirring them toward the study of Torah with yiras shamayim, the fulfillment of mitzvos, and so on.

Regarding what you write—that you find the matter of *birur hamidos* (refining one's character traits) difficult—I do not fully understand your intent. Are you speaking of traits between man and his fellow, or of other matters? Surely, next time you will clarify your words on this.

As for what you write—that you are puzzled by world politics in relation to the coming of Moshiach—you did not specify what exactly you are puzzled about. Surely you remember what we spoke about when you were here: that the Ribono shel Olam did not create a single thing in the world in vain. This is also true regarding world events and understanding their meaning. Consequently, if this were relevant to a person’s service to his Creator, there would surely be people engaged in fortune-telling to make the person’s service easier. The fact that there is no one who tells the future is itself proof that we are required to perform our service even without knowing what the future holds.

I already stated my words about two months ago: that in my opinion, there will not be a war in the coming months. Therefore, there is no need to be alarmed; everyone should occupy themselves with their appropriate affairs, the essence of which is to fulfill the task of their neshamah in the world. This is achieved through the study of Torah and the fulfillment of mitzvos, as all of this is explained in the teachings of Chassidus.

And one who has merited that the Rebbe—my father-in-law, of blessed memory—has placed a shlichus upon him, this too is part of fulfilling the task of his neshamah in this world.

Continuing our previous conversation, when you asked me several questions regarding Eretz Yisrael: surely it is now clear to you as well that we see tangibly how the Ribono shel Olam is trying to explain to the Bnei Yisrael that this is not the hoped-for Geulah. He is trying to explain this with kindness and mercy, so that they will understand that this is only a minor rescue, and that for many reasons, the Geulah for which our prophets prophesied and for which we pray every single day—"And to Jerusalem, Your city, etc.," "The sprout of David, Your servant, etc.," "And may our eyes behold, etc."—has not yet come. Note that before "To Jerusalem, Your city," we say, "And we shall never be put to shame, for we have trusted in You," and before "And may our eyes behold," we say, "Father of mercy, have mercy upon us." This means that the Geulah will come specifically from Hashem Yisbarach and is bound up with Torah and mitzvos, as explained in the statements of our Sages in many places, among them: "Yisrael will only be redeemed through teshuvah; Yisrael will only be redeemed through tzedakah." Every man and woman among the sons and daughters of Yisrael must know that with every good action he or she performs, they are bringing the end closer—the end of the galus and the darkness—and are bringing closer the complete and true Geulah through Moshiach Tzidkeinu. This is the only path to the redemption of the Jewish people, just as Moshe Rabbeinu told the entire Jewish people more than three thousand years ago, and it is written at length in the Torah of Havayah in Parshas Nitzavim (Chapter 30).

Regarding your request to mention you at the tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, I will do as you requested. As for your writing that you do not have an understanding of this: surely when you eat, drink, and sleep, you do not first contemplate how this affects your body and your neshamah, yet you do all of this even if you do not understand how the process works. It is the same in this matter.

Regarding what you write—that it seems as if one is speaking to the dead, chas v'shalom, or directing one's thoughts to another, chas v'shalom—you surely understand yourself that this is not the case, as it is agreed that Calev ben Yefuneh, as well as many Tannaim, Amoraim, and Tzaddikim throughout the generations, did exactly this.

To explain your difficulty briefly: even when people would come to the Rebbe to ask for a blessing, they did not come to him because of the virtue of his body, but because of the virtue of his neshamah. The entire concept of death applies only to the body, for the neshamah is eternal. Especially the neshamah of a Tzaddik, which does not belong at all to Gehinom, *kaf hakela*, etc. The concept of death for such a soul is merely *histalkus*—a departure to a higher level—and it is not called "dead," chas v'shalom. This is as written in the Zohar (Vol. III, p. 71a). Regarding what you write about directing your thought to another: briefly, this is not the case. For (a) the request is that the Tzaddik, in his great righteousness, should advocate for the petitioner before the King of Kings, the Holy One, blessed be He. (b) A second intention here is that every Chossid and one who is *mekushar* has a neshamah that is a part of the neshamah of the Tzaddik, which is the great whole. It is likened to a head in relation to its individual parts, as explained in Tanya, Chapter 2. Just as every limb, even though it receives its vitality from the neshamah, is first enclothed in the head and brain, and from the head and brain the vitality is then distributed to each limb according to its nature—so too is it with a Chossid and a Rebbe. Since the head is healthy and strong, it contains all the different vitalities of every limb. In order for the limb to be healthy, the connection with the head must be complete—that is, the veins and nerves connecting the head to the limbs must be open, so that the vitality belonging to that limb will be drawn to it.

This is, in general, the concept of the connection of a Chossid to a Rebbe, through which the Chossid receives everything he needs, both materially and spiritually.

Time does not permit me to elaborate further, but I hope that this, too, is sufficient. If you have any questions regarding what is written above, surely you will inform me, and in my spare time I will endeavor to answer them as well.

I am enclosing herewith the *Kuntres* for Chai Elul, and I sign with the blessing of *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah* for a good and sweet year,

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 456

B"H, 17 Elul, 5716

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Rabbi Ch. M. (may he live and be well),

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 7 Elul, thank you for the information you write regarding the manner of temporary burial and subsequent reinterment in Eretz Yisrael.

Regarding your question: you and your brother (may they live and be well) both wish to lead the davening, and as a result, there are sometimes only seven people in each of your respective minyanim. You ask if it would be better for only one of you to serve as the shaliach tzibbur so that there will be a complete minyan of ten. I have not heard a specific ruling on this, but in my opinion, it is better that each of you leads the davening. In most instances, there will surely be ten people in each minyan, and if at times you are forced to make do with six, etc., you may rely on the Shulchan Aruch of the Alter Rebbe, which states that they are "like a complete congregation" (Shulchan Aruch Admur HaZaken, end of siman 69, seif 6)—and he has broad shoulders...

As you requested, I will mention you and your family (may they live and be well) at the tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory).

I sign with the blessing of *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah* and that you settle soon in a manner that is appropriate for you, both materially and spiritually, and I await good news.

**Menachem Schneerson**

***456***

Published in Likkutei Sichos, Vol. 14, p. 228, and completed based on the secretariat's copy.

**Temporary burial:** As per the request in the aforementioned letter 449.

## 5747

B"H, 17 Elul, 5747

To the avreich, the refined and G-d-fearing, etc., R' [Name] shlita,

Shalom u’Bracha!

In response to your letter from Motzaei Shabbos, 7 Elul, regarding your question: you have been studying at the yeshiva in Tel Aviv, and you ask whether, since you have been feeling a "lack" lately, it might be worthwhile to change your location.

In general, you should present this matter to your mashpia, the refined R' Shlomo Chaim shlita Kaselman, and inform me of his opinion on the matter.

Regarding what you write—that you feel a "lack"—you do not clarify what you mean by this lack. Do you not have in your possession the kuntreisim of the Torah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and his sichos? In them, one finds instructions and advice on how not to allow the "clever one" (the yetzer hara) to distract one from Torah, avodah, and yiras shamayim.

There is reason to suspect that this feeling of a "lack" is also one of the distractions of the yetzer hara, which disguises itself in a garment tailored to the spirit of each individual it wishes to entice.

Surely you have set times for studying specifically the Torah of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and you also recite Chapter 71 of Tehillim every day. May it be the will of Hashem that you merit to be a Chassid, a yirei shamayim, and a lamdan, and that through you, the will of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, will be fulfilled—a detail of which is "lamps to illuminate," meaning that you should illuminate your surroundings with the light of Torah and yiras shamayim. Please share good news regarding this.

I will read the pidyon nefesh enclosed in your letter at the tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. A tzaddik who has departed is found in this world even more than during his lifetime; may he grant his blessing, and may Hashem fulfill your requests for the good.

With the blessing of Kesivah v’Chasimah Tovah,

**Menachem Schneerson**

#### ***5747***

From the secretariat copy.

**Chapter 71:** See above, Letter 444, and the references in the notes there.

## תשמח

B"H, 17 Elul, 5710

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter from 18 Menachem Av, in which you ask regarding the relocation from our camp now, and to where:

In my opinion, you should look into whether it is possible for you to obtain papers for the U.S.A., Canada, or Australia, and approximately how much time that would take. If it is not possible for you, please let me know.

...Regarding what you wrote that your son... does not have much desire for his studies: explain it to him in terms that are appropriate for him. *Now it is completely different*, and he must know that the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, is the Nasi and the head of all his Chassidim and those connected to him. Since the head is, in the simplest sense, healthy and strong, all the powers and vitality pertaining to each and every one of those connected to him are present within him **in their entirety**, and the matter depends only on them. If, G-d forbid, they are not aligned with the will of the head, this does not affect the one connected to him alone.

When he contemplates this even for a short while, he will surely see the great responsibility that rests upon him. In accordance with his state of health, he should surrender and give himself over—*to give oneself over entirely*—to the will of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, and to the fulfillment of his words and instructions found in his sichos and his general or private letters, in which each and every Jew will find instructions for their path in life. *And one must not be intimidated, thinking, "What connection does a small boy have with such a great Rebbe?"* as this is explained in several places in Chassidus, and also in the maamar of 18 Elul of this year, Chapter 7, and in the *Kuntres* mentioned there.

I am sure that you and your son-in-law, may he live, have set times specifically for the teachings of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, and that you also recite Chapter 71 of Tehillim.

As you requested, I will mention you and your entire family, may they live, at the Tziyun of the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*.

With the blessing of *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year,

**Menachem Schneerson**

***
