## 5709

B”H, 18 Elul, 5709
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To our brothers and sisters, the sons and daughters of Israel,
In every place and location,
May Hashem grant them life.

Greetings and blessings!

As we stand on the threshold of the new year, which is approaching for us and for all the sons and daughters of Israel for good and for blessing, each and every one of us awakens to take stock of everything that has occurred over the past year—both what we have done and what has been done to us. We resolve to improve our ways and turn in tefillah to our Father in Heaven, that He may write and seal us—each man, each woman, and all that is theirs—for a good and sweet year, in both gashmius and ruchnius.

Our Sages of blessed memory have said that through the giving of tzedakah to the poor, tefillah is effective in drawing down good life, Geulah and yeshuah, parnassah and sustenance.

Now, man—like all created beings, and even the angels on high—is composed of both body and soul. Just as there is a poor person in terms of the body and the needs of the body, so too is there a poor person in terms of the soul and the needs of the soul. Therefore, regarding tzedakah as well, there is tzedakah in gashmius and tzedakah in ruchnius. As our Sages of blessed memory stated (Tanna D’bei Eliyahu Rabbah, ch. 27): “If you see a naked person and you clothe him”—how is this done? If you see a person who lacks words of Torah, bring him into your home and teach him Krias Shema and tefillah, teach him [Torah], and encourage him in the performance of mitzvos.

Just as the request of each and every one of us is to be written and sealed for good in both gashmius and ruchnius, so too must each and every one of us strive in both gashmius tzedakah and ruchnius tzedakah together, especially during the days of Elul and Tishrei.

When we come before the King of Kings, the Holy One, Blessed be He, during these days, may each and every one of us—all of us—have in our hands a great account—great according to one’s own capacity—of the tzedakah we have performed to assist the poor in gashmius, and the tzedakah we have performed to assist the poor in ruchnius.

And just as even a poor person in gashmius is obligated in gashmius tzedakah—for there is no poor person who cannot find a way to help a fellow poor person—so too is the poor person in ruchnius obligated in ruchnius tzedakah. For there is no man or woman in Israel who cannot influence other sons and daughters of Israel to bring them closer to yiras shamayim, Torah, and mitzvos.

And *l’fum gamla shichna* (according to the camel is the load): those who are wealthy in gashmius and those who are wealthy in ruchnius—the masters of Torah, those who sit in the tents of Torah, and the yeshiva students—must spend generously from their wealth and from their knowledge for the salvation, healing, and strengthening of the souls and bodies of their brothers and sisters.

May our Father, the Father of mercy, write and seal each and every one of us within the entirety of the Jewish people for a good and sweet year, in revealed and manifest goodness, in gashmius and in ruchnius, and may He bring us, speedily and in our days, the true and complete Geulah through Mashiach Tzidkeinu, amen.

Blessing and being blessed with a good writing and sealing,

**Menachem Mendel Schneerson**

## 550

B”H, 18 Elul, 5710

To our brothers and sisters, the sons and daughters of Israel,
Wherever you may be,
May Hashem grant them life.

Peace and blessings!

Standing on the threshold of the new year, which is approaching for us and for all of Israel for the good, each one of us is stirred to make a *cheshbon hanefesh* regarding everything that has transpired with him over the past year. Each person resolves that the new year should be better and higher in all respects, and turns with a *tefillah* to our Father in Heaven to grant him a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*, a good and sweet year in both *gashmius* and *ruchnius*.

Our Sages, of blessed memory, say that through giving *tzedakah*, *tefillah* is accepted and it is effective in bringing Hashem to grant us health, *parnassah*, and happiness.

Just as a person consists of a body and a *neshamah*, it follows that when we speak of poverty, it exists both in a physical sense—the needs pertaining to the body—and in a spiritual sense—the needs pertaining to the *neshamah*.

Likewise, regarding *tzedakah*, there is *tzedakah* for the body and *tzedakah* for the *neshamah*. Our Sages tell us (*Tanna D’bei Eliyahu Rabbah*, ch. 27), “If you see one who is naked, cover him”—what does the prophet mean by this? He means that when you see a person lacking Torah, you should teach him *Krias Shema* and *tefillah*, and encourage him to perform *mitzvos*, study Torah, and so on.

In these days of *cheshbon hanefesh*, every person, man or woman, must take stock: how much *tzedakah* have they given to those in material need, and how much *tzedakah* have they given to those in spiritual need? One must also account for whether this is sufficient in proportion to the extent of their own resources, both spiritual and material. And now is still a time to rectify what one has neglected.

And just as even a poor person can and must help his poor fellow with whatever he has, so too can and must every Jew, even if he is spiritually poor, help another who is spiritually impoverished with whatever he possesses in a spiritual sense—exerting a good influence on those around him to bring them closer to Torah and *mitzvos*.

And those who are materially wealthy, as well as those who possess spiritual treasures—the *baalei Torah*, the *bnei hayeshiva*, and so on—must give with an **open** hand from their money and their spiritual treasures, to save, heal, and strengthen the *neshamah* and the body of their brothers and sisters.

I conclude with a wish and a blessing that our Father in Heaven, the *Av Harachamim*, should grant us all a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*, for a good and sweet year in *gashmius* and *ruchnius*, and send us the *Geulah* *amitis* *v’shleimah* through *Moshiach Tzidkeinu* speedily in our days, amen.

He who blesses and is blessed with a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Mendel son of Chana**

**Schneerson**

Son-in-law of the *Nasi*, our holy master,
our teacher and rabbi (of Lubavitch),
*ztzvkllh"h*
*nvg"m* *zi"a* *hak"m*

## 551

551

B.H., First Day of Selichos, 5711

To the Yeshiva students, may Hashem grant them life,

Greetings and blessings!

With the permission of the esteemed Rabbis, the heads of the Yeshivos, I am writing to you, my friends, the Yeshiva students, in particular, in addition to my letter of the 18th of Elul.

The great fortune that Divine Providence has granted you to be among those whose portion is the Torah of Hashem, and the unique quality that Hashem has imbued in youth—that they possess greater strength and energy—these two factors place upon you a special responsibility to be among the first, both in doing and in causing others to do, to provide spiritual tzedakah to those who are "poor in knowledge," and to give with a generous eye and an open hand.

Our Sages have ruled: The mitzvah of Torah study is greater, in and of itself, than other mitzvos. It is equivalent to all of them because study leads to action. However, if a mitzvah cannot be performed by others—for instance, if one’s words are more readily heard, or **if the actions of others are insufficient for this mitzvah**—one interrupts one’s study to perform the mitzvah, and only afterwards returns to one’s study.

In the days of the coming month of Tishrei, the month in which it is the duty and privilege of every person in Israel to "renew his deeds and improve his deeds," you, the Yeshiva students, must gird yourselves with greater intensity and strength:

(a) In your work with yourselves: To awaken with a true awakening of teshuvah, the acceptance of the yoke of His Kingdom, may He be blessed, and the acceptance of the yoke of Torah with yiras shamayim and the performance of mitzvos with simchah.

(b) In your work with others: To bring close those who need to be brought close, and to strengthen those who need strengthening—in the work of teshuvah, the study of Torah, and the performance of mitzvos. **And in particular, to conduct a wide-ranging campaign**, in all appropriate ways, so that every Jewish boy and girl, young man and young woman, is educated in a proper education, in the spirit of our ancestors, without compromises.

"And the Holy One, blessed be He, said to Israel: If you improve your deeds, I will rise from the Throne of Judgment and sit upon the Throne of Mercy, and I will transform the attribute of judgment into the attribute of mercy for you."

May you be blessed and be a source of blessing with a *kesivah vachasimah tovah*.

**Menachem Mendel son of Channah**
**Schneerson**
Son-in-law of our holy master, our teacher and Rebbe (of Lubavitch)
May the merit of the righteous protect us.

## 552

B"H

It is well known, as explained in many places in Chassidus, why it is called *Rosh Hashanah* (Head of the Year) and not *Techilas Hashanah* (Beginning of the Year). This is because this day is to the rest of the days of the year as the head is to the body and its limbs. Just as the head contains the vitality of all the limbs—and subsequently, in a revealed manner, vitality is drawn from the head to each individual limb —so too, the day of *Rosh Hashanah* includes within it the vitality of all the days of the year. They are all included within it in a concealed state, and subsequently, this vitality is distributed in a revealed manner to the individual days.

It can be said that this is one of the reasons for the warning we have been given to cherish the time during the days of *Rosh Hashanah* above all else. For the hours and moments of these days are general and all-encompassing, and they are superior to the rest of the days of the year—not only in quantity, but also in quality, similar to the substance of the brain in the head compared to the substance of the other limbs of the body.

It is understood that just as with the head and the limbs of the body—when is a person healthy? When the vitality in the head is in its perfection, and it is also drawn from there to each and every limb according to its specific need—so too is the *avodah* of the days of *Rosh Hashanah*. These days must be appropriate to their essence: the head of the entire year. Furthermore, they must be connected to the rest of the days of the year, to influence them with the vitality of accepting the yoke of His Kingdom, may He be blessed, the study of Torah, and the fulfillment of *mitzvos*.

* * *

Now, among the matters that assist in *avodah* in general, and in the *avodah* of connecting *Rosh Hashanah* with the rest of the days of the year in particular, is the connection with the "Head of the thousands of Yisrael", whose soul is in the category of the head and the *mochin* (intellectual faculties) relative to the other souls of his generation. From him is their nourishment and vitality, and through him they are connected and unified with their original essence and root.

Likewise, for each and every one of us, the connection with the *Nasi* and head of our generation, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—who has already explained that this connection is through studying his Torah and walking in the straight path that he has taught us, to walk in his ways—this is precisely what will assist in the *avodah* of the days of *Rosh Hashanah* and their connection with the rest of the days of the year.

And may this be the channel through which a *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah* will be drawn down, in goodness that is seen and revealed, both materially and spiritually—during the days of *Rosh Hashanah* and subsequently throughout the entire year.

**Menachem Mendel son of Chana**
**Schneerson**

First day of *Selichos*, 5712
Brooklyn, N.Y.

## 453

B"H, Tuesday of Selichos, 5713
Brooklyn, N.Y.

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., Rabbi Y.

Greetings and blessings!

Enclosed is the Kuntres for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah, and you will surely merit to share it with all those in your surroundings. It would be most appropriate to study it together in a group, for if our Sages have always extolled the virtue of studying not in isolation, then how much more so during the days before Rosh Hashanah, on Rosh Hashanah, and during the Aseres Yemei Teshuvah—when there is a special virtue in the bonding of friends.

As is explained in the teachings of Chassidus regarding the verse, "You are standing today, all of you," which is always read before Rosh Hashanah, it is hinted in the word "today" (hayom) that it refers to Rosh Hashanah, when all the sparks of the souls stand and are elevated to their source "before Hashem." And it must be "all of you," meaning that everyone gathers together to be united as one—your heads, your tribes, etc., down to the drawer of your water.

My father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, concludes with this—which he heard from his father, the holy Rebbe, who had it by tradition from Rebbe to Rebbe: that when the Alter Rebbe was in Mezritch, his teacher—the Maggid of Mezritch—revealed to him who he was... and that his avodah was to reveal and explain the teachings of the Baal Shem Tov regarding ahavas Hashem and ahavas Yisrael, and to reveal the path of Chassidus Chabad. And, first and foremost—his teacher, the Maggid of Mezritch, transmitted to him in the name of his teacher, the Baal Shem Tov—the path of avodah is that one must also bring the simple people close, for one must love a Yid because he is a Yid.

He adds an explanation to this: The Baal Shem Tov revealed the concept of the connection of ahavas Hashem, ahavas HaTorah, and ahavas Yisrael. The Maggid of Mezritch revealed the intellectual understanding and grasp of the synthesis of these three loves, with the internal explanation of the three modes of love: "with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your might." And our Rebbe, the Alter Rebbe, revealed the path of how every single person **can, should,** and **must** bring these three loves into actual practice, stating that ahavas Yisrael is the "upper gate," upon which is inscribed in luminous letters, "This is the gate to Hashem."

And through this—that we are all standing together as one—"Hashem our G-d establishes us today," this day of Rosh Hashanah, "as a people to Him, and He will be to us as a G-d," destined to illuminate us with an eternal light and redeem us with the true and complete Geulah, speedily in our days, literally.

Wishing you to be blessed with a good and sweet year, and to be inscribed and sealed for good.

**M. Schneerson**

***

Regarding what you wrote in your letter about yourself, your shortcomings, etc.—upon reading your letter, I was reminded of what my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, said regarding a certain story: One must not speak lashon hara about a Yid. Not even about oneself.

I look forward to hearing good news and celebrations.

***

In your letter of the 8th of Elul, you complain that I did not answer your questions—but you did not ask me anything; you only sent a Pidyon Nefesh to be read at the Ohel, and I fulfilled your request, as is understood.

- If it is not so suitable, you can study with the young ones from the teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, a few times a week, and not in writing.

## 454

B"H, 24 Elul, 5714

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., R' Sh. D. (may he live and be well),

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 22 Elul, informing me of the birth of your daughter, mazel tov, I hereby express my congratulations—mazel tov, mazel tov—and may you and your wife (may she live and be well) merit to raise her to Torah, the chuppah, and good deeds.

The *pidyon nefesh* that you enclosed in your letter, I will read at the gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), at an auspicious time.

It is surprising that you do not mention whether you are participating in the *chalukas haShas*, as it is well known that every person should learn at least one tractate of Gemara per year (see *Likkutei Dibburim*, Vol. 3, at the end).

I am sure you will continue with your memoirs, even though none were included in this letter.

With blessings for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for you and your entire family (may they live and be well),

**Menachem Schneerson**

Enclosed here is the *kuntres* for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah and general letters; it would be appropriate to publicize them in the most suitable manner.

## 355

B"H, 24 Elul, 5715

To the esteemed and distinguished Rabbi... may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

You surely received the *kuntres* for Chai Elul and the letter attached to it in due time, and you also read what I wrote in the margin of the letter.

Due to a lack of time, I did not elaborate then as I should have, and I also thought that perhaps there was no need for elaboration and that a few brief words would suffice. But since you remained silent and did not respond to me regarding this, it is known in the *Gemara* that there are two interpretations of silence: either "silence is an admission," or that he dismissed the words entirely. Since there is room for doubt, I am writing at greater length. This is the situation as I see it:

My revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, chose you and made you his *shaliach* to lead the congregation of the children of Israel in his camp.

A person’s *shaliach* is like the person himself, and one of the reasons for this is that the sender imparts his own powers to the *shaliach*. This is true of any person in general, and even more so when the person is internal, and all the more so if the sender is an *etzem* (essence). It is known from the teaching of the Baal Shem Tov that when you grasp a part of the essence, you grasp the whole of it (cited in the *maamar* for Rosh Hashanah, Chapter 2).

Thus, when the sender gives of his power to the *shaliach*, it is as if he himself is entirely present there.

For what purpose did my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, send you? It is clear that it is to lead his congregation in the ways of Torah and *mitzvos* and to assist them so that **each and every one** of them may ascend the path that leads to the House of Hashem. It is also simple that for this, the *shaliach* himself must ascend from level to level **much more** than the congregation he is appointed to lead. By what means can the *shaliach* ascend? There is the path of the King, the King of Kings, a path paved and explained by Chazal in general, by the *Nesi'ei* Chabad in particular, and by our *Nasi*, my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, specifically: how one can be rid of the enticements of the *yetzer hara*—called "the clever one"—which is the line of "turn from evil," and how to ascend in the line of "do good."

After all this, if, G-d forbid, the rumor I heard regarding your desire concerning college is true, there are several consequences to this: a) A large portion of your limited time—which is already insufficient—will be spent on matters other than the effort to fulfill your aforementioned role. b) You will use a portion of your time for matters that are contrary to the fulfillment of that role. c) The members of your congregation will see you and do likewise, with even greater addition, saying: "If the *shaliach* of the Rebbe of Lubavitch, after studying for several years in his *yeshiva*, still finds it necessary to study the 'Torah' of college in order to *zu vern a mentsch* (to become a person), then why should we live in error? It would be better for us to send our sons and daughters to receive such an education from the outset." d) And this is the most significant of all: *was hot er tzum Rebb'n was er schlept em mit zich in college* (what does he have against the Rebbe that he drags him along with him into college)? For the powers of the sender are within him, and when his body goes to college, the *nefesh elokis* bound within that body must also go, and consequently, the powers of the Rebbe that are clothed in this *nefesh elokis*—as mentioned above, that a part of the essence, etc.—are also there. In effect, he is dragging the Rebbe along with him.

I have no desire to elaborate further on all this, nor do I see a necessity for it, as the above is surely more than enough.

I do not know if the trial is truly so great for you, but in any case, my hope and confidence are that you will withstand this trial, and then you will surely see that this is the nature of trials—that there was no substance to the matter at all. As is known regarding the distinction between trials and *birurim*. You should increase your study of Torah in general, the teachings of Chabad in particular, and the teachings of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, specifically; all of this is a counsel so that thoughts which should be foreign to you will not disturb you, and you will be able to fulfill your role—the role of your soul—in this world with *menuchas hanefesh*.

I do not know the attitude of your wife, may she live, toward the aforementioned question, but in any case, the choice is yours, and seemingly it is appropriate that you share the content of my letter regarding this matter with her.

I am sure you will inform me of your good decision regarding all of this, and I thank you in advance.

I would be interested to know if there were good results from what we discussed while you were here—that you should establish a study session together with your wife, may she live, in some of the books of Chazal that are more suited to her spirit.

I will conclude with a blessing of *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for you and your wife, may she live, that you may merit that through you the desires of my revered father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, will be fulfilled completely, and that you may be able to bring them from potential into action with *menuchas hanefesh*, *menuchas haguf*, and with expansion in both material and spiritual matters.

Awaiting good tidings,

**Menachem Schneerson**

Enclosed is the *kuntres* for Rosh Hashanah with the letter attached to it, and a general letter in three languages. It would be appropriate to distribute the general letter in a manner suitable for various circles.

## 556

B"H, 24 Elul, 5716

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc. [Name], may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter of 9 Elul, regarding the reasons [Name], may he live, gives for sending his son to an education [system] other than our yeshiva—the first reason being that he is a member of Agudah, and the second reason being for the sake of his future prospects:

In truth, both reasons are invalid.

1) Regarding his membership in Agudah: It is well known that every individual must strive to influence their environment, not the other way around. Since he has a connection to Chabad and was also connected to my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, and he finds himself in an Agudah environment, this is surely by Divine Providence so that *he* should make Chabadniks out of the Agudah members, and not that *they* should make Agudah members out of him, his wife, and his children, who are Chabadniks. "If one has two hundred, he has a hundred"—the greater influence should prevail, not the reverse. I will not elaborate on this, as it is simple, and further words are unnecessary.

2) Regarding the reason given about "future prospects": Our Sages have written that "a person does not know in what he will profit"—this refers to both spiritual profit and material profit. If this is true for adults, then it is all the more so for children who are still at the beginning of their education; it is impossible to know at all in what he will profit when he grows up and becomes a man.

Regarding what you write about the assistance for the Merkos L'Inyonei Chinuch and Kehot, which apparently is proceeding with difficulty according to your letter: I have enclosed a copy of what I wrote to someone else regarding this, and a word to the wise is sufficient.

Enclosed here is the Kuntres for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah, along with the letter attached to it and a general letter. Also, a letter to the yeshiva students. In my opinion, it is appropriate that these two letters be publicized in an appropriate manner within various circles.

With the blessing of *Kesivah V'chasimah Tovah* for him and his entire family, may they live, that he may merit to fulfill the will of the one who sent him—my father-in-law, the Rebbe—to its fullest, with abundance and peace of mind.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 5757

B"H, 24 Elul, 5757

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, etc., R' Chaim Shaul, may he live.

Peace and blessing!

In response to your letter and the *pidyon nefesh* that I received today,

As you requested, I will read them at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. A *tzaddik* who has departed is found in this world even more than during his lifetime; may he grant his blessing to each and every one mentioned therein for everything they need, both materially and spiritually, and may Hashem fulfill His blessings to their fullest extent.

Many thanks for the precious gift you sent in your letter, which is a page from the holy handwriting of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory. It is explained in many places in *Chassidus* that the power of the craftsman remains in the vessel, and based on this, it is understood that the power of the writer is likewise present in his writing. This is especially true since this page concerns the teachings of *Chassidus*, for the Rebbe and *Chassidus* are one in essence.

It is surely superfluous to urge you to strengthen your *kvius itim*—specifically in the study of the teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, in addition to your other *shiurim*. And how good it is if such study also takes place in a group, where "each man helps his fellow" to strengthen and inspire.

I sign with the blessing of *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah* and success in your holy work of influencing the students with Torah and *mitzvos* in *yiras shamayim*, and regarding the *inyana d'yoma*, with the awakening of true *teshuvah*, as stated in the words of the Rav, my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, printed in the *kuntres* for Rosh Hashanah, enclosed herewith, which he said on Erev Rosh Hashanah of the year 5757. May it be His will that you be able to share good news regarding this frequently.

**M. Schneerson**

Also enclosed is a general letter and a letter to the *yeshiva* students—not only for our *yeshiva*, but for all *yeshivos*. I am certain you will publicize them in an appropriate manner.

## 558

B"H, 24 Elul, 5718

To the illustrious Rabbi, the G-d-fearing man, distinguished in Torah and lineage, a man of many accomplishments and a master of good deeds, who sits in judgment and leads his community beside still waters, our master, Rabbi Refael Baruch, may he live, the spokesman of the holy community of the city of Meknes, whose influence extends throughout the entire country,

Peace and abundant blessing:

Your letter from the 9th of the month of mercy was received in due time, and I thank Hashem for the good news that you have returned to health. May Hashem grant that you continue to be healthy and whole, as the Rambam writes in Hilchos Deos, beginning of chapter 4, that the health of the body is one of the ways of Hashem. May you sit securely on the throne of your rabbinate and be able to lead your community in Torah, avodah, and acts of kindness, until the coming of Yinnon, speedily in our days, truly.

It is my hope to Hashem Yisbarach that we will succeed—meaning, through your efforts, your honor, and our efforts from here—in engaging in the dissemination of Torah and yiras shamayim in our pure camp, based on the principle of our Sages, of blessed memory, that "one who comes to purify himself is assisted from Above," and when we sanctify a little from below, they sanctify much from Above. I am confident that the light of Torah and the "Luminary" within the Torah will shine ever brighter, until we all merit the fulfillment of the prophecy, "and the night shall shine as the day."

Enclosed here is the Kuntres published for the day of Chai Elul, and I am certain your honor will find it of interest.

I will conclude with wishes and blessings for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year for your honor and for all your household, may they live. May the coming year be for us and for all of Yisrael a year of Geulah—the true and complete Geulah—through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, the prerequisite for which is the awakening of teshuvah and the matter of tzedakah, both material tzedakah and spiritual tzedakah, as explained in the letter for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah, a copy of which is enclosed.

And immediately to teshuvah, immediately to Geulah, and immediately for good, long lives and peace.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 459

B”H, 25 Elul, 5719

May the mountains bear peace and blessing to the brilliant Rabbi, the pious man, the G-d-fearing, who sits on the seat of judgment, a man of valor and many accomplishments, who leads his congregation with integrity, who illuminates and shines, who sees and understands well, of the holy seed, etc., Rabbi Meir Abuchatzeira, may he live, leader of the holy congregation of Midelt, may it be built and established.

After offering my regards to his greatness:

With great pleasure I read the letter from your greatness dated the 14th of Rachamim. It is my hope that your greatness—and through you, certainly those who stand under your influence as well—are prepared to participate in organizing and strengthening the kosher education of the boys and girls in your country, and to increase Torah and make it glorious. For Hashem desires that our hands succeed in seeing good fruit from our labor, the labor of Torah which is "above the sun" (Zohar Vol. I, 223b).

Regarding the theme of the day: On this day, the 25th of Elul, the world was created. Our Sages of blessed memory taught regarding the verse "In the beginning... the heavens and the earth," that their "offspring" (the entire history of the world) were also created then. The inner meaning of this is that all six thousand years of the world’s existence and their events were included in the creation of the first day, in a single utterance—for it contains within it all ten utterances.

If so, it is clear that the purpose of creation and its perfection following the days of exile must also be included in the first day. And indeed, this is so.

The order of creation, as explained in the books of Kabbalah, begins with Tzimtzum, which provided space for the worlds. Afterward, the Kav (the line of light) shone into the worlds themselves—initially building worlds and destroying them, the world of Tohu—and subsequently, He created and said, "This one pleases Me," the world of Tikun.

This is as our Sages hinted (Shabbos 77b), "First darkness, then light." "And there was evening and there was morning."

The purpose of creation and its perfection is so that man should not eat the "bread of shame," but rather derive enjoyment from the fruit of his labor in his battle with the Yetzer Hara, until he illuminates the darkness of the world through his avodah, and specifically transforms the darkness into light.

This is hinted at in the creation of the first day: "And there was evening"—the two thousand years of Tohu, where the world conducted itself in darkness.

"And there was morning"—the two thousand years of Torah, for "Torah is light." "One day"—the two thousand years of the days of Moshiach. At that time, the avodah is the difficult labor of the time of exile, the purpose of which is to connect the evening and the morning so that they become one day, one essence. For "the night shall shine like the day," in the light of the one Hashem and His one Name.

This is also the general task of every man and woman among Bnei Yisrael: to illuminate the darkness of the world through the "candle of a mitzvah and the light of Torah," until Yinnon comes—"Before the sun, Yinnon is His Name"—and the prophecy is fulfilled: "And Hashem shall be for you an eternal light."

In accordance with the request of your greatness, I will mention your name, the names of your household members may they live, and the individuals you mentioned in your esteemed letter, at the gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, both materially and spiritually.

Blessing and being blessed with a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah*,

**Menachem Schneerson**

Enclosed is the Kuntres for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah, along with a copy of the general letter pertaining to the Kuntres, as well as a letter to all our brethren, the Children of Israel, may Hashem preserve them.

## 660

B"H, 25 Elul, 5710

To the venerable Rabbi, the G-d-fearing man, full of Torah and wisdom, who supports the pillars of the world, who organizes and analyzes, possessing noble character traits, who seeks the good of his people and speaks peace, etc.,
Rabbi Y. HaLevi, Chief Rabbi

Greetings and blessings!

Approaching the New Year, which is coming upon us and upon all of Israel for good, I hereby wish you and all those associated with you a year of life, peace, and abundant blessing in both material and spiritual matters—a fruitful and successful year in your avodah for the benefit of the community and the individual—and may the desire of Hashem succeed in your hand.

With blessings for a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, and a year of Geulah through Moshiach Tzidkeinu, speedily and in our days.

**M. Schneerson**

Enclosed is the *kuntres* for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah—from the teachings of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory—which has just been published, and I am certain you will find it of interest.

A brief note regarding the significance of this day: On the 25th of Elul the world was created, and "there was evening and there was morning, one day." There are two opinions regarding time: whether it is a continuous flow or whether the days are distinct from one another. It could be said that the various *sugyos* of the *Gemara* and Rava in *Nazir* (7a) are divided on this. However, according to all opinions, night and day are one, and they are not distinct, for "day and night" is an occurrence added to time, not part of its essence. This is not the case regarding one day from another; there is an opinion that they are distinct, for each and every day performs its specific task (*Zohar* Vol. III, 94b). Therefore, there are seven days that are distinct from one another, and only seven, for afterward, the cycle repeats: the first day, etc.

Regarding a person’s *avodah* to his Creator: "Night and day"—ultimately, "no one shall be cast out from Him," and deliberate sins become for him like merits, for He will remove the spirit of impurity, and "the night will shine like the day." But the days are distinct, for even in the time to come, every *tzaddik* will be scorched by the canopy of his fellow. And this is not the place to elaborate.

***660***

From a photocopy of the letter.
Also written on 7 Tishrei 5711 and printed in *Likkutei Sichos* Vol. 17, p. 544.
**Rabbi Y.:** Herzog.

## 6061

B"H, 25 Elul, 5713

To the esteemed Chossid, G-d-fearing, and distinguished, etc., R' Y. Sh. Z. (may he live and be well),

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 8 Elul:

As you requested, I will read the list of students at the tziyun of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year, for success in their Torah studies and *yiras shamayim*, and for the fulfillment of *mitzvos* with *kabbalas ol* and joy combined.

It is good news that a permanent committee has been organized to support the yeshiva in Shepparton. Surely the committee members will recognize the great merit that Divine Providence has granted them to participate with my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory), in his holy work, and they will dedicate themselves to their most responsible task with all their soul. Please convey my regards to everyone in general, and to the chairman... in particular. My blessing is that they succeed in their general and specific work, both materially and spiritually.

Regarding the specific organization of the yeshiva’s administration and the *ramim*, you must consult with the senior *anash* (may they live and be well) on the spot. I ask that any of *anash* who are qualified to be a *ram*, *mashpia*, or administrator should be very careful to avoid misplaced humility, and should strive, with the help of Hashem, to fulfill their role.

Certainly, the perspective of *anash* (may they live and be well) is correct: even if it is necessary to add a scholarly *ram* to the work—provided he is also suitable in terms of his *yiras shamayim* and his connection to Chassidus for such a position—the head of the institution must, in any case, be a person whose foundation is in the holy mountains of Lubavitch, and who received the majority of his influence and education there.

As for your concern that if you are a *shaliach* you will not be able to express your opinion broadly, and it will not be as well-received by the public, I have already said many times that in these matters, each one of us is a *shaliach* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe (of blessed memory). We walk upon his shoulders and rely on his breadth. When the public senses this, many obstacles, gossip, and so on, are nullified.

This is as explained regarding the subject of *nisyonos* (as opposed to the subject of *birurim*). It is understood that "breadth" does not mean tearing someone else’s nose off; rather, it means inner strength and the absolute resolution that, ultimately, the Rebbe’s will—he who sends us—must be carried out and will be carried out. When things are said with *nachas*, they are heard and they are effective.

May Hashem grant each of us the merit to aim for the truth in fulfilling our role and mission.

Enclosed is the *kuntres* for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah and the letter to Levi Eliyahu.

With blessings for a *kesivah v'chasimah tovah* for him, his family (may they live and be well), and the entire group (may they live).

**M. Schneerson**

## 602

B"H, 25 Elul, 5710

To the esteemed Rabbi, the Chossid, G-d-fearing, and learned, who engages in communal needs with faith, etc., R’ Sh.Y. [shiyichyeh],

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter of 9 Elul:

As you requested, I will read the enclosed *pidyon nefesh* at the *tziyun* of my father-in-law, the Rebbe [hakadosh], at an *eis ratzon*. May it be Hashem’s will that all your requests be fulfilled in a revealed and good manner.

Regarding your suggestion to encourage *anash* [shiyichyeh] who are currently in Paris to hasten their move to *Eretz Yisrael* [tivaneh v’tikonen bimheirah biyameinu]:

As they have written here, the situation is that, with the exception of a few individuals, they do not wish to move to *Eretz Yisrael* for several reasons. Furthermore, you are aware of the details you mentioned (and in general, how can one issue a single, sweeping directive when the circumstances of each individual differ?). As I know, even my father-in-law, the Rebbe [zatzuklaleh, navgam, zya, hakadosh]—who had very broad shoulders—did not treat the question of relocating the *anash* refugees to any given place as a question for the collective as a whole, **as a command or order.** Rather, he would deliberate and take into account the situation, the desire, and the specific question of each individual separately. It is also understood, quite simply, that my father-in-law, the Rebbe [hakadosh], exerted great effort so that those of *anash* [shiyichyeh] who are in *Eretz Yisrael*, and those who, with his consent, are destined to settle there, should be settled—with Hashem’s help—in the best and most appropriate manner, both materially and spiritually. Therefore, I am confident that this endeavor of yours will also be successful, both materially and spiritually.

Enclosed is the *kuntres* for the upcoming Rosh Hashanah, along with a copy of a letter to Levi for him.

Wishing you and your household [shiyichyeh] a *kesivah v’chasimah tovah* for a good and sweet year. May we all merit to share and hear only good news.

**Menachem Schneerson**

## 563

B"H, 26 Elul, 5713

To the avreich, the chossid, etc., R’... may he live,

Greetings and blessings!

In response to your letter:

May it be Hashem’s will that you be a fitting vessel for the fulfillment of all the blessings that the Rebbe, my father-in-law, of blessed memory, bestowed upon you, in both material and spiritual matters.

You have surely seen the excerpt in *Hayom Yom* for 11 Nissan regarding conduct on one’s birthday, particularly on this birthday, which concludes your twenty-second year.

See *Kiddushin* (30a) regarding the opinion that [the period of youth/education] lasts until twenty-two; see there.

With blessings for a *Kesivah Vachasimah Tovah* and that you may soon be settled in a manner appropriate for you, in good health and prosperity.

**Rabbi Menachem Schneerson**

***

#### 563

From the secretariat copy.

**Regarding the opinion:** See above, Letter 596, and the references in the footnotes there.

## 464

[Late 5710]

Perhaps the intention is that this is indeed done in practice, for "action is essential," in accordance with the words of our Sages (Bava Basra 130b). It is possible that the words "three times, intermittently" were omitted in the transcription of the letter (after the words "on the right first"). The custom of the Chassidim refutes the opinions that it should not be done intermittently (these opinions are collected in the sefer *Shaarei Tefillah* by R. Y. Rokach, p. 4), or that it should be done four times (the custom of the Gra and the Rebbe of Munkatch, author of *Minchas Elazar*, etc.).

#### 464

Printed in the *Yagdil Torah* (N.Y.) collection, issue 53, section 52, from the Rebbe’s own handwriting in the margins of a query from R. Avraham Chaim Naeh, dated 10 Tammuz 5710. He asked regarding what was stated in a letter from the Rebbe Rayatz (his *Igros Kodesh*, Vol. 1, Letter 100), which states: "Regarding *netilah*, all *netilos* are on the right first—both taking the vessel in the right hand first, and likewise the pouring must be on the right hand first... And in order that both (the taking and the *netilah*) be fulfilled with the right, all Chassidim are accustomed to take the vessel in the right hand, transfer it to the left hand, and pour on the right first. And in this there is a further intention, for all *hashpa'ah* is through the right, and specifically *netilas yadayim*, which is the drawing down of *Chochmah* into the *Middos*—water onto the hands—and through *Chochmah* the hair is clarified from this to the right."

Regarding this, R. Avraham Chaim Naeh wrote: "Regarding what was written, 'all Chassidim are accustomed to take the vessel in the right hand, etc.'—I would point out that this is explicit in the *Shulchan Aruch*. And it is in the *Beis Yosef* in the name of the *Zohar*. The expression 'are accustomed' requires study."

## 465

465

*(Late Elul, 5711)*

To the *Tamim*, the G-d-fearing, etc., our teacher, Rabbi [Name],

Peace and blessing!

Your *pan* was received, and I will read it, G-d willing, at the gravesite of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory.

And a *tzaddik* who has departed, who is found in this world even more than during his lifetime, will surely bestow his blessing, and Hashem will fulfill his blessing in both material and spiritual matters.

With blessings for a *chag sameach* and a *ksiva v’chasima tova*,

**M. Schneerson**

Between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, 5711

I read the *pan* on Erev Rosh Hashanah at the gravesite, and afterwards in the chamber of my father-in-law, the Rebbe, of blessed memory, where one enters to submit *pidyonos* and for *yechidus*. The one who gives, etc., should be informed.

You have surely seen my general letter, and I request that you inform me what has been done regarding this during the month of Tishrei. Thank you in advance.

* * *

Regarding what you write, "Please have mercy on us and be..." — for this, one needs the capacity, the essential powers, the transcendent powers, the revealed powers, and the perfection of the garments of *chochma, binah, and da’as*, etc., etc.

* * *

You will surely inform me of the progress of your efforts to obtain all the necessary documents and permits.

***

**465**

From a photocopy of the letter.

The letter was sent to several people.

**And regarding what you write:** The following segment is to Rabbi Yitzchak Gansburg.

**Have mercy on us:** To accept the *nesiut* (leadership). See above, letters 569, 615.

**You will surely inform me:** The following segment is to Rabbi Shlomo Matusov. Additional letters to him — above 587, and in the references noted there.

**The gathering:** To travel to Morocco, as above, letters 695, 726.
